content:
# Pydantic People

Pydantic has an amazing community of contributors, reviewers, and experts that help propel the project forward.
Here, we celebrate those people and their contributions.

## Maintainers

These are the current maintainers of the Pydantic repository. Feel free to tag us if you have questions, review requests, or feature requests for which you'd like feedback!

{{ maintainers }}

## Experts

These are the users that have helped others the most with questions in GitHub through *all time*.

{{ experts }}

### Most active users last month

These are the users that have helped others the most with questions in GitHub during the last month.

{{ most_active_users }}

## Top contributors

These are the users that have created the most pull requests that have been *merged*.

{{ top_contributors }}

## Top Reviewers

These are the users that have reviewed the most Pull Requests from others, assisting with code quality, documentation, bug fixes, feature requests, etc.

{{ top_reviewers }}

## About the data

The data displayed above is calculated monthly via the Github GraphQL API (see
[source code](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/tree/main/.github/actions/people/people.py),
many thanks to [Sebastián Ramírez](https://github.com/tiangolo) for the script from which we based this logic).

Depending on changing conditions, the thresholds for the different categories of contributors may change in the future.

metadata.title: Pydantic People

content:
First of all, we recognize that the transitions from Pydantic V1 to V2 has been and will be painful for some users.
We're sorry about this pain :pray:, it was an unfortunate but necessary step to correct design mistakes of V1.

**There will not be another breaking change of this magnitude!**

## Pydantic V1

Active development of V1 has already stopped, however critical bug fixes and security vulnerabilities will be fixed in V1 until
the release of Pydantic V3.

## Pydantic V2

We will not intentionally make breaking changes in minor releases of V2.

Functionality marked as deprecated will not be removed until the next major V3 release.

Of course, some apparently safe changes and bug fixes will inevitably break some users' code — obligatory link to [xkcd](https://xkcd.com/1172/).

The following changes will **NOT** be considered breaking changes, and may occur in minor releases:

* Bug fixes that may result in existing code breaking, provided that such code was relying on undocumented features/constructs.
* Changing the format of JSON Schema [references](https://json-schema.org/understanding-json-schema/structuring#dollarref).
* Changing the `msg`, `ctx`, and `loc` fields of [`ValidationError`][pydantic_core.ValidationError] exceptions. `type` will not change — if you're programmatically parsing error messages, you should use `type`.
* Adding new keys to [`ValidationError`][pydantic_core.ValidationError] exceptions — e.g. we intend to add `line_number` and `column_number` to errors when validating JSON once we migrate to a new JSON parser.
* Adding new [`ValidationError`][pydantic_core.ValidationError] errors.
* Changing how `__repr__` behaves, even of public classes.
* The contents of the [core schemas](./internals/architecture.md#communicating-between-pydantic-and-pydantic-core-the-core-schema) (usually available under the

  [`__pydantic_core_schema__`][pydantic.BaseModel.__pydantic_core_schema__] attribute for Pydantic models and `core_schema` for [type adapters](./concepts/type_adapter.md))
  may change between releases (this is a low-level format Pydantic uses to plan how to execute validation and serialization).

In all cases we will aim to minimize churn and do so only when justified by the increase of quality of Pydantic for users.

## Pydantic V3 and beyond

Any associated breaking changes in the V3 release will be introduced with care. This will include bug fixes that can't be solved in V2 without introducing a change in behavior
(e.g. [fixing configuration not following the MRO](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/9992)), or changes that don't remove existing functionality or doesn't provide
proper alternatives.

In V3, it is expected to merged `pydantic-core` as an internal sub-module of Pydantic, meaning it won't be published as a standalone library anymore.

## Tagging conventions

Pydantic uses Git tags to identify all published versions on PyPI (including alpha and beta releases), prefixed with the `v` character. Since v2.13, `pydantic-core`
has been merged as a workspace member inside the `pydantic` repository, and subsequent `pydantic-core` releases are tagged as `core-vx.y.z`.

## Experimental Features

At Pydantic, we like to move quickly and innovate! To that end, we may introduce experimental features in minor releases.

    To learn more about our current experimental features, see the [experimental features documentation](./concepts/experimental.md).

Please keep in mind, experimental features are active works in progress. If these features are successful, they'll eventually become part of Pydantic. If unsuccessful, said features will be removed with little notice. While in its experimental phase, a feature's API and behaviors may not be stable, and it's very possible that changes made to the feature will not be backward-compatible.

### Naming Conventions

We use one of the following naming conventions to indicate that a feature is experimental:

1. The feature is located in the [`experimental`](api/experimental.md) module. In this case, you can access the feature like this:

    ```python {test="skip" lint="skip"}
    from pydantic.experimental import feature_name
    ```

2. The feature is located in the main module, but prefixed with `experimental_`. This case occurs when we add a new field, argument, or method to an existing data structure already within the main `pydantic` module.

New features with these naming conventions are subject to change or removal, and we are looking for feedback and suggestions before making them a permanent part of Pydantic. See the [feedback section](./concepts/experimental.md#feedback) for more information.

### Lifecycle of Experimental Features

1. A new feature is added, either in the [`experimental`](api/experimental.md) module or with the `experimental_` prefix.
2. The behavior is often modified during patch/minor releases, with potential API/behavior changes.
3. If the feature is successful, we promote it to Pydantic with the following steps:

    a. If it was in the [`experimental`](api/experimental.md) module, the feature is cloned to Pydantic's main module. The original experimental feature still remains in the [`experimental`](api/experimental.md) module, but it will show a warning when used. If the feature was already in the main Pydantic module, we create a copy of the feature without the `experimental_` prefix, so the feature exists with both the official and experimental names. A deprecation warning is attached to the experimental version.

    b. At some point, the code of the experimental feature is removed, but there will still be a stub of the feature that provides an error message with appropriate instructions.

    c. As a last step, the experimental version of the feature is entirely removed from the codebase.

If the feature is unsuccessful or unpopular, it's removed with little notice. A stub will remain in the location of the deprecated feature with an error message.

Thanks to [streamlit](https://docs.streamlit.io/develop/quick-reference/prerelease) for the inspiration for the lifecycle and naming conventions of our new experimental feature patterns.

## Support for Python versions

Pydantic will drop support for a Python version when the following conditions are met:

* The Python version has reached its [expected end of life](https://devguide.python.org/versions/).
* less than 5% of downloads of the most recent minor release are using that version.

metadata.title: Version Policy

content:
# Why use Pydantic Validation?

Today, Pydantic is downloaded many times a month and used by some of the largest and most recognisable organisations in the world.

It's hard to know why so many people have adopted Pydantic since its inception six years ago, but here are a few guesses.

## Type hints powering schema validation {#type-hints}

The schema that Pydantic validates against is generally defined by Python [type hints](https://docs.python.org/3/glossary.html#term-type-hint).

Type hints are great for this since, if you're writing modern Python, you already know how to use them.
Using type hints also means that Pydantic integrates well with static typing tools
(like [mypy](https://www.mypy-lang.org/) and [Pyright](https://github.com/microsoft/pyright/))
and IDEs (like [PyCharm](https://www.jetbrains.com/pycharm/) and [VSCode](https://code.visualstudio.com/)).

    ```python
    from typing import Annotated, Literal

    from annotated_types import Gt

    from pydantic import BaseModel

    class Fruit(BaseModel):
        name: str  # (1)!
        color: Literal['red', 'green']  # (2)!
        weight: Annotated[float, Gt(0)]  # (3)!
        bazam: dict[str, list[tuple[int, bool, float]]]  # (4)!

    print(
        Fruit(
            name='Apple',
            color='red',
            weight=4.2,
            bazam={'foobar': [(1, True, 0.1)]},
        )
    )
    #> name='Apple' color='red' weight=4.2 bazam={'foobar': [(1, True, 0.1)]}
    ```

    1. The `name` field is simply annotated with `str` — any string is allowed.
    2. The [`Literal`][typing.Literal] type is used to enforce that `color` is either `'red'` or `'green'`.
    3. Even when we want to apply constraints not encapsulated in Python types, we can use [`Annotated`][typing.Annotated]
       and [`annotated-types`](https://github.com/annotated-types/annotated-types) to enforce constraints while still keeping typing support.
    4. I'm not claiming "bazam" is really an attribute of fruit, but rather to show that arbitrarily complex types can easily be validated.

    See the [documentation on supported types](concepts/types.md).

## Performance

Pydantic's core validation logic is implemented in a separate package ([`pydantic-core`](https://pypi.org/project/pydantic-core)),
where validation for most types is implemented in Rust.

As a result, Pydantic is among the fastest data validation libraries for Python.

    In general, dedicated code should be much faster than a general-purpose validator, but in this example
    Pydantic is >300% faster than dedicated code when parsing JSON and validating URLs.

    ```python {title="Performance Example" test="skip"}
    import json
    import timeit
    from urllib.parse import urlparse

    import requests

    from pydantic import HttpUrl, TypeAdapter

    reps = 7
    number = 100
    r = requests.get('https://api.github.com/emojis')
    r.raise_for_status()
    emojis_json = r.content

    def emojis_pure_python(raw_data):
        data = json.loads(raw_data)
        output = {}
        for key, value in data.items():
            assert isinstance(key, str)
            url = urlparse(value)
            assert url.scheme in ('https', 'http')
            output[key] = url

    emojis_pure_python_times = timeit.repeat(
        'emojis_pure_python(emojis_json)',
        globals={
            'emojis_pure_python': emojis_pure_python,
            'emojis_json': emojis_json,
        },
        repeat=reps,
        number=number,
    )
    print(f'pure python: {min(emojis_pure_python_times) / number * 1000:0.2f}ms')
    #> pure python: 5.32ms

    type_adapter = TypeAdapter(dict[str, HttpUrl])
    emojis_pydantic_times = timeit.repeat(
        'type_adapter.validate_json(emojis_json)',
        globals={
            'type_adapter': type_adapter,
            'HttpUrl': HttpUrl,
            'emojis_json': emojis_json,
        },
        repeat=reps,
        number=number,
    )
    print(f'pydantic: {min(emojis_pydantic_times) / number * 1000:0.2f}ms')
    #> pydantic: 1.54ms

    print(
        f'Pydantic {min(emojis_pure_python_times) / min(emojis_pydantic_times):0.2f}x faster'
    )
    #> Pydantic 3.45x faster
    ```

Unlike other performance-centric libraries written in compiled languages, Pydantic also has excellent support for customizing validation via [functional validators](#customisation).

    Samuel Colvin's [talk at PyCon 2023](https://youtu.be/pWZw7hYoRVU) explains how [`pydantic-core`](https://pypi.org/project/pydantic-core)
    works and how it integrates with Pydantic.

## Serialization

Pydantic provides functionality to serialize model in three ways:

1. To a Python `dict` made up of the associated Python objects.
2. To a Python `dict` made up only of "jsonable" types.
3. To a JSON string.

In all three modes, the output can be customized by excluding specific fields, excluding unset fields, excluding default values, and excluding `None` values.

    ```python
    from datetime import datetime

    from pydantic import BaseModel

    class Meeting(BaseModel):
        when: datetime
        where: bytes
        why: str = 'No idea'

    m = Meeting(when='2020-01-01T12:00', where='home')
    print(m.model_dump(exclude_unset=True))
    #> {'when': datetime.datetime(2020, 1, 1, 12, 0), 'where': b'home'}
    print(m.model_dump(exclude={'where'}, mode='json'))
    #> {'when': '2020-01-01T12:00:00', 'why': 'No idea'}
    print(m.model_dump_json(exclude_defaults=True))
    #> {"when":"2020-01-01T12:00:00","where":"home"}
    ```

    See the [documentation on serialization](concepts/serialization.md).

## JSON Schema

A [JSON Schema](https://json-schema.org/) can be generated for any Pydantic schema — allowing self-documenting APIs and integration with a wide variety of tools which support the JSON Schema format.

    ```python
    from datetime import datetime

    from pydantic import BaseModel

    class Address(BaseModel):
        street: str
        city: str
        zipcode: str

    class Meeting(BaseModel):
        when: datetime
        where: Address
        why: str = 'No idea'

    print(Meeting.model_json_schema())
    """
    {
        '$defs': {
            'Address': {
                'properties': {
                    'street': {'title': 'Street', 'type': 'string'},
                    'city': {'title': 'City', 'type': 'string'},
                    'zipcode': {'title': 'Zipcode', 'type': 'string'},
                },
                'required': ['street', 'city', 'zipcode'],
                'title': 'Address',
                'type': 'object',
            }
        },
        'properties': {
            'when': {'format': 'date-time', 'title': 'When', 'type': 'string'},
            'where': {'$ref': '#/$defs/Address'},
            'why': {'default': 'No idea', 'title': 'Why', 'type': 'string'},
        },
        'required': ['when', 'where'],
        'title': 'Meeting',
        'type': 'object',
    }
    """
    ```

Pydantic is compliant with the latest version of JSON Schema specification
([2020-12](https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/release-notes.html)), which
is compatible with [OpenAPI 3.1](https://spec.openapis.org/oas/v3.1.0.html).

    See the [documentation on JSON Schema](concepts/json_schema.md).

## Strict mode and data coercion {#strict-lax}

By default, Pydantic is tolerant to common incorrect types and coerces data to the right type —
e.g. a numeric string passed to an `int` field will be parsed as an `int`.

Pydantic also has as [strict mode](concepts/strict_mode.md), where types are not coerced and a
validation error is raised unless the input data exactly matches the expected schema.

But strict mode would be pretty useless when validating JSON data since JSON doesn't have types matching
many common Python types like [`datetime`][datetime.datetime], [`UUID`][uuid.UUID] or [`bytes`][].

To solve this, Pydantic can parse and validate JSON in one step. This allows sensible data conversion
(e.g. when parsing strings into [`datetime`][datetime.datetime] objects). Since the JSON parsing is
implemented in Rust, it's also very performant.

    ```python
    from datetime import datetime

    from pydantic import BaseModel, ValidationError

    class Meeting(BaseModel):
        when: datetime
        where: bytes

    m = Meeting.model_validate({'when': '2020-01-01T12:00', 'where': 'home'})
    print(m)
    #> when=datetime.datetime(2020, 1, 1, 12, 0) where=b'home'
    try:
        m = Meeting.model_validate(
            {'when': '2020-01-01T12:00', 'where': 'home'}, strict=True
        )
    except ValidationError as e:
        print(e)
        """
        2 validation errors for Meeting
        when
          Input should be a valid datetime [type=datetime_type, input_value='2020-01-01T12:00', input_type=str]
        where
          Input should be a valid bytes [type=bytes_type, input_value='home', input_type=str]
        """

    m_json = Meeting.model_validate_json(
        '{"when": "2020-01-01T12:00", "where": "home"}'
    )
    print(m_json)
    #> when=datetime.datetime(2020, 1, 1, 12, 0) where=b'home'
    ```

    See the [documentation on strict mode](concepts/strict_mode.md).

## Dataclasses, TypedDicts, and more {#dataclasses-typeddict-more}

Pydantic provides four ways to create schemas and perform validation and serialization:

1. [`BaseModel`](concepts/models.md) — Pydantic's own super class with many common utilities available via instance methods.
2. [Pydantic dataclasses](concepts/dataclasses.md) — a wrapper around standard dataclasses with additional validation performed.
3. [`TypeAdapter`][pydantic.type_adapter.TypeAdapter] — a general way to adapt any type for validation and serialization.
   This allows types like [`TypedDict`](api/standard_library_types.md#typeddict) and [`NamedTuple`](api/standard_library_types.md#named-tuples)
   to be validated as well as simple types (like [`int`][] or [`timedelta`][datetime.timedelta]) — [all types](concepts/types.md) supported
   can be used with [`TypeAdapter`][pydantic.type_adapter.TypeAdapter].
4. [`validate_call`](concepts/validation_decorator.md) — a decorator to perform validation when calling a function.

    ```python
    from datetime import datetime

    from typing_extensions import NotRequired, TypedDict

    from pydantic import TypeAdapter

    class Meeting(TypedDict):
        when: datetime
        where: bytes
        why: NotRequired[str]

    meeting_adapter = TypeAdapter(Meeting)
    m = meeting_adapter.validate_python(  # (1)!
        {'when': '2020-01-01T12:00', 'where': 'home'}
    )
    print(m)
    #> {'when': datetime.datetime(2020, 1, 1, 12, 0), 'where': b'home'}
    meeting_adapter.dump_python(m, exclude={'where'})  # (2)!

    print(meeting_adapter.json_schema())  # (3)!
    """
    {
        'properties': {
            'when': {'format': 'date-time', 'title': 'When', 'type': 'string'},
            'where': {'format': 'binary', 'title': 'Where', 'type': 'string'},
            'why': {'title': 'Why', 'type': 'string'},
        },
        'required': ['when', 'where'],
        'title': 'Meeting',
        'type': 'object',
    }
    """
    ```

    1. [`TypeAdapter`][pydantic.type_adapter.TypeAdapter] for a [`TypedDict`][typing.TypedDict] performing validation,
       it can also validate JSON data directly with [`validate_json`][pydantic.type_adapter.TypeAdapter.validate_json].
    2. [`dump_python`][pydantic.type_adapter.TypeAdapter.dump_python] to serialise a [`TypedDict`][typing.TypedDict]
       to a python object, it can also serialise to JSON with [`dump_json`][pydantic.type_adapter.TypeAdapter.dump_json].
    3. [`TypeAdapter`][pydantic.type_adapter.TypeAdapter] can also generate a JSON Schema.

## Customisation

Functional validators and serializers, as well as a powerful protocol for custom types, means the way Pydantic operates can be customized on a per-field or per-type basis.

    "wrap validators" are new in Pydantic V2 and are one of the most powerful ways to customize validation.

    ```python
    from datetime import datetime, timezone
    from typing import Any

    from pydantic_core.core_schema import ValidatorFunctionWrapHandler

    from pydantic import BaseModel, field_validator

    class Meeting(BaseModel):
        when: datetime

        @field_validator('when', mode='wrap')
        def when_now(
            cls, input_value: Any, handler: ValidatorFunctionWrapHandler
        ) -> datetime:
            if input_value == 'now':
                return datetime.now()
            when = handler(input_value)
            # in this specific application we know tz naive datetimes are in UTC
            if when.tzinfo is None:
                when = when.replace(tzinfo=timezone.utc)
            return when

    print(Meeting(when='2020-01-01T12:00+01:00'))
    #> when=datetime.datetime(2020, 1, 1, 12, 0, tzinfo=TzInfo(3600))
    print(Meeting(when='now'))
    #> when=datetime.datetime(2032, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6)
    print(Meeting(when='2020-01-01T12:00'))
    #> when=datetime.datetime(2020, 1, 1, 12, 0, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)
    ```

    See the documentation on [validators](concepts/validators.md), [custom serializers](concepts/serialization.md#serializers),
    and [custom types](concepts/types.md#custom-types).

## Ecosystem

At the time of writing there are 466,400 repositories on GitHub and 8,119 packages on PyPI that depend on Pydantic.

Some notable libraries that depend on Pydantic:

{{ libraries }}

More libraries using Pydantic can be found at [`Kludex/awesome-pydantic`](https://github.com/Kludex/awesome-pydantic).

## Organisations using Pydantic {#using-pydantic}

Some notable companies and organisations using Pydantic together with comments on why/how we know they're using Pydantic.

The organisations below are included because they match one or more of the following criteria:

* Using Pydantic as a dependency in a public repository.
* Referring traffic to the Pydantic documentation site from an organization-internal domain — specific referrers are not included since they're generally not in the public domain.
* Direct communication between the Pydantic team and engineers employed by the organization about usage of Pydantic within the organization.

We've included some extra detail where appropriate and already in the public domain.

content:
# How to customize fields？

### Initial Checks

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

I want to add my own fields to an existing field with type annotations. How can I do this? Can I inherit FieldInfo and set new properties?

For example

```py
class CustomFiledInfo(FieldInfo):
    c_type: bool = None

class Demo(BaseModel):
    tags: str = CustomFiledInfo(c_type=False)
```

### Affected Components

- [ ] [Compatibility between releases](https://docs.pydantic.dev/changelog/)
- [ ] [Data validation/parsing](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/models/#basic-model-usage)
- [ ] [Data serialization](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/serialization/) - `.model_dump()` and `.model_dump_json()`
- [ ] [JSON Schema](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/json_schema/)
- [ ] [Dataclasses](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/dataclasses/)
- [ ] [Model Config](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/config/)
- [ ] [Field Types](https://docs.pydantic.dev/api/types/) - adding or changing a particular data type
- [ ] [Function validation decorator](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/validation_decorator/)
- [ ] [Generic Models](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/models/#generic-models)
- [ ] [Other Model behaviour](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/models/) - `model_construct()`, pickling, private attributes, ORM mode
- [ ] [Plugins](https://docs.pydantic.dev/) and integration with other tools - mypy, FastAPI, python-devtools, Hypothesis, VS Code, PyCharm, etc.

## Maintainer reply

Several libraries are subclassing the `FieldInfo` class to provide extra attributes (notably FastAPI). I would strongly discourage doing so for a couple reasons:
- The `FieldInfo` class is considered public API, but is still quite fragile. There are a number of design flaws with the architecture and logic of the class, in particular the methods attached to it. We also have some logic to merge instances together, e.g. when doing `field: Annotated[int, Field(...)] = Field(...)`. This merging logic will not work well if you provide extra attributes.
- We make use of the [`@dataclass_transform`](https://typing.readthedocs.io/en/latest/spec/dataclasses.html#the-dataclass-transform-decorator) typing feature to indicate to static type checkers that `BaseModel` behaves like a dataclass. `pydantic.Field()` is indicated to be a [field specifier](https://typing.readthedocs.io/en/latest/spec/dataclasses.html#field-specifiers), and so `CustomFieldInfo` won't be recognized.

Could provide more info on what is your use case? How is `c_type` going to be used?

metadata.title: How to customize fields？

# json validation loses data element in casting to python object

### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

I have noticed an odd problem. It may be related to issue #9544.

Given:
- A source file with json format data, containing an element "production_date" at four levels deep in the hierarchy. There are multiple tuples at this level and each has a production date value similar to "May 24".
- A Pydantic model "StatementContract" that is comprised of ten classes defining a document structure and and eleventh class that references the other ten.
- A "json_data" is dictionary read from a file. The file was built using the same StatementContract in another module.

When I step over this statement:
`report_data = StatementContract(**json_data)`

The corresponding element in report_data are empty strings. In the example provided, the production date in json_data is "May 24" and that same element in report_data is ' '. All other data element found in the json source are present in report_data.

The archive "json_convert_test.zip" contains four files necessary to run the test; source data in json format, pydantic model, required validator file, and the test code.

[json_convert_test.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/18848425/json_convert_test.zip)

Note: this is a duplicate of #11447, which was closed before I uploaded the test files.

### Example Code

```Python
import json
from model_revenue_stmt_0103_test import RunStatementContract

print("test json validation and load python object")
json_source_file = "Revenue_Statement.json"
try:
    with open(json_source_file, "r") as f:
        nested_json = json.load(f)
        print("nested_json loaded")
except Exception as e:
    raise Exception(f"Unexpected error reading file: {json_source_file}\n Error: {str(e)}")

# validate the model and load report_data
try:
    report_data = RunStatementContract(**nested_json)
except Exception as e:
    raise Exception(f"Error parsing JSON data into RunStatementContract object. Error: {str(e)}")

for property_report in report_data.property_reports:
    for product in property_report.products:
        for detail in product.transaction_details:
            assert detail.production_date == "May 24"
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.10.6
pydantic-core version: 2.29.0
pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=false
install path: ...venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/pydantic
python version: 3.12.8 (main, Dec  3 2024, 18:42:41) [Clang 16.0.0 (clang-1600.0.26.4)]
platform: macOS-15.3.1-arm64-arm-64bit
related packages: typing_extensions-4.12.2
commit: unknown
```

## Maintainer reply

Hi,

You'll need to remove the `alias` line from this class:

```py
class TransactionDetail(BaseModel):

    production_date: str = Field(
        description="The production date of the transaction, column name: 'Production Date' position: 0.",
        # alias='Sale Date',
        examples=['02/12/2025', 'May 24', '05/2025', '05/25', '0525'],
        default='',
    )
```

The `production_date` is found under the `production_date` name, not the `Sale Date` alias, ex:

```json
"transaction_details": [
	{
		"production_date": "May 24",
		"transaction_code_type": "RI",
		"tax_deduct_code": "",
		"owner_interest": "0.02500000",
		"owner_distribution_interest": "0.02500000",
		"property_btu": "1.3624",
		"property_volume": "77982.00",
		"property_price": "1949.54",
		"property_value": "-60123.48",
		"property_taxes": "-0.77",
		"property_net_value": "-60123.48",
		"owner_volume": "1949.54",
		"owner_value": "0.01",
		"owner_taxes": "0.01",
		"owner_net_value": "0.01"
	},
]
```

content:
# Unable to generate pydantic-core schema for a subclass of str

### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

I have a subclass of str which implements `__new__` to validate or generate a raw UUID string and is used as a default factory for a model field. It worked with Pydantic v1, but now it fails when attempting to generate the schema:

```
pydantic.errors.PydanticSchemaGenerationError: Unable to generate pydantic-core schema for <class '__main__.ID'>. Set `arbitrary_types_allowed=True` in the model_config to ignore this error or implement `__get_pydantic_core_schema__` on your type to fully support it.

If you got this error by calling handler(<some type>) within `__get_pydantic_core_schema__` then you likely need to call `handler.generate_schema(<some type>)` since we do not call `__get_pydantic_core_schema__` on `<some type>` otherwise to avoid infinite recursion
```

I have zero knowledge of Pydantic under the hood, but it works with `if issubclass(obj, str)` instead of `if obj is str`.
In `pydantic/_internal/_generate_schema.py::GenerateSchema::match_type`

Please advise. Many thanks! 🙏

### Example Code

```Python
from uuid import UUID, uuid4
import typing as t

from pydantic import BaseModel, Field

class ID(str):
    def __new__(cls, value: t.Optional[str] = None) -> "ID":
        if value:
            # Validation
            UUID(value)
            id_ = value
        else:
            id_ = str(uuid4())
        return t.cast("ID", id_)

class DatabaseRecord(BaseModel):
    id_: ID = Field(
        default_factory=ID,
        alias="_id",
        frozen=True,
    )
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.10.6
pydantic-core version: 2.27.2
pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=false
install path: .venv/lib/python3.13/site-packages/pydantic
python version: 3.13.2 (main, Feb  5 2025, 18:58:04) [Clang 19.1.6 ]
platform: macOS-15.2-arm64-arm-64bit-Mach-O
related packages: typing_extensions-4.12.2
```

## Maintainer reply

You'll have to define a `__get_pydantic_core_schema__()` method on your subclass, as per https://docs.pydantic.dev/dev/concepts/types/#as-a-method-on-a-custom-type.

Note that defining core schemas will require some knowledge of the `pydantic_core` schemas defined in https://docs.pydantic.dev/dev/api/pydantic_core_schema/. We are aware of the complexity, and are working on a alternative API.

metadata.title: Unable to generate pydantic-core schema for a subclass of str

content:
# Nested Validation Errors in Pydantic Models Do Not Propagate Properly

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

When creating nested Pydantic models, validation errors from an inner model are not fully propagated to the outer model in certain scenarios, especially when exceptions are re-raised or wrapped during outer model validation. This behavior makes it difficult to debug issues in complex model hierarchies.

In the example below, an InnerModel fails validation because a field does not meet its constraints. However, when this failure occurs during the validation of an OuterModel, the error message only reflects the outer validation failure, potentially obscuring the root cause.

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel, ValidationError, validator

class InnerModel(BaseModel):
    age: int

    @validator("age")
    def check_age_must_be_positive(cls, value):
        if value <= 0:
            raise ValueError("Age must be positive")
        return value

class OuterModel(BaseModel):
    name: str

    @validator("name")
    def create_inner_model(cls, value):
        # Attempt to create the InnerModel with invalid data
        # Intentionally use a negative age to trigger an error
        InnerModel(age=-1)
        return value

if __name__ == "__main__":
    try:
        OuterModel(name="Testing")
    except ValidationError as e:
        print("----- Python Exception Output -----")
        print(e)            # Human-readable error
        print("----- JSON Error Output -----")
        print(e.json())     # JSON-formatted error
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.9.2
        pydantic-core version: 2.23.4
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=false
                 install path: /home/mlivne/Research/local/lib/python3.10/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.10.12 (main, Nov  6 2024, 20:22:13) [GCC 11.4.0]
                     platform: Linux-5.15.0-127-generic-x86_64-with-glibc2.35
             related packages: typing_extensions-4.12.2
                       commit: unknown
```

## Maintainer reply

Validation errors are meant to hold information about the field where validation failed. if you raise a `ValidationError` inside the `name` field validator, Pydantic is going to get confused (as you can see, the loc is `name.age`).

What are you trying to achieve? Validating other models inside field validators is pretty uncommon.

metadata.title: Nested Validation Errors in Pydantic Models Do Not Propagate Properly

# RedisDsn.build Adds always adds path /0

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

RedisDsn.build adds a path even though it should not, as is described in the documentation.
Behaviour changes from 2.9.x to 2.10.x.

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import RedisDsn

key_value_store: RedisDsn = RedisDsn.build(
    scheme='redis',
    username='username',
    password='password',
    host='localhost',
    port=6379,
)

print(key_value_store)
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.10.4
        pydantic-core version: 2.27.2
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=false
                 install path: /xxx/virtualenvs/xxxx/lib/python3.12/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.12.8 (main, Jan  7 2025, 09:49:22) [Clang 16.0.0 (clang-1600.0.26.6)]
                     platform: macOS-15.2-arm64-arm-64bit
             related packages: fastapi-0.115.6 mypy-1.14.1 pydantic-settings-2.7.1 typing_extensions-4.12.2
                       commit: unknown
```

## Maintainer reply

@Narquadah,

Thanks for the report, this is because we have a `default_path` specified for `RedisDsn`:

```py
class RedisDsn(AnyUrl):
    """A type that will accept any Redis DSN.

    * User info required
    * TLD not required
    * Host required (e.g., `rediss://:pass@localhost`)
    """

    _constraints = UrlConstraints(
        allowed_schemes=['redis', 'rediss'],
        default_host='localhost',
        default_port=6379,
        default_path='/0',
        host_required=True,
    )
```

metadata.title: RedisDsn.build Adds always adds path /0

content:
# PostgresDsn required host - should be optional

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

There is assumption in the code that host is required parameter in PostgresDsn. 

I do think there a number of cases where you won't submit `host` as a parameter as it's not required in many PG drivers. In Google Cloud you can connect in Cloud Run and other environment via unix socket file never going via host network. Example: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/python-docs-samples/blob/main/cloud-sql/postgres/sqlalchemy/connect_unix.py#L36-L37 
```python
        # postgresql+pg8000://<db_user>:<db_pass>@/<db_name>
        #                         ?unix_sock=<INSTANCE_UNIX_SOCKET>/.s.PGSQL.5432
        # Note: Some drivers require the `unix_sock` query parameter to use a different key.
        # For example, 'psycopg2' uses the path set to `host` in order to connect successfully.
        sqlalchemy.engine.url.URL.create(
            drivername="postgresql+pg8000",
            username=db_user,
            password=db_pass,
            database=db_name,
            query={"unix_sock": f"{unix_socket_path}/.s.PGSQL.5432"},
        ),
```
The URI part `postgresql+pg8000://<db_user>:<db_pass>@/<db_name>?unix_sock=<INSTANCE_UNIX_SOCKET>/.s.PGSQL.5432` doesn't have at all host and that is ok for a lot of drivers.

### Example Code & unittest

```python
PostgresDsn.build(scheme="postgres")
```
unittest:
```python
class Model(BaseModel):
    a: PostgresDsn

empty_host = Model(a='postgres://user:pass@/app?unix=/cloudsql/project:us-east1:server/.s.PGSQL.5432').a
assert str(empty_host) == 'postgres://user:pass@/app?unix=/cloudsql/project:us-east1:server/.s.PGSQL.5432'
 ```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.10.4
        pydantic-core version: 2.27.2
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=false
                 install path: /Users/kamil/Projects/OpenAMA/.venv/lib/python3.13/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.13.0 (main, Nov 25 2024, 08:23:03) [Clang 16.0.0 (clang-1600.0.26.4)]
                     platform: macOS-15.1.1-arm64-arm-64bit-Mach-O
             related packages: fastapi-0.115.6 pydantic-settings-2.7.0 typing_extensions-4.12.2
                       commit: unknown
```

## Maintainer reply

Happy to look into this later this week, might need to be fixed in v2.10.5.

from pydantic import BaseModel, SkipValidation, Field
import numpy as np

from typing import Generic, TypeVar, Annotated
import functools

_T = TypeVar("_T")


class BaseGeneric(BaseModel, Generic[_T]):
    pytype: Annotated[type[_T], SkipValidation, Field(frozen=True, exclude=True)]

    def __init__(self, **kwargs):
        super().__init__(pytype=self._cls_get_pytype(), **kwargs)

    @classmethod
    @functools.cache
    def _cls_get_pytype(cls) -> type[_T]:
        return typing.get_args(cls.model_fields["pytype"].annotation)[0]

content:
# 'Url' type casts left around when serializing model to a dictionary

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

When serializing a model using URLs back to a dictionary, provided the URLs are properly represented, and validated using any of the network URL types, `Url/AnyUrl/HttpUrl` are explicitly formatted as type casts to the serialized dictionary  (depending on the exact pydantic v2 release)  which makes it hard to deterministically compare data as plain dictionaries in unit tests.
I used the word "deterministically" because prior pydantic  2.10.2 it seems like all URL types where formatted as `Url(<url>)`, but after **2.10.2** where there were adjustments to the URL validation constraints the actual types are honored in the serialized dictionary.
I don't think the type casts should be dumped to a serialized dictionary once a model has been successfully validated, making it completely transparent to the user what the model does/did in the background.

[1] https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/concepts/serialization/#modelmodel_dump

### Example Code

```Python
from typing import Optional
from pydantic import BaseModel, AnyUrl
from pprint import pprint


class SubModel(BaseModel):
    bar: AnyUrl
    baz: int = 1


class MainModel(BaseModel):
    foo: Optional[str] = None
    submodels: list[SubModel] = []


m = {
    "foo": "foo",
    "submodels":
        [
            {"bar": "https://example.com"},
            {"bar": "http://www.example.com/path", "baz": 2}
        ],
}

pprint(MainModel.model_validate(m).model_dump())


>>> python3 test.py
{'foo': 'foo',
 'submodels': [{'bar': AnyUrl('https://example.com/'), 'baz': 1},
               {'bar': AnyUrl('http://www.example.com/path'), 'baz': 2}]}
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.10.2
pydantic-core version: 2.27.1
pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=false
python version: 3.12.7 (main, Oct  1 2024, 00:00:00) [GCC 14.2.1 20240912 (Red Hat 14.2.1-3)]
platform: Linux-6.11.5-200.fc40.x86_64-x86_64-with-glibc2.39
related packages: mypy-1.13.0 typing_extensions-4.12.2 mypy-1.13.0 typing_extensions-4.12.2
```

## Maintainer reply

Hi!

Thanks for your question. I don't think this is something we're going to change now - we're trying to reduce further churn on the url front, and this behavior is much more like that which you'd expect from other concrete python classes.

That being said, one of the following solutions might be helpful to you:

```py
class SubModel(BaseModel):
    bar: Annotated[AnyUrl, PlainSerializer(lambda x: x._url)]
    baz: int = 1
```

```py
class SubModel(BaseModel):
    bar: Annotated[AnyUrl, PlainSerializer(lambda x: str(x))]
    baz: int = 1
```

I'd recommend the second approach, as it makes deterministic comparisons easier, and it doesn't depend on internal implementation details of the url class variations.

Hope this helps. Happy to chat more if you have questions!

metadata.title: 'Url' type casts left around when serializing model to a dictionary

content:
# Union validation fails with AttributeError on types that aren't first in the union

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

We first noticed an issue where Union validation wasn't being applied to nested LangChain models in an expected way, calling the nested model's validator in a way that raised an AttributeError and stopped model creation entirely (simple fix to remove typing on ControlFlow repo is [here](https://github.com/PrefectHQ/ControlFlow/pull/376)). 

However, I created an MRE that demonstrates the behavior without any of our logic. The code is below. We create a BaseModel with a single `model` field that is a union of the ChatOpenAI and AzureChatOpenAI langchain models. In one BaseModel, the OpenAI model is listed first in the union; in the other, the Azure model is listed first. 

When you instantiate the BaseModels, whichever langchain model was listed first in the union can be loaded successfully; whichever one is listed second fails. 

Note that this is using default union logic; setting to `left_to_right` doesnt seem to make a difference.

### Example Code

```Python
import controlflow as cf
from langchain_openai import AzureChatOpenAI, ChatOpenAI
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from typing import Union

azure = AzureChatOpenAI(api_version='1', azure_endpoint='2')
openai = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o")


class OpenAIFirst(BaseModel):
    model: Union[ChatOpenAI, AzureChatOpenAI]

class AzureFirst(BaseModel):
    model: Union[AzureChatOpenAI, ChatOpenAI]



# when chatopenai is on the left, azure fails     


OpenAIFirst(model=openai)
print("openai passed")

try:
    OpenAIFirst(model=azure)
except Exception as e:
    print(e)


# when azure is on the left, openai fails


AzureFirst(model=azure)
print("azure passed")

try:
    AzureFirst(model=openai)
except Exception as e:
    print(e)
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.9.2
        pydantic-core version: 2.23.4
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=false
                 install path: /Users/jlowin/Developer/controlflow/.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.12.2 (main, Feb 25 2024, 03:55:42) [Clang 17.0.6 ]
                     platform: macOS-15.1-arm64-arm-64bit
             related packages: pydantic-settings-2.6.1 mypy-1.13.0 fastapi-0.115.4 pydantic-extra-types-2.10.0 typing_extensions-4.12.2
                       commit: unknown
```

## Maintainer reply

@jlowin,

What error are you getting? Can you show the `print(e)` output?

# Alias Generator Not Converting Field Names to CamelCase with .model_dump(by_alias=True)

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

Hey,

I'm trying to use `Field` and `alias_generator` in Pydantic, but it doesn't seem to affect the field names when I use `.model_dump(by_alias=True)`. The fields remain in `snake_case` instead of being converted to camelCase.

The output I get is `{'current_account': '12345'}`

The expected output is `{'currentAccount': '12345'}`

Could you please help me resolve this issue?

Thanks!

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict, Field
from pydantic.alias_generators import to_camel

class Account(BaseModel):
    model_config = ConfigDict(populate_by_name=True, alias_generator=to_camel)
    my_account: str = Field(serialization_alias="current_account")

class Account_2(BaseModel):
    model_config = ConfigDict(populate_by_name=True, alias_generator=to_camel)
    my_account: str

if __name__ == '__main__':
    a = Account(my_account="12345")
    print(a.model_dump())
    print(a.model_dump(by_alias=True))
    a_2 = Account_2(my_account="6789")
    print(a_2.model_dump())
    print(a_2.model_dump(by_alias=True))

# output

{'my_account': '12345'}
{'current_account': '12345'}  # expected `{'currentAccount': '12345'}`
{'my_account': '6789'}
{'myAccount': '6789'}

`
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.9.2
pydantic-core version: 2.23.4
pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=false
install path: C:\Users\MYUSER\PycharmProjects\INTERPRETER\gw\Lib\site-packages\pydantic
python version: 3.11.6 (tags/v3.11.6:8b6ee5b, Oct  2 2023, 14:57:12) [MSC v.1935 64 bit (AMD64)]
platform: Windows-10-10.0.19045-SP0
related packages: fastapi-0.115.0 mypy-1.11.2 pydantic-extra-types-2.9.0 pydantic-settings-2.5.2 typing_extensions-4.12.2
commit: unknown
```

## Maintainer reply

As per [the documentation](https://docs.pydantic.dev/dev/concepts/alias/#using-an-aliasgenerator), the `serialization_alias` takes precedence over the `alias_generator`, unless you change the alias priority. If you want the `serialization_alias` to be converted using `to_camel`, this is not possible. What prevents you from doing `my_account: str = Field(serialization_alias="currentAccount")`?

content:
# `str` does not converted to `Enum` as expected when extra typehints provided.

### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

Single typehint `Enum` are converted successfully but after providing extra, things just don't work properly. 
Case where `decimal` -> `float` conveting works as expected whatever typehints provided after leading one.
Might be a mistake somewhere...

### Example Code

```Python
from enum import Enum
from typing import Any
from decimal import Decimal
from pydantic import BaseModel


class PeriodType(Enum):
    PAST_DAY: str = 'PAST_DAY'
    PAST_WEEK: str = 'PAST_WEEK'
    PAST_MONTH: str = 'PAST_MONTH'
    PAST_YEAR: str = 'PAST_YEAR'
    CURRENT_DAY: str = 'CURRENT_DAY'


class ModelWithConvertingToEnum(BaseModel):
    values: list[PeriodType]  # converts as expected
    value_non_list: PeriodType  # converts as expected


class ModelWithoutConvertingToEnum(BaseModel):
    values: list[PeriodType | Any]  # do not converts str -> enum due first position enum type
    value_non_list: PeriodType | Any  # do not converts str -> enum due first position enum type


class ModelWithConvertingOtherWay(BaseModel):
    values: list[float | Any]  # converts decimal -> float due first position float type
    value_non_list: float | Any  # converts decimal -> float due first position float type


# values=[<PeriodType.PAST_DAY: 'PAST_DAY'>] value_non_list=<PeriodType.PAST_DAY: 'PAST_DAY'>
print(ModelWithConvertingToEnum(values=[PeriodType.PAST_DAY.value], value_non_list=PeriodType.PAST_DAY.value))

# values=['PAST_DAY'] value_non_list='PAST_DAY'
print(ModelWithoutConvertingToEnum(values=[PeriodType.PAST_DAY.value], value_non_list=PeriodType.PAST_DAY.value))

# values=[1.1] value_non_list=1.1
print(ModelWithConvertingOtherWay(values=[Decimal(1.1), ], value_non_list=Decimal(1.1)))
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.9.2
        pydantic-core version: 2.23.4
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=false
                 install path: /home/james/projects/bi-reports-api/venv_313/lib/python3.13/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.13.0 (main, Oct  8 2024, 08:51:27) [GCC 9.4.0]
                     platform: Linux-5.15.153.1-microsoft-standard-WSL2-x86_64-with-glibc2.31
             related packages: pydantic-settings-2.5.2 pydantic-extra-types-2.9.0 typing_extensions-4.12.2 fastapi-0.115.2
                       commit: unknown
```

## Maintainer reply

@JamesCrumble,

Thanks for your question. We offer builtin support for this kind of preference. You can use our `union_mode` setting with a `left_to_right` specification as follows:

Instead of doing:
```py
PeriodType | Any
```

Do:
```py
from typing import Annotated
from pydantic import Field

Annotated[PeriodType | Any, Field(union_mode='left_to_right')]
```

This ensures that the first union member that qualifies as a match (either in lax or strict mode is used), so in this case, you'll ge the expected result of conversion to an enum.

We default to `smart` mode which attempts to pick the best match. In the case above, it was choosing `Any`, as the match to your `PeriodType` enum was lax.

Let me know if you have any other questions, hope this helps!

metadata.title: `str` does not converted to `Enum` as expected when extra typehints provided.

# No validation of timezone when using annotated datetime with annotated-types

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

I have a datetime that I want to be validated as aware and in UTC. I'm following the docs [here](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/concepts/types/#composing-types-via-annotated) to annotate the datetime as such. I expect the initialization of the model to fail, but it succeeds. When I try a similar approach by annotating an integer as greater than a value the validation works as expected.

I hope this is clear enough, and that I'm using the library correctly.

I'm on `annotated-types==0.7.0` if it matters.

Thanks for this great library by the way. Excellent stuff!

### Example Code

```Python
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from typing import Annotated

from annotated_types import Gt, Timezone
from pydantic import BaseModel, ValidationError


class Event(BaseModel):
    date: Annotated[datetime, Timezone(timezone.utc)]


class Adult(BaseModel):
    age: Annotated[int, Gt(18)]


try:
    Adult(age=12)
except ValidationError:
    pass  # This is expected


try:
    Event(date=datetime(2024, 1, 1, 12, 0, 0))
except ValidationError:
    pass  # This is expected
else:
    raise Exception("No validation error 😢")
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.9.2
        pydantic-core version: 2.23.4
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=false
                 install path: /home/tgurion/code/pydantic-annotated-types/.venv/lib/python3.13/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.13.0rc2 (main, Sep  9 2024, 22:13:26) [Clang 18.1.8 ]
                     platform: Linux-6.8.0-47-generic-x86_64-with-glibc2.35
             related packages: typing_extensions-4.12.2
                       commit: unknown
```

## Maintainer reply

@Nagasaki45,

Thanks for your question!

We actually have a pretty detailed example in the [docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/examples/custom_validators/#custom-datetime-validator-via-annotated-metadata) showing how to do validation with a constrained timezone.

I'm hoping that helps to clarify the issue here. That being said, if you think an additional docs update would be helpful here, feel free to open a PR and I'd be happy to review!

One other point - we don't support `Timezone` as a constraint at the moment, hence your initial example not working :)

content:
# Private attribute values are retrieved differently in instance or class methods

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

Hi ! Thanks a lot for Pydantic
There is a difference in the way `PrivateAttr` is treated between instance methods and class methods.

* instance methods access private attributes seamlessly. On the minimal example `_float` can be casted to float
* class methods don't work with the same piece of code. `_float` is now retrieved as a `PrivateAttr`, and the value must be accessed through `PrivateAttr.default`

I wouldn't expect that behavior, but i must admit i don't know Pydantic that well

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel


class Model(BaseModel):
    _float = 2.1

    @classmethod
    def f(cls) -> None:
        float(cls._float)  # Failing

    def g(self) -> None:
        float(self._float)  # Passes


Model().g()  # Passes
Model.f()  # Failing
```


### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.9.2
        pydantic-core version: 2.23.4
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=false
                 install path: C:\Users\XXX\.venv\Lib\site-packages\pydantic
               python version: 3.12.2 (tags/v3.12.2:6abddd9, Feb  6 2024, 21:26:36) [MSC v.1937 64 bit (AMD64)]
                     platform: Windows-11-10.0.22631-SP0
             related packages: typing_extensions-4.12.2
                       commit: unknown
```

## Maintainer reply

Hi @ldouteau,

You can use a `ClassVar` annotation if you'd like to be able to access `_float` from the `cls` seamlessly:

```py
from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import ClassVar


class Model(BaseModel):
    _float: ClassVar = 2.1

    @classmethod
    def f(cls) -> None:
        float(cls._float)  # Failing

    def g(self) -> None:
        float(self._float)  # Passes


Model().g()  # Passes
Model.f()  # Failing
```

Going to close as resolved for now. Basically, it's inferred that `_float` is an instance attribute unless you specify otherwise.

metadata.title: Private attribute values are retrieved differently in instance or class methods

content:
# revalidate_instances conflict with SerializeAsAny

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

When using SerializeAsAny +  revalidate_instances + layered inheritances, SerializeAsAny is not anymore respected.

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel, SerializeAsAny


class commonbase(
    BaseModel,
    revalidate_instances="subclass-instances",  # <--- toogle to generate error or not
): ...


class basechild(commonbase):
    test_val: int = 1


class derivedchild(basechild):
    test_val2: int = 2


class container(commonbase):

    ct_child_1: dict[str, basechild] = {}
    ct_child_2: SerializeAsAny[dict[str, basechild]] = {}
    ct_child_3: dict[str, SerializeAsAny[basechild]] = {}


if __name__ == "__main__":
    test_val = container(
        ct_child_1={"test1": derivedchild()},
        ct_child_2={"test2": derivedchild()},
        ct_child_3={"test3": derivedchild()},
    )

    # print(test_val.model_dump_json(indent=1))
    # print(test_val.model_dump())

    assert "test_val2" not in test_val.model_dump()["ct_child_1"]["test1"]
    assert "test_val2" in test_val.model_dump()["ct_child_2"]["test2"]
    assert "test_val2" in test_val.model_dump()["ct_child_3"]["test3"]
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.9.2
        pydantic-core version: 2.23.4
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=false
                 install path: C:\Users\chacha\AppData\Roaming\Python\Python311\site-packages\pydantic
               python version: 3.11.8 (tags/v3.11.8:db85d51, Feb  6 2024, 22:03:32) [MSC v.1937 64 bit (AMD64)]
                     platform: Windows-10-10.0.22631-SP0
             related packages: mypy-1.9.0 typing_extensions-4.10.0
                       commit: unknown
```

## Maintainer reply

Hi @cclecle,

Thanks for your question. This is the behavior we'd expect. When you revalidate subclass instances, you effectively discard attributes associated with the subclass and not the superclass that you're validating against.

So post validation, you have the following:

```py
print(repr(test_val))
#> container(ct_child_1={'test1': basechild(test_val=1)}, ct_child_2={'test2': basechild(test_val=1)}, ct_child_3={'test3': basechild(test_val=1)})
```

Which is why it makes sense that no `test_val2` keys would ever be present in the `model_dump` output, no matter your `SerializeAsAny` specification.

If you'd still like to preserve that information, I'd recommend not setting the `revalidate_instances` setting as you have it.

metadata.title: revalidate_instances conflict with SerializeAsAny

content:
# Allow customizing `loc` for model validators

### Initial Checks

- [X] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
  - (I have actually searched GH for similar requests and found #8092 and #9686, which touch upon this.) 
- [X] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

Given something like

```python
from typing import Literal, Self

import pydantic


class ServiceConfiguration(pydantic.BaseModel):
    access_key: str | None = None
    endpoint_url: pydantic.HttpUrl | Literal[""] | None = None

    @pydantic.model_validator(mode="after")
    def check_access_key_format(self) -> Self:
        if self.endpoint_url:  # Customized endpoint, do not check format.
            return self
        if self.access_key and not self.access_key.islower():
            raise ValueError(
                "Access key must be lower case. The default service does not tolerate shouting and loud noises.")
        return self


ServiceConfiguration.model_validate({"access_key": "VERYVERYLOUD"})
```

I would like to be able to customize the `loc` for the resulting error to be `'access_key'`, since the error is actually an error in that field, so I can eventually format the `pydantic.ValidationError` to neatly nested JSON with a reference to the actual field that had the issue. 

Some ideas for implementing this:

* Allow setting `field_name` for `model_validator()`s too, and use it for `loc`. Not very dynamic, maybe overloads the `field_name` name too much?
* Read a `loc` or `_pydantic_loc` or similar field off the exception user code raises, use it if available. Simple enough, but also relies on the implicit magic that this is a thing, and creating such an exception is not idiomatic (and may not be possible, if the exception class has e.g. slots):
  ```python
  exc = ValueError("No shouting!")
  exc.loc = ("access_key",)
  raise exc
  ```
* A Pydantic-specific extra error info wrapper exception type?
  ```python
  raise pydantic.ErrorWithContext(ValueError("No shouting!"), loc=("access_key", ))
  ```
  Feels pretty elegant, should be easy to peel off and process in the Rust core code?
* Add `loc` to [`PydanticCustomError`](https://docs.pydantic.dev/dev/api/pydantic_core/#pydantic_core.PydanticCustomError)?


### Affected Components

- [ ] [Compatibility between releases](https://docs.pydantic.dev/changelog/)
- [X] [Data validation/parsing](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/models/#basic-model-usage)
- [ ] [Data serialization](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/serialization/) - `.model_dump()` and `.model_dump_json()`
- [ ] [JSON Schema](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/json_schema/)
- [ ] [Dataclasses](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/dataclasses/)
- [ ] [Model Config](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/config/)
- [ ] [Field Types](https://docs.pydantic.dev/api/types/) - adding or changing a particular data type
- [ ] [Function validation decorator](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/validation_decorator/)
- [ ] [Generic Models](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/models/#generic-models)
- [ ] [Other Model behaviour](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/models/) - `model_construct()`, pickling, private attributes, ORM mode
- [ ] [Plugins](https://docs.pydantic.dev/) and integration with other tools - mypy, FastAPI, python-devtools, Hypothesis, VS Code, PyCharm, etc.

## Maintainer reply

Hi @akx,

Thanks for your inquiry.

You can do what you're hoping to with the following:

```py
from typing import Literal, Self

import pydantic
from pydantic_core import InitErrorDetails, PydanticCustomError
from pydantic import ValidationError


class ServiceConfiguration(pydantic.BaseModel):
    access_key: str | None = None
    endpoint_url: pydantic.HttpUrl | Literal[''] | None = None

    @pydantic.model_validator(mode='after')
    def check_access_key_format(self) -> Self:
        if self.endpoint_url:  # Customized endpoint, do not check format.
            return self
        if self.access_key and not self.access_key.islower():
            raise ValidationError.from_exception_data(
                'LowerCaseError',
                [
                    InitErrorDetails(
                        type=PydanticCustomError(
                            'str_not_lower',
                            'Access key must be lower case. The default service does not tolerate shouting and loud noises.',
                        ),
                        loc=('access_key',),
                        input=self.access_key,
                    ),
                ],
            )
        return self


try:
    ServiceConfiguration.model_validate({'access_key': 'VERYVERYLOUD'})
except ValidationError as e:
    print(e.errors())
    """
    [{'type': 'str_not_lower', 'loc': ('access_key',), 'msg': 'Access key must be lower case. The default service does not tolerate shouting and loud noises.', 'input': 'VERYVERYLOUD'}]
    """
```

I'll be expanding our docs on this subject shortly. Going to mark as resolved for now 👍 

Also note, #9686 has recently been closed (resolved), so that will take effect in v2.10!

metadata.title: Allow customizing `loc` for model validators

content:
# pydantic.validate_call makes a copy of the list in order to validate it?

This is new to me. Could someone help me understand why pydantic has to make a copy of the list in order to validate it? 

If so, does it mean `pydantic.validate_call` is not supposed to be used for any function that does in-place operation inside? Is the behavior mentioned somewhere in the doc?

Thanks,

_Originally posted by @xyshell in https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/8165#issuecomment-2381601386_

## Maintainer reply

Hi @xyshell,

Thanks for the question. From our [migration guide](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/migration/#changes-to-pydanticbasemodel):

> In many cases, arguments passed to the constructor will be copied in order to perform validation and, where necessary, coercion. This is notable in the case of passing mutable objects as arguments to a constructor. You can see an example + more detail [here](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/concepts/models/#attribute-copies).

This applies to `validate_call` as well. I would say if you do want to do in place mutation of a type, but still want to validate said value, you'd be best suited to use a custom validator for said type to avoid the copying.

metadata.title: pydantic.validate_call makes a copy of the list in order to validate it?

content:
# model_validator is not respecting alias_generator or alias

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

I have a model_validator whos's aim is to convert None values to either 0 or "" depending on the default type. 

however when it is checking the class it doesn't respect the allias to do the matching pair.


### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict, Field, field_validator, model_validator

class Transaction(BaseModel):
    model_config = ConfigDict(alias_generator=to_camel)
    block_hash: Optional[str] = Field(None, alias="blockHash")
    block_number: Optional[int] = Field(None, alias="blockNumber")

    @model_validator(mode='before')
    def replace_none_values(cls, values):
        for field, value in values.items():
            if value is None:
                if cls.__annotations__.get(field) == Optional[int]: # LHS = None field is blockHash if interpreted correctly it should be Optional[int]
                    values[field] = 0
                elif cls.__annotations__.get(field) == Optional[str]:
                    values[field] = ""
        return values
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
python -c "import pydantic.version; print(pydantic.version.version_info())"
             pydantic version: 2.9.1
        pydantic-core version: 2.23.3
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=false
                 install path: /home/READACTED/.cache/pypoetry/virtualenvs/mempool-GBmrkzvG-py3.12/lib/python3.12/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.12.6 (main, Sep 10 2024, 00:05:17) [GCC 11.4.0]
                     platform: Linux-6.9.3-76060903-generic-x86_64-with-glibc2.35
             related packages: pydantic-settings-2.5.2 typing_extensions-4.12.2
                       commit: unknown
```

## Maintainer reply

Hi @Murat-U-Saglam,

This is because `cls.__annotations__` uses the field names directly.

You could do something like:

```py
from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict, Field, field_validator, model_validator
from pydantic.alias_generators import to_camel, to_snake
from typing import Optional


class Transaction(BaseModel):
    model_config = ConfigDict(alias_generator=to_camel)
    block_hash: Optional[str] = Field(None, alias='blockHash')
    block_number: Optional[int] = Field(None, alias='blockNumber')

    @model_validator(mode='before')
    def replace_none_values(cls, values):
        for field, value in values.items():
            if value is None:
                if (
                    cls.__annotations__.get(to_snake(field)) == Optional[int]
                ):  # LHS = None field is blockHash if interpreted correctly it should be Optional[int]
                    values[field] = 0
                elif cls.__annotations__.get(to_snake(field)) == Optional[str]:
                    values[field] = ''
        return values

txn = Transaction(blockHash=None, blockNumber=None)
print(repr(txn))
#> Transaction(block_hash='', block_number=0)
```

metadata.title: model_validator is not respecting alias_generator or alias

# json schema breaks with `Secret` type that uses default

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

When using the `Secret` support and the default value is set using the `Secret` type itself, pydantic raises a `TypeError`:
```
TypeError: Can't get secret type from Secret. Please use Secret[<type>], or subclass from Secret[<type>] instead.
```

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import Secret, BaseModel, Field

class InnerModel(BaseModel):
    val: str | None = None

InnerModelType = Secret[InnerModel]

class OuterModel(BaseModel):
    inner_model: InnerModelType = Field(default=InnerModelType(secret_value=InnerModel()))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    print(OuterModel.model_json_schema())
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.9.1
        pydantic-core version: 2.23.3
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=false
                 install path: /Users/romanisecke/.pyenv/versions/3.12.2/envs/platform-plugins-controller/lib/python3.12/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.12.2 (main, May  2 2024, 12:25:29) [Clang 15.0.0 (clang-1500.3.9.4)]
                     platform: macOS-14.6.1-arm64-arm-64bit
             related packages: pydantic-settings-2.5.2 fastapi-0.114.1 typing_extensions-4.12.2
                       commit: unknown
```

## Maintainer reply

Thanks for your question! Things should work as expected if you use `validate_default`, as shown below.

Indeed, you should just set the default with the inner type, not the secret wrapper.

```py
from pydantic import Secret, BaseModel, Field

class InnerModel(BaseModel):
    val: str | None = None

InnerModelType = Secret[InnerModel]

class OuterModel(BaseModel):
    inner_model: InnerModelType = Field(default=InnerModel(), validate_default=True)

print(OuterModel().model_dump())
#> {'inner_model': Secret('**********')}

```

content:
# Field parsing order is not taken into account for Decimal | str

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

First of all thanks for the amazing library 👏 

I have observed a change in behavior confirmed in 2.9.0 and 2.9.1 compared to 2.8.2.

The type order for Decimal and string in not taken into account. I haven't checked for other types that could behave the same.

# How it used to behave
Having a field annotated `Decimal | str` used to first try to parse to `Decimal` in `2.8.2` then to `str` if the string was not a valid Decimal (e.g. `"5%"`) .

# How it now behaves
In 2.9.0 and 2.9.1, it does not try to parse to Decimal, or at least it always parses to str if the input is a string.

# Changelog
I checked the changelog for 2.9.0 and didn't see anything directly mentioning this change but may have missed something 😕 

There is a tiny change mentionning `Decimal` but it does not look related.
> Move decimal.Decimal validation to _generate_schema.py by @sydney-runkle in https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/9977


With a little guidance, I'm up to try and fix this 🙂 

### Example Code

```Python
from decimal import Decimal

from pydantic import BaseModel

class Cover(BaseModel):
  deductible: Decimal | str

cover = Cover.model_validate({"deductible": "0"})

assert isinstance(cover.deductible, Decimal) # evaluates to "False"
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.9.1
        pydantic-core version: 2.23.3
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=false
                 install path: /Users/olivier/dev/test/.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.12.6 (main, Sep  6 2024, 19:03:47) [Clang 15.0.0 (clang-1500.3.9.4)]
                     platform: macOS-14.5-arm64-arm-64bit
             related packages: typing_extensions-4.11.0
                       commit: unknown
```

## Maintainer reply

Hi @olivierdolle,

Thanks for your question. This is the correct behavior - "0" validates in strict mode as a `str`, but only in `lax` mode as a `Decimal`, and so `str` is the "smarter" decision here (you're using smart mode for unions by default). If you'd like to prefer `Decimal` here, you can use `Field(union_mode='left_to_right')`:

```py
from decimal import Decimal

from pydantic import BaseModel, Field

class Cover(BaseModel):
  deductible: Decimal | str = Field(union_mode='left_to_right')

cover = Cover.model_validate({"deductible": "0"})

assert isinstance(cover.deductible, Decimal)
```

metadata.title: Field parsing order is not taken into account for Decimal | str

# Strict broken for floats

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

A strict mode model will accept integers into float fields.

The opposite /does/ work however (fails when inserting a float into an int field)

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict


class Test(BaseModel):
  model_config = ConfigDict(strict=True)
  a: float

x: int = int(1)
assert type(x) is not float
assert type(x) is int
assert not issubclass(float, int)
assert not issubclass(float, int)

Test(a = x) # should fail
```


### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.8.2
        pydantic-core version: 2.20.1
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
                 install path: /Users/owner/code/codewars/.venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.11.7 (main, Feb 11 2024, 14:54:11) [Clang 15.0.0 (clang-1500.0.40.1)]
                     platform: macOS-13.5-arm64-arm-64bit
             related packages: typing_extensions-4.12.2
                       commit: unknown
```

## Maintainer reply

@Mardoxx,

Thanks for your question. Though this seems counterintuitive at first, we've sourced this logic / behavior from [PEP 484](https://peps.python.org/pep-0484/#the-numeric-tower). Specifically:

> when an argument is annotated as having type float, an argument of type int is acceptable

content:
# Mutating `model_fields` and calling `model_rebuild` does not work as expected

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description



Was expecting dynamic updates made to `model_fields` to carry over to a child class.

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel
from pydantic.fields import FieldInfo

class Parent(BaseModel):
    foo: str

# Dynamically add field to Parent model
Parent.model_fields["bar"] = FieldInfo(annotation=str)
Parent.model_rebuild(force=True)

class Child(Parent):
    pass

Parent(foo="foo") # Validation error, Field 'bar' required
Child(foo="foo") # Works

print(Parent.model_fields) # {'foo': FieldInfo(annotation=str, required=True), 'bar': FieldInfo(annotation=str, required=True)}
print(Child.model_fields) # {'foo': FieldInfo(annotation=str, required=True)}
```
<details>

<summary>Original issue:</summary>

Was expecting dynamic updates made to `model_fields` to carry over to a child class when using `create_model()` in conjunction with the `__base__` argument. Seems this is not the case. Would have expected `model_fields` to be identical for the base and child class.

A working alternative is to iterate over `Parent.model_fields.items()` and pass as `**field_definitions` to `create_model()`

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel, create_model
from pydantic.fields import FieldInfo

class Parent(BaseModel):
    foo: str

# Dynamically add field to Parent model
Parent.model_fields["bar"] = FieldInfo(annotation=str)
Parent.model_rebuild(force=True)

# Expecting Child to inherit from Parent
Child = create_model("Child", __base__=Parent)

Parent(foo="foo") # Validation error, Field 'bar' required
Child(foo="foo") # Works

print(Parent.model_fields) # {'foo': FieldInfo(annotation=str, required=True), 'bar': FieldInfo(annotation=str, required=True)}
print(Child.model_fields) # {'foo': FieldInfo(annotation=str, required=True)}
```

</details>


### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.8.2
        pydantic-core version: 2.20.1
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
               python version: 3.10.13 (main, Jan 23 2024, 20:34:07) [Clang 15.0.0 (clang-1500.1.0.2.5)]
                     platform: macOS-14.6.1-arm64-arm-64bit
             related packages: fastapi-0.109.2 typing_extensions-4.12.2
                       commit: unknown
```

## Maintainer reply

Ah, interesting. This is because we don't update `Parent.__annotations__` when we do the model rebuild.

# ConfigDict setting ser_json_bytes not working as expected

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

Due to I receive UTF-8 encoding error during saving my Pydantic object to file which contains a `bytes` field, I stumbled over the `ser_json_bytes` configuration setting.

Fortunately, if I set `ser_json_bytes` from the default value **utf8** to **base64**, the save error goes away, but when I load the object back from file the `bytes` field **is not decoded automatically** as it is encoded during storing.

output of the code below:

```bash
pydantic version: 2.8.2
original:
name='my-item' id=123 data=b'\xff\xaaU\x01'
ffaa5501
{"name":"my-item","id":123,"data":"_6pVAQ=="}
read back:
name='my-item' id=123 data=b'_6pVAQ=='
5f36705641513d3d
```

After read back the object: when the data field is accessed it contains the **base64 string** and not the decoded data itself!
Do I something missing ?


### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict, version


class Base64Test(BaseModel):
    model_config = ConfigDict(ser_json_bytes='base64')

    name: str = ''
    id: int
    data: bytes


if __name__ == '__main__':

    base64_test = Base64Test(name='my-item', id=123, data=bytes([0xFF, 0xAA, 0x55, 0x01]))

    print(f'pydantic version: {version.VERSION}')

    print('original:')
    print(base64_test)
    print(base64_test.data.hex())

    json_string = base64_test.model_dump_json()
    print(json_string)

    print('read back:')
    read_back = base64_test.model_validate_json(json_string)
    print(read_back)
    print(read_back.data.hex())
```


### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
python -c "import pydantic.version; print(pydantic.version.version_info())"
             pydantic version: 2.8.2
        pydantic-core version: 2.20.1
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
                 install path: /home/ ... /lib-c5Oc5WvZ-py3.10/lib/python3.10/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.10.12 (main, Jul 29 2024, 16:56:48) [GCC 11.4.0]
                     platform: Linux-6.5.0-45-generic-x86_64-with-glibc2.38
             related packages: typing_extensions-4.12.2
                       commit: unknown
```

## Maintainer reply

Great question! We just added a `val_json_bytes` config flag. You can use it like this:

```py
from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict, version


class Base64Test(BaseModel):
    model_config = ConfigDict(val_json_bytes='base64', ser_json_bytes='base64')

    name: str = ''
    id: int
    data: bytes


base64_test = Base64Test(name='my-item', id=123, data=bytes([0xFF, 0xAA, 0x55, 0x01]))

print(f'pydantic version: {version.VERSION}')

print('original:')
print(base64_test)
print(base64_test.data.hex())

json_string = base64_test.model_dump_json()
print(json_string)

print('read back:')
read_back = base64_test.model_validate_json(json_string)
print(read_back)
print(read_back.data.hex())
```

This will be available in v2.9, released in a week or two. You can access it from `main` with v2.22.0 of `pydantic-core`!
