# The method `model_validate(cls, <derived-model-class>)` returns its derived model class, not self 

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

I really appreciate your hard work on the project. 😀

I found that the method `model_validate` can accept its derived model classes as the argument `obj` and returns its derived model, not self. But I'm not sure **1) if this is a bug** or **2) if I'm not using the method correctly**.

### Example Code

```Python
class Model(BaseModel):
    name: Annotated[
        str,
        Field()
    ]

class SubModel(Model):
    path: Annotated[
        str,
        Field()
    ]

model = Model.model_validate(
    SubModel(name="sub-model", path=".")
)

print(model, type(model))
# name='sub-model' path='.' <class '__main__.SubModel'>
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.7.4
        pydantic-core version: 2.18.4
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
                 install path: /home/user-ubuntu/projects/pyfdc/.venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.11.7 (main, Dec  8 2023, 18:56:58) [GCC 11.4.0]

             related packages: fastapi-0.111.0 pydantic-settings-2.3.3 typing_extensions-4.12.2
                       commit: unknown
```

## Maintainer reply

Hi @kim-youngseop-developer,

Good question - you can use the `revalidate-instances` config setting if you'd like to fine tune this behavior:

```py
from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict

class Model(BaseModel):
    name: str

    model_config = ConfigDict(revalidate_instances='subclass-instances')

class SubModel(Model):
    path: str

model = Model.model_validate(
    SubModel(name="sub-model", path=".")
)

print(model, type(model))
# name='sub-model' <class '__main__.Model'>
```

See more here: https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/api/config/#pydantic.config.ConfigDict.revalidate_instances

# Lazy Import for Field Types

### Initial Checks

- [X] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [X] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

Currently if you do:
```python
from typing import TYPE_CHECKING
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field

if TYPE_CHECKING:
    from somelib import SomeClass

class A(BaseModel, arbitrary_types_allowed=True):
    a: "SomeClass | None" = None

A()
```
This will raise:
```python
PydanticUserError: `A` is not fully defined; you should define `SomeClass`, then call `A.model_rebuild()`.
```

You will then have to do:
```python
from somelib import SomeClass
A.model_rebuild()
A()
```

I'm wondering if it's possible to define the depending import statement for the model, so that I can still use string type hint for defining model and only actually import it when the model is imported somewhere.

The main benefit of this is avoiding a very long import time when you have defined a lot of models with custom classes as field types which have to be imported from some other packages.

### Affected Components

- [ ] [Compatibility between releases](https://docs.pydantic.dev/changelog/)
- [ ] [Data validation/parsing](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/models/#basic-model-usage)
- [ ] [Data serialization](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/serialization/) - `.model_dump()` and `.model_dump_json()`
- [ ] [JSON Schema](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/json_schema/)
- [ ] [Dataclasses](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/dataclasses/)
- [ ] [Model Config](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/config/)
- [ ] [Field Types](https://docs.pydantic.dev/api/types/) - adding or changing a particular data type
- [ ] [Function validation decorator](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/validation_decorator/)
- [ ] [Generic Models](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/models/#generic-models)
- [X] [Other Model behaviour](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/models/) - `model_construct()`, pickling, private attributes, ORM mode
- [ ] [Plugins](https://docs.pydantic.dev/) and integration with other tools - mypy, FastAPI, python-devtools, Hypothesis, VS Code, PyCharm, etc.

## Maintainer reply

> and only actually import it when the model is imported somewhere.

There's no way for us to know when a model (or any object for that matter) is imported somewhere. `model_rebuild` will have to be called at some point. However, you can make use of the `_types_namespace` argument to avoid having to `from somelib import SomeClass` in the other module where you are also importing your model.

Maybe something like this could work for you:

```python
from importlib import import_module
from functools import partial
from typing import Any, ClassVar

from pydantic import BaseModel

def _import(spec: str) -> Any:
    module_path, obj = spec.rsplit(".", 1)
    module = import_module(module_path)
    return getattr(module, obj)

class A(BaseModel):
    build_imports: ClassVar[dict[str, Any]] = {
        "SomeClass": partial(_import, "somelib.SomeClass"),
    }

    @classmethod
    def model_rebuild(
        cls,
        *,
        force: bool = False,
        raise_errors: bool = True,
        _parent_namespace_depth: int = 2,
        _types_namespace: dict[str, Any] | None = None,
    ) -> bool | None:
        _types_namespace = _types_namespace or {}
        _types_namespace.update({k: imp() for k, imp in cls.build_imports.items()})
        return super().model_rebuild(force=force, raise_errors=raise_errors, _parent_namespace_depth=_parent_namespace_depth, _types_namespace=_types_namespace)

```

# Replacing __root__ usage inside create_model call which contains a discriminated union impossible in v2?

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

I am migrating my companies code from v1 to v2 but have come up against a snag. Most are easy changes, but I have spent two full days trying to figure out how to replace __root__ being used inside create_model. I have read a lot about RootModel and create_model but I can't find a way to not use __root__ _and_ do the discriminated union in one line. The initial error caused by running the code below with v2 is: "TypeError: To define root models, use `pydantic.RootModel` rather than a field called '__root__'."

I have tried replacing __root__ simply with root, using Annotated to hold the discriminated union, and not using create_model at all, but no luck.

Basically there is a tuple of pydantic models that can grow or shrink depending on what is returned by "m.SpecModel for m in get_calc_module_registry().mapping.values()". I believe this is the reason we want to create a model dynamically, but not 100% sure create_model is necessary after researching the topic.

I would really appreciate any help getting migrating this code to v2.

### Example Code

```Python
UnionCalcSpecsModel = create_model(
    'UnionCalcSpecsModel',
    __root__=(
        Union[tuple(m.SpecModel for m in get_calc_module_registry().mapping.values())], # this is nothing more than a tuple of pydantic models which all have the field calc_module
        Field(..., discriminator='calc_module')
    )
)
 
 
class CalcCoreConfigModel(BaseModel):
    """Define components for the full index config including calc_specs."""
 
    product_name: str
    date_range: str
    date_column: str
    id_column: str
    calc_specs: List[UnionCalcSpecsModel]  # type: ignore
    column_to_calc_weight: Optional[str]
    columns_relate_to_weight: Optional[List]
    base_profile_column_type: Optional[Dict[str, str]]
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.8.2
        pydantic-core version: 2.20.1
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
                 install path: /tmp_nt/ybhme/ybdev/wevns/PycharmProjects/proen/venv/lib/python3.8/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.8.18 (default, Oct 24 2023, 08:42:25)  [GCC 9.1.1 20190605 (Red Hat 9.1.1-2)]
                     platform: Linux-3.10.0-1160.114.2.el7.x86_64-x86_64-with-glibc2.2.5
             related packages: mypy-1.3.0 typing_extensions-4.12.2 fastapi-0.112.0 email-validator-2.0.0.post2 mypy-1.3.0 typing_extensions-4.12.2 fastapi-0.112.0 email-validator-2.0.0.post2
                       commit: unknown
```

## Maintainer reply

Hey @wynneevans,

Perhaps something like this would work? We don't really support `RootModel` nested usage.

```py
from pydantic import BaseModel, RootModel, Discriminator
from typing import Literal, Union, Dict, List, Type
from typing_extensions import Annotated


class Model1(BaseModel):
    calc_module: Literal['model1'] = 'model1'


class Model2(BaseModel):
    calc_module: Literal['model2'] = 'model2'


class Model3(BaseModel):
    calc_module: Literal['model3'] = 'model3'


def get_calc_module_registry() -> Dict[str, Type[BaseModel]]:
    """Simulating some dynamic registry."""
    return {
        'model1': Model1,
        'model2': Model2,
        'model3': Model3,
    }


class CalcCoreConfigModel(BaseModel):
    """Define components for the full index config including calc_specs."""

    calc_specs: List[
        Annotated[Union[tuple(m for m in get_calc_module_registry().values())], Discriminator('calc_module')]  # type: ignore
    ]


cccm = CalcCoreConfigModel.model_validate(
    {
        'calc_specs': [
            {'calc_module': 'model1'},
            {'calc_module': 'model2'},
            {'calc_module': 'model3'},
        ]
    }
)
print(repr(cccm))
# > CalcCoreConfigModel(calc_specs=[Model1(calc_module='model1'), Model2(calc_module='model2'), Model3(calc_module='model3')])
```

Marking as resolved for now, but feel free to follow up with me if you have more questions!

# `field_validator('*')` not called for extra fields

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

I am attempting to do some coercion for all fields, including any extra fields, but i can't do so with a `field_validator`. 

I see [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev/2.7/concepts/models/#extra-fields) say "by default, no validation will be applied to these extra items," but i would expect that i should be able to supply my own validation for those extra columns above and beyond the `__pydantic_extra__` annotation.

I am able to work around the problem using `@model_validator` , but it seems like all supplied fields, including extras, should be passed to `field_validator('*')`

If this is a bug rather than a wontfix, i would be happy to PR the behavior with a little guidance on what an acceptable implementation would look like since i know y'all have a high volume of issues <3

also sorry if this is a duplicate, i searched but couldn't find a previous issue



### Example Code

```Python
from typing import Any
from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict, field_validator, ValidationInfo, ValidatorFunctionWrapHandler
from numpydantic import NDArray

class MyModel(BaseModel):
    model_config = ConfigDict(extra="allow")
    __pydantic_extra__: dict[str, NDArray]
    expected_field: NDArray
    
    @field_validator("*", mode="wrap")
    @classmethod
    def cast_fields_wrap(
        cls, val: Any, handler: ValidatorFunctionWrapHandler, info: ValidationInfo
    ) -> Any:
        print(f"wrap: {info.field_name}")
        return handler(val)
    
    @field_validator("*", mode="before")
    @classmethod
    def cast_fields_before(
        cls, val: Any, info: ValidationInfo
    ) -> Any:
        print(f"before: {info.field_name}")
        return val
    
    @field_validator("*", mode="after")
    @classmethod
    def cast_fields_after(
        cls, val: Any, info: ValidationInfo
    ) -> Any:
        print(f"after: {info.field_name}")
        return val

# validator not called for extra_field
instance = MyModel(expected_field = [1,2,3], extra_field = [4,5,6])
# before: expected_field
# wrap: expected_field
# after: expected_field

# but the coercion that happens from the `NDArray` type is applied
instance
# MyModel(expected_field=array([1, 2, 3]), extra_field=array([4, 5, 6]))
```


### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.8.2
        pydantic-core version: 2.20.1
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=false
                 install path: .venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.11.3 (main, Jun  6 2023, 17:36:45) [Clang 12.0.5 (clang-1205.0.22.9)]
                     platform: macOS-14.3.1-x86_64-i386-64bit
             related packages: pydantic-settings-2.3.4 typing_extensions-4.12.2
                       commit: unknown
```

## Maintainer reply

Hi!

While we don't currently support `*` validation support via field validators, you can use annotations to express before / after / wrap validation logic for extra values.

For example:

```py
from typing import Any
from typing_extensions import Annotated
from pydantic import (
    BaseModel,
    ConfigDict,
    ValidationInfo,
    ValidatorFunctionWrapHandler,
    BeforeValidator,
    WrapValidator,
    AfterValidator,
)


def cast_fields_wrap(val: Any, handler: ValidatorFunctionWrapHandler, info: ValidationInfo) -> Any:
    print(f'wrap: {info.field_name}')
    return handler(val)


def cast_fields_before(val: Any, info: ValidationInfo) -> Any:
    print(f'before: {info.field_name}')
    return val


def cast_fields_after(val: Any, info: ValidationInfo) -> Any:
    print(f'after: {info.field_name}')
    return val


MyInt = Annotated[
    int, BeforeValidator(cast_fields_before), WrapValidator(cast_fields_wrap), AfterValidator(cast_fields_after)
]


class MyModel(BaseModel):
    model_config = ConfigDict(extra='allow')

    expected_field: MyInt

    __pydantic_extra__: dict[str, MyInt]


# validation called for extra fields and your field
instance = MyModel(expected_field='1', extra_field='2')
print(repr(instance))
# MyModel(expected_field=1, extra_field=2)
```

Going to close this as not planned for now. Let me know if you have any other questions!

metadata.title: `field_validator('*')` not called for extra fields

# Default time as string gives a serialization warning

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

Hi, if I add default time as string to a model, it gives serialization warning:

```python

from datetime import datetime
from pydantic import AwareDatetime
from pydantic import BaseModel

class SomethingWithDate(BaseModel):
    some_date: AwareDatetime = Field("2023-07-30T00:00:00Z", serialization_alias="someDate")
    # some_date: datetime = Field("2023-07-30T00:00:00Z", serialization_alias="someDate")

something_with_date = SomethingWithDate()
print(something_with_date.model_dump_json())
```

AwareDatetime and datetime as well gives:
```
.venv\Lib\site-packages\pydantic\main.py:415: UserWarning: Pydantic serializer warnings:
  Expected `datetime` but got `str` - serialized value may not be as expected
  return self.__pydantic_serializer__.to_json(
{"some_date":"2023-07-30T00:00:00Z"}
```


if I add the value at instantiation, it works well:


```python

class SomethingWithDate(BaseModel):
    # some_date: AwareDatetime = Field(serialization_alias="someDate")
    some_date: datetime = Field(serialization_alias="someDate")

something_with_date = SomethingWithDate(some_date="2023-07-30T00:00:00Z")
print(something_with_date.model_dump_json())
```

BR,
George

### Example Code

_No response_

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.8.2
        pydantic-core version: 2.20.1
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
                 install path: C:\<snip>\.venv\Lib\site-packages\pydantic
               python version: 3.11.8 (tags/v3.11.8:db85d51, Feb  6 2024, 22:03:32) [MSC v.1937 64 bit (AMD64)]
                     platform: Windows-10-10.0.22631-SP0
             related packages: mypy-1.8.0 typing_extensions-4.12.2
                       commit: unknown
```

## Maintainer reply

You'll have to use `validate_default` if you'd like to skirt the serialization error:

```py
from datetime import datetime
from pydantic import AwareDatetime
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field

class SomethingWithDate(BaseModel):
    some_date: AwareDatetime = Field("2023-07-30T00:00:00Z", serialization_alias="someDate", validate_default=True)
    # some_date: datetime = Field("2023-07-30T00:00:00Z", serialization_alias="someDate")

something_with_date = SomethingWithDate()
print(something_with_date.model_dump_json())
```

from uuid import UUID, uuid4

from pydantic import (
    BaseModel,
    EmailStr,
    Field,
    field_validator,
    model_validator,
)

class Department(Enum):
    HR = "HR"
    SALES = "SALES"
    IT = "IT"
    ENGINEERING = "ENGINEERING"


class Employee(BaseModel):
    employee_id: UUID = Field(default_factory=uuid4, frozen=True)
    name: str = Field(min_length=1, frozen=True)
    email: EmailStr = Field(pattern=r".+@example\.com$")
    date_of_birth: date = Field(alias="birth_date", repr=False, frozen=True)
    salary: float = Field(alias="compensation", gt=0, repr=False)
    department: Department
    elected_benefits: bool

    @field_validator("date_of_birth")
    @classmethod
    def check_valid_age(cls, date_of_birth: date) -> date:
        today = date.today()
        eighteen_years_ago = date(today.year - 18, today.month, today.day)

        if date_of_birth > eighteen_years_ago:
            raise ValueError("Employees must be at least 18 years old.")

        return date_of_birth

    @model_validator(mode="after")
    def check_it_benefits(self) -> Self:
        department = self.department
        elected_benefits = self.elected_benefits

        if department == Department.IT and elected_benefits:
            raise ValueError(
                "IT employees are contractors and don't qualify for benefits."
            )
        return self

-------------------------------------------
replacing:

Employee(
    name="Chris DeTuma",
    email="cdetuma@example.com",
    date_of_birth="1998-04-02",
    salary=123_000.00,
    department="IT",
    elected_benefits=True,
    )

with this, to force 'strict=False':

Employee.model_validate({"name":"Chris DeTuma", "email":"cdetuma@example.com","date_of_birth":"1998-04-02","salary":123_000.00,"department":"IT","elected_benefits":False}, strict=False)

...makes no difference
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.8.2
pydantic-core version: 2.20.1
pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
install path: /home/ra/.cache/pypoetry/virtualenvs/rp-tut-22AcObYS-py3.11/lib/python3.11/site-packages/pydantic
python version: 3.11.2 (main, May  2 2024, 11:59:08) [GCC 12.2.0]
platform: Linux-6.1.0-23-amd64-x86_64-with-glibc2.36
related packages: typing_extensions-4.12.2 pydantic-settings-2.3.4
commit: unknown
```

## Maintainer reply

Hi @alphaveneno,

You need to use the `populate_by_name` config setting to use the field name or alias during validation:

```py
from datetime import date

from datetime import date
from enum import Enum
from typing_extensions import Self
from uuid import UUID, uuid4

from pydantic import (
    BaseModel,
    ConfigDict,
    EmailStr,
    Field,
    field_validator,
    model_validator,
)

class Department(Enum):
    HR = "HR"
    SALES = "SALES"
    IT = "IT"
    ENGINEERING = "ENGINEERING"


class Employee(BaseModel):
    employee_id: UUID = Field(default_factory=uuid4, frozen=True)
    name: str = Field(min_length=1, frozen=True)
    email: EmailStr = Field(pattern=r".+@example\.com$")
    date_of_birth: date = Field(alias="birth_date", repr=False, frozen=True)
    salary: float = Field(alias="compensation", gt=0, repr=False)
    department: Department
    elected_benefits: bool

    model_config = ConfigDict(populate_by_name=True)

    @field_validator("date_of_birth")
    @classmethod
    def check_valid_age(cls, date_of_birth: date) -> date:
        today = date.today()
        eighteen_years_ago = date(today.year - 18, today.month, today.day)

        if date_of_birth > eighteen_years_ago:
            raise ValueError("Employees must be at least 18 years old.")

        return date_of_birth

    @model_validator(mode="after")
    def check_it_benefits(self) -> Self:
        department = self.department
        elected_benefits = self.elected_benefits

        if department == Department.IT and elected_benefits:
            raise ValueError(
                "IT employees are contractors and don't qualify for benefits."
            )
        return self

Employee(
    name="Chris DeTuma",
    email="cdetuma@example.com",
    date_of_birth="1998-04-02",
    salary=123_000.00,
    department="IT",
    elected_benefits=True,
)
    
print('finished')
```

https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/api/config/#pydantic.config.ConfigDict.populate_by_name

content:
# unittest.mock.autospec and hastattr() ### Discussed in https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/discussions/2853
Originally posted by **atskae** May 28, 2021 I was mocking out some Pydantic `BaseModel` classes in a `pytest` environment since some of the model's fields were objects from an external package. I learned that if I `autospec` a `BaseModel` *class* (not object), then `hasattr()` is always `False`. This is not the case for normal Python classes: ``` >>> from pydantic import BaseModel >>> class Cat: ... name = None ... >>> class PydanticCat(BaseModel): ... name: str ... >>> cat = create_autospec(Cat) >>> hasattr(cat, 'name') True >>> pydantic_cat = create_autospec(PydanticCat) >>> hasattr(pydantic_cat, 'name') False ``` If I pass in an object `pydantic_cat = create_autospec(PydanticCat(name='meowth'))`, then `hasattr()` works as expected. I was just curious if this design was intentional and whether it is bad practice to `autospec` a Pydantic `BaseModel` class in this way, or if it even makes sense. Thanks!
## Maintainer reply Hi! This is the expected behavior - the `cat` attribute is attached to the instance, not the class. If you'd like this to be reflected as an attribute of the class, you can use the `ClassVar` annotation. Additionally, you can see that the `name` attribute is present on the instance and instance mock: ```py from unittest.mock import create_autospec from pydantic import BaseModel from typing import ClassVar class Cat(BaseModel): name: str print(hasattr(Cat, 'name')) # False CatMock = create_autospec(Cat) print(hasattr(CatMock, 'name')) # False class Cat2(BaseModel): name: ClassVar[str] = 'Cat2' print(hasattr(Cat2, 'name')) # True CatMock2 = create_autospec(Cat2) print(hasattr(CatMock2, 'name')) # True cat_instance = Cat(name='Belinda') print(hasattr(cat_instance, 'name')) # True cat_instance_mock = create_autospec(cat_instance) print(hasattr(cat_instance_mock, 'name')) # True ``` Thanks for your question!

metadata.title: unittest.mock.autospec and hastattr()

content:
# `default` should be keyword only in `Field` otherwise type checkers don't recognize it

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

when specifying the default value to `Field` as a positional argument, neither mypy or pyright recognizes it and incorrectly treats the field as mandatory.

i believe this is because of how `typing.dataclass_transform` works. since `dataclasses.field` takes `default` as a keyword only argument, `pydantic.fields.Field` shouldn't allow it to be specified positionally.

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import Field
from pydantic.dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class Foo:
    a: int | None = Field(None)
    b: int | None = Field(default=None)


foo = Foo() # mypy & pyright error: Argument missing for parameter "a"
```


### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.8.2
        pydantic-core version: 2.20.1
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
                 install path: C:\Users\user\project\.venv\Lib\site-packages\pydantic
               python version: 3.12.0 (tags/v3.12.0:0fb18b0, Oct  2 2023, 13:03:39) [MSC v.1935 64 bit (AMD64)]
                     platform: Windows-11-10.0.22621-SP0
             related packages: typing_extensions-4.12.2
                       commit: unknown
```

## Maintainer reply

This was discussed here: https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/discussions/7379#discussioncomment-6951557.

Unfortunately making `default` a kw-only argument is a breaking change so I don't think this can be changed easily.

As the documentation says:

So my thought is that I can work with dataclass decorator together with BaseModel heritage but Pydantic keeps throwing me this error like `models_fields` was a field that is not meant to be a field. I don't event know if that makes sense.

## Maintainer reply

Hi @senese,

Using v2.8.0, you get a more helpful error:

```py
from pydantic.dataclasses import dataclass
from pydantic import BaseModel


@dataclass(slots=True)
class TestClass(BaseModel):
    test: str

"""
pydantic.errors.PydanticUserError: Cannot create a Pydantic dataclass from TestClass as it is already a Pydantic model
"""
```

Marking this as resolved - mixing pydantic dataclasses and models in this way isn't supported.

content:
# 2.8.0 unexpectedly change `create_model()`arg `__model_name` to `model_name`

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

Hi!

Not sure if it is a bug or an intended change, but I could not find a mention of it in the release notes, so I'm reporting it anyway.

So essentially, we have some dynamic model generation running, looking like this:
```
def inject_default_from_instance(instance_w_defaults: BaseModel) -> Type[BaseModel]:
    model_w_defaults = create_model(
        __model_name=instance_w_defaults.__repr_name__() + "WithDefaults",
        __base__=type(instance_w_defaults),
        **{
            k: (
                instance_w_defaults.model_fields[k].annotation,
                FieldInfo.merge_field_infos(
                    instance_w_defaults.model_fields[k],
                    default=getattr(instance_w_defaults, k)
                )
            )
            for k in instance_w_defaults.model_fields.keys()
        }
    )
    return model_w_defaults
```

It's a bit hacky and is used to overcome limitations in a different framework, so it may not be the best example of pydantic usage.

Also (our bad, yeah) we have pydantic version set up as "pydantic>=2,<3", so today one of the pipelines picked up the new 2.8.0 version and failed during the test with
TypeError: create_model() missing 1 required positional argument: 'model_name'
from the above-mentioned snippet.

After some digging, I found a [change](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/9479/files#diff-8708af8d26c26644976866f974e0b810b0940ede43ff487e457057d5a1f07999L1446) causing an error in the snippet. Looking at the description, I'm not perfectly sure if the change of the argument name was intended or not.
Probably, if the change is intended, it should be mentioned in the release notes, since it is technically a compatibility change of the public API.

Right now I'll fix the pydantic version more strictly to 2.7, but still would like to know if the change is intended, so I should change the create_model() call accordingly.

### Example Code

_No response_

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.8.0
pydantic-core version: 2.20.0
pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
install path: /path/to/project/.venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/pydantic
python version: 3.11.5 (main, Aug 24 2023, 15:09:45) [Clang 14.0.3 (clang-1403.0.22.14.1)]
platform: macOS-14.5-arm64-arm-64bit
related packages: typing_extensions-4.10.0
commit: unknown
```

## Maintainer reply

@mudravrik,

> After some reading on topic I got to this [part](https://peps.python.org/pep-0570/#using-as-a-per-argument-marker) of PEP-570 explaining, that __model_name is a naming convetion meaning, that it is positional-only argument and should not be used with key at the first place.

Indeed! Going to mark this as resolved, accordingly!

metadata.title: 2.8.0 unexpectedly change `create_model()`arg `__model_name` to `model_name`

# allow to have missing values on init

### Initial Checks

- [X] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [X] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

current code cause an error
```python
from pydantic import BaseModel


class Scheme(BaseModel):
    x: str
    y: int = None


if __name__ == "__main__":
    config = Scheme()
```
```bash
1 validation error for Scheme
x
  Field required [type=missing, input_value={}, input_type=dict]
    For further information visit https://errors.pydantic.dev/2.7/v/missing
```
I want to have a way to allow for missing values on init and validate them later (on freeze for example)

### Affected Components

- [ ] [Compatibility between releases](https://docs.pydantic.dev/changelog/)
- [X] [Data validation/parsing](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/models/#basic-model-usage)
- [ ] [Data serialization](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/serialization/) - `.model_dump()` and `.model_dump_json()`
- [ ] [JSON Schema](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/json_schema/)
- [X] [Dataclasses](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/dataclasses/)

- [X] [Field Types](https://docs.pydantic.dev/api/types/) - adding or changing a particular data type
- [X] [Function validation decorator](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/validation_decorator/)
- [ ] [Generic Models](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/models/#generic-models)

## Maintainer reply

Does this work for your use case?

```py
from pydantic import BaseModel, SkipValidation


class Scheme(BaseModel):
    x: str | None = None
    y: int | None = None


print(repr(Scheme()))
#> Scheme(x=None, y=None)
```

Then you can perform other validations on `x` down the line whenever you please!

class Environment(BaseSettings):
    table_name: str = Field(..., validation_alias="TABLE_NAME")

env = Environment(table_name="table")

print(env)
Expected: Enviroment{"table_name": "table"}

# However, if there is a env file, it will print the value in env file.

             related packages: mypy-1.10.1 pydantic-settings-2.3.4 typing_extensions-4.12.2

## Maintainer reply

Hi @cute149q,

Thanks for your question - this is the expected behavior. Going to close this, accordingly. `pydantic-settings` really just focuses on extracting data from environments / other settings sources. If you'd like pass data directly to a model, you should just use `BaseModel` from `pydantic`.

@hramezani is our local `pydantic-settings` expert - if you have any follow up questions, he is super helpful!

content:
# InstanceOf does not dump fields in child classes when serializing

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

InstanceOf is a great addition to pydantic v2. When dumping data though it only picks up fields which were specified in the parent model. Really annoying this, I want to use instance of instead of unions.

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel, InstanceOf

class SubModel(BaseModel):
    field1: int

class ChildSubModel(SubModel):
    field2: int

class TestModel(BaseModel):
    subModel: InstanceOf[SubModel]


testmodel = TestModel(subModel=ChildSubModel(field1=5, field2=6))

print(testmodel.model_dump(mode="json"))
```
outputs:
{'subModel': {'field1': 5}}



### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.7.4
        pydantic-core version: 2.18.4
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
                 install path: home/.venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.11.9 (main, Apr  6 2024, 17:59:24) [GCC 11.4.0]
                     platform: Linux-6.5.0-1023-oem-x86_64-with-glibc2.35
             related packages: pydantic-extra-types-2.6.0 typing_extensions-4.12.2 pydantic-settings-2.2.1
                       commit: unknown
```

## Maintainer reply

Hi @orfisko,

You can use the `SerializeAsAny` annotation to get he behavior you're looking for:

```py
from pydantic import BaseModel, InstanceOf, SerializeAsAny

class SubModel(BaseModel):
    field1: int

class ChildSubModel(SubModel):
    field2: int

class TestModel(BaseModel):
    subModel: SerializeAsAny[InstanceOf[SubModel]]


testmodel = TestModel(subModel=ChildSubModel(field1=5, field2=6))

print(testmodel.model_dump(mode="json"))
#> {'subModel': {'field1': 5, 'field2': 6}}
```
See the [docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/concepts/serialization/#serializing-with-duck-typing) for more details.

metadata.title: InstanceOf does not dump fields in child classes when serializing

content:
# Request: built-in `TypeAlias` for a Pydantic serializable object

### Initial Checks

- [X] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [X] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

I have some Python 3.12 code like this for a `Field` that is serializable by native Pydantic v2:

```python
from typing import Annotated, TypeAlias

from pydantic import BaseModel, WrapSerializer

Serializable: TypeAlias = None | dict | list | int | float | str | bool | BaseModel


class SomethingSerializable(BaseModel):
    @staticmethod
    def _custom_serializer(value: Serializable, handler, info):  # noqa: ARG004
        if isinstance(value, BaseModel):
            return value.model_dump()
        return handler(value)

    serializable: Annotated[Serializable, WrapSerializer(_custom_serializer)]
```

It would be pretty useful if `Serializable` could be imported from Pydantic. Perhaps it can be a `Field` subtype

### Affected Components

- [ ] [Compatibility between releases](https://docs.pydantic.dev/changelog/)
- [ ] [Data validation/parsing](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/models/#basic-model-usage)
- [X] [Data serialization](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/serialization/) - `.model_dump()` and `.model_dump_json()`
- [ ] [JSON Schema](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/json_schema/)
- [ ] [Dataclasses](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/dataclasses/)
- [ ] [Model Config](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/config/)
- [X] [Field Types](https://docs.pydantic.dev/api/types/) - adding or changing a particular data type
- [ ] [Function validation decorator](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/validation_decorator/)
- [ ] [Generic Models](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/models/#generic-models)
- [ ] [Other Model behaviour](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/models/) - `model_construct()`, pickling, private attributes, ORM mode
- [ ] [Plugins](https://docs.pydantic.dev/) and integration with other tools - mypy, FastAPI, python-devtools, Hypothesis, VS Code, PyCharm, etc.

## Maintainer reply

Hi @jamesbraza,

We have support for a `JsonValue` type in `pydantic`:

```py
JsonValue: TypeAlias = Union[
        List['JsonValue'],
        Dict[str, 'JsonValue'],
        str,
        bool,
        int,
        float,
        None,
    ]
```

So I think for your case, you could do something like:

```py
from typing import Annotated, TypeAlias

from pydantic import BaseModel, JsonValue, WrapSerializer

Serializable: TypeAlias = JsonValue | BaseModel


class SomethingSerializable(BaseModel):
    @staticmethod
    def _custom_serializer(value: Serializable, handler, info):  # noqa: ARG004
        if isinstance(value, BaseModel):
            return value.model_dump()
        return handler(value)

    serializable: Annotated[Serializable, WrapSerializer(_custom_serializer)]
```

Marking this as resolved for now - the `JsonValue` type covers the majority of these cases, and I think it can be the responsibility of the user to cover additional cases like `BaseModel`, or pydantic dataclasses, etc.

Thanks for your question!

metadata.title: Request: built-in `TypeAlias` for a Pydantic serializable object

# __pydantic_extra__ value validators passed entire dict

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

I have this model:

```python
from __future__ import annotations
from openapi.types import BaseModel
from openapi.utils import serialize_int, validate_int
import pydantic
from pydantic import ConfigDict
from pydantic.functional_serializers import PlainSerializer
from pydantic.functional_validators import BeforeValidator
from typing import Dict
from typing_extensions import Annotated


class ObjWithComplexNumbersAdditionalProperties(BaseModel):
    model_config = ConfigDict(populate_by_name=True, arbitrary_types_allowed=True, extra="allow")
    __pydantic_extra__:  Dict[str, Annotated[int, BeforeValidator(validate_int), PlainSerializer(serialize_int(True))]] = {}
    
    normal_field: Annotated[str, pydantic.Field(alias="normalField")]
```

which I instantiate like:

```python
ObjWithComplexNumbersAdditionalProperties(
    normal_field="normal",
    __pydantic_extra__={
        "key1": 1,
    },
)
```

This returns a `ValidationError` as so:

```
E       pydantic_core._pydantic_core.ValidationError: 1 validation error for ObjWithComplexNumbersAdditionalProperties
E       __pydantic_extra__
E         Value error, Expected string [type=value_error, input_value={'key1': 1}, input_type=dict]
E           For further information visit https://errors.pydantic.dev/2.7/v/value_error
```

This behaviour is at odds with validation of a dict in a normal field which works with the same annotation setup

### Example Code

_No response_

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.7.3
        pydantic-core version: 2.18.4
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
                 install path: /home/trist/.cache/pypoetry/virtualenvs/openapi-MeiyxmEW-py3.8/lib/python3.8/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.8.19 (default, Mar 19 2024, 16:05:24)  [GCC 11.4.0]
                     platform: Linux-5.15.146.1-microsoft-standard-WSL2-x86_64-with-glibc2.34
             related packages: typing_extensions-4.12.1
                       commit: unknown
```

## Maintainer reply

@andresliszt is correct - I don't think we really support passing in data via `__pydantic_extra__`, but rather just extra arguments to a constructor call (or key/value pairs in a dict).

If you utilize type hinting with `__pydantic_extra__` and pass in additional values in ^^ this way, things should work as expected. Here are some docs on that: https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/concepts/models/#extra-fields

Marking as resolved. Thanks @andresliszt for the in-depth explanation, and @TristanSpeakEasy for your questions!

metadata.title: __pydantic_extra__ value validators passed entire dict

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import ConfigDict, Field, field_serializer
from typing import Optional
from framework import BaseEntity

class ShopCart(BaseEntity):
    """购物车实体"""

    model_config = ConfigDict(from_attributes=True)

    appid: Optional[str] = Field(default=None, description='应用ID')
    user_id: Optional[int] = Field(default=None, description='用户ID')

    ids: Optional[typing.Union[str, typing.List[int]]] = Field(default=None, description='ID列表')

    def model_post_init(self, __context: typing.Any) -> None:

## Maintainer reply

Hi!

I think this is related to https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/7163. I'm guessing your `BaseEntity` might be influencing some of the observed behavior.

If you could attach a MRE (minimal reproducible example) with just pure pydantic, that'd be helpful. Closing as resolved for now. We can reopen with a MRE if it makes sense!

content:
# Nested models with model_validators sets a field to None

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

Hello Team,

In the below example, mpr.runControl is `None`. Clearly though MPR.runControl is a `not None` field. And is being explicitly provided to the constructor.

Potentially related to this issue: https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/8452

### Example Code

```Python
from datetime import date

from enum import Enum

from typing import List, Optional

 

from pydantic import BaseModel, model_validator

 

class SomeEnum(Enum):

    A = 'A'

    B = 'B'

 

class RegionAndDate(BaseModel):

    region: SomeEnum

    cob: date

 

class ControlStepsMeasures(BaseModel):

    calculate: Optional[List[str]] = (

        None  

    )

    writeToFs: Optional[List[str]] = (

        None  

    )

    writeToDB: Optional[List[str]] = (

        None  

    )

 

class RC(BaseModel):

    base: RegionAndDate

    secondary: RegionAndDate

    runSteps: ControlStepsMeasures = ControlStepsMeasures()

    calculatedColumns: ControlStepsMeasures = ControlStepsMeasures()

 

    @model_validator(mode="after")

    def snap_and_cutoffs(self):

        assert self.runSteps.calculate == self.calculatedColumns.calculate

 

class MPR(BaseModel):

    runControl: RC

    runSteps: Optional[str] = None

 

rc = RC(

        base=RegionAndDate(region="A", cob="2024-06-04"),

        secondary=RegionAndDate(region="B", cob="2024-06-03"),

    )

 

mpr = MPR(

        runControl=rc

    )

 

mpr.runControl
```


### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic 2.7.1
pydantic-core 2.18.2
windows 10
```

## Maintainer reply

@AnatolyBuga,

You need to return `self` from the model validator, see:

```py
from datetime import date

from enum import Enum

from typing import List, Optional


from pydantic import BaseModel, model_validator


class SomeEnum(Enum):
    A = 'A'
    B = 'B'


class RegionAndDate(BaseModel):
    region: SomeEnum
    cob: date


class ControlStepsMeasures(BaseModel):
    calculate: Optional[List[str]] = None
    writeToFs: Optional[List[str]] = None
    writeToDB: Optional[List[str]] = None


class RC(BaseModel):
    base: RegionAndDate
    secondary: RegionAndDate
    runSteps: ControlStepsMeasures = ControlStepsMeasures()
    calculatedColumns: ControlStepsMeasures = ControlStepsMeasures()

    @model_validator(mode='after')
    def snap_and_cutoffs(self):
        assert self.runSteps.calculate == self.calculatedColumns.calculate
        return self


class MPR(BaseModel):
    runControl: RC
    runSteps: Optional[str] = None


rc = RC(
    base=RegionAndDate(region='A', cob='2024-06-04'),
    secondary=RegionAndDate(region='B', cob='2024-06-03'),
)


mpr = MPR(runControl=rc)
print(repr(mpr.runControl))
"""
RC(base=RegionAndDate(region=<SomeEnum.A: 'A'>, cob=datetime.date(2024, 6, 4)), secondary=RegionAndDate(region=<SomeEnum.B: 'B'>, cob=datetime.date(2024, 6, 3)), runSteps=ControlStepsMeasures(calculate=None, writeToFs=None, writeToDB=None), calculatedColumns=ControlStepsMeasures(calculate=None, writeToFs=None, writeToDB=None))
"""
```

content:
# `SecretStr` fields throw type hint errors at user when passing a string

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

I'm not sure if this is a bug per-se, but in both VSCode and Pycharm, I get type hint errors when passing a `str` to a `SecretStr` field:

![image](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/assets/136370015/7256073a-780e-4dd5-af95-ee9e2252c43f)

My understanding is that this is the intended pattern of usage (the documentation itself has an example like this), and so I would expect as a user to be able to just pass a string and it would interpret it as a valid argument.

Use case is an end user library where I don't want users to have to know about the Pydantic model under the hood (there are helper methods to dump the model fully plaintext). 

This type warning implies they need to import `SecretStr` from Pydantic and use it (unless of course they look it up and know how it works), which feels like a poor user experience.

There is a "workaround" using `Union[SecretStr, str]` but it feels rather flimsy and potentially dangerous (especially since `Union[str, SecretStr]` doesn't generate a secret).

I also attempted doing some things with `Secret[Annotated[str]]` but that resulted in other issues.

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel, SecretStr


class Model(BaseModel):
    username: str
    password: SecretStr

m = Model(username='blah', password='blah')
```


### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.7.3
        pydantic-core version: 2.18.4
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
               python version: 3.8.17
                     platform: Linux-6.4.6-200.fc38.x86_64-x86_64-with-glibc2.34
             related packages: typing_extensions-4.8.0 pydantic-settings-2.3.0
```

## Maintainer reply

@tim-quix,

Thanks for reporting this - if you use our `mypy` plugin, you shouldn't get this type error. That being said, there might be something internally that we can do with our typing to prevent this. I'll look into this!

# Serialization Warning when using Literal and model_dump

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

I get the following error when serializing some models:

```
 UserWarning: Pydantic serializer warnings:
  Expected `str` but got `_LiteralGenericAlias` - serialized value may not be as expected
  return self.__pydantic_serializer__.to_python(
{'any_of_schemas': typing.Literal['ServiceType', 'object']}
```

Code example

```python
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from typing_extensions import Literal

class MyModel(BaseModel):
    any_of_schemas: List[str] = Field(default=Literal["ServiceType", "object"])

my_model = MyModel()
my_model.model_dump()
```

### Example Code

_No response_

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
>>> print(pydantic.version.version_info())

             pydantic version: 2.7.2
        pydantic-core version: 2.18.3
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
                 install path: /Users/joel.barenco/Library/Caches/pypoetry/virtualenvs/product-page-wu9beR97-py3.11/lib/python3.11/site-packages/pydantic

                       commit: unknown

```
```

## Maintainer reply

@jobarjo,

This error is expected - `Literal["ServiceType", "object"]` is not a valid `List[str]`. If you use `validate_default=True` for said field, you'll get a validation error.

Marking as resolved - if you use ["ServiceType", "object"] as the default, you won't get an error 👍

content:
# @field_validator not working when wrapped with @classmethod

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

I was trying around with pydantic, when I came across this issue. None of my fields got validated. My error was, that I had put the classmethod decorator before the field_validator. After some thought on why Pydantic behaves like that, the reason for this issue is pretty clear to me, but it is a pitty for me either way. Is it possible to add support for this way of doing it?

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel, field_validator


class Model(BaseModel):
    a: str

    @classmethod  # This causes that pydantic doesn't discover the field_validator
    @field_validator("a")
    def validate_a(cls, v: str) -> str:
        if v == "a":
            raise ValueError("a is not allowed")
        return v
    

if __name__ == "__main__":
    model = Model(a="b")  # This should work
    model2 = Model(a="a")  # This should raise a ValueError, but it doesn't
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.7.2
        pydantic-core version: 2.18.3
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
                 install path: C:\Users\jakob\PycharmProjects\exp-coord-server\.venv\Lib\site-packages\pydantic
               python version: 3.12.1 (tags/v3.12.1:2305ca5, Dec  7 2023, 22:03:25) [MSC v.1937 64 bit (AMD64)]
                     platform: Windows-11-10.0.22631-SP0
             related packages: pydantic-settings-2.2.1 typing_extensions-4.12.0
                       commit: unknown
```

## Maintainer reply

Hi @HomerusJa,

Thanks for your question. See [this section](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/concepts/validators/#field-validators) of the docs for clarification.

> @field_validators are "class methods", so the first argument value they receive is the UserModel class, not an instance of UserModel. We recommend you use the @classmethod decorator on them below the @field_validator decorator to get proper type checking.

This isn't likely to be changed in the future, so we'd recommend using the `@field_validator` decorator first and the `@classmethod` decorator second.

content:
# Allow for model discrimination without the creation of another BaseModel

### Initial Checks

- [X] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [X] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

Hello,

I want to select the right model based on user input. The user control the value of only one field, and the rest of the model is assumed to be supplied correctly. I read about [discriminated unions](https://docs.pydantic.dev/2.0/usage/types/unions/#nested-discriminated-unions) but the slight issue is that I'm forced to create a class to be able to type one attribute of this class with the actual type union I want.

What I was imagining was a function available in pydantic that does that. Here is my implementation:

```python
def discriminate_pydantic_unions(input: dict[str, Any], model_unions: type):
    """
    This function is purely a pydantic utilitarian function. Read
    https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/9512 for more context.

    Given a union of pydantic models, will output the one that matches
    with the input. It is based on the discriminated union principle
    https://docs.pydantic.dev/2.0/usage/types/unions/#nested-discriminated-unions
    """

    class Discri(BaseModel):
        results: model_unions

    return Discri(results=input).results


class G(BaseModel):
    one: str

class H(BaseModel):
    one: int
```

It yields:

```python
discriminate_unions({"one": "h"}, G | H)
>>> G(one="h")
discriminate_unions({"one": 3}, G | H)
>>> H(one=3)
```

Here is a more elaborate example:

```python
class LikelihoodA(BaseModel):
    likelihood: Literal["a"]
    regularization: Literal["None"]

class LikelihoodB(BaseModel):
    likelihood: Literal["b"]
    regularization: Literal["None"]

class RegularizationA(BaseModel):
    regularization: Literal["a"]

class RegularizationB(BaseModel):
    regularization: Literal["b"]


linear_regression_models = Annotated[
    LikelihoodA
    | LikelihoodB,
    Field(discriminator="likelihood"),
]
regression_models = Annotated[
    RegularizationA
    | RegularizationB
    | linear_regression_models,
    Field(discriminator="regularization"),
]

discriminate_pydantic_unions({"regularization": "a"}, regression_models)
>>> RegularizationA(regularization='a')
discriminate_pydantic_unions({"regularization": "b", "likelihood": "a"}, regression_models)
>>> RegularizationB(regularization='b')
discriminate_pydantic_unions({"regularization": "None", "likelihood": "a"}, regression_models)
>>> LikelihoodA(likelihood='a')
```

### Affected Components

- [ ] [Compatibility between releases](https://docs.pydantic.dev/changelog/)
- [ ] [Data validation/parsing](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/models/#basic-model-usage)
- [ ] [Data serialization](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/serialization/) - `.model_dump()` and `.model_dump_json()`
- [ ] [JSON Schema](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/json_schema/)
- [ ] [Dataclasses](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/dataclasses/)
- [ ] [Model Config](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/config/)
- [ ] [Field Types](https://docs.pydantic.dev/api/types/) - adding or changing a particular data type
- [ ] [Function validation decorator](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/validation_decorator/)
- [ ] [Generic Models](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/models/#generic-models)
- [ ] [Other Model behaviour](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/models/) - `model_construct()`, pickling, private attributes, ORM mode
- [ ] [Plugins](https://docs.pydantic.dev/) and integration with other tools - mypy, FastAPI, python-devtools, Hypothesis, VS Code, PyCharm, etc.

## Maintainer reply

@MarcBresson,

You can use [`TypeAdapter`](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/api/type_adapter/) for your use case. For example:

```py
from typing import Literal, Annotated

from pydantic import BaseModel, Field, TypeAdapter

class LikelihoodA(BaseModel):
    likelihood: Literal["a"]
    regularization: Literal["None"]

class LikelihoodB(BaseModel):
    likelihood: Literal["b"]
    regularization: Literal["None"]

class RegularizationA(BaseModel):
    regularization: Literal["a"]

class RegularizationB(BaseModel):
    regularization: Literal["b"]


linear_regression_models = Annotated[
    LikelihoodA
    | LikelihoodB,
    Field(discriminator="likelihood"),
]
regression_models = Annotated[
    RegularizationA
    | RegularizationB
    | linear_regression_models,
    Field(discriminator="regularization"),
]

ta = TypeAdapter(regression_models)
print(repr(ta.validate_python({"regularization": "a"})))
#> RegularizationA(regularization='a')

print(repr(ta.validate_python({"regularization": "b", "likelihood": "a"})))
#> RegularizationB(regularization='b')

print(repr(ta.validate_python({"regularization": "None", "likelihood": "a"})))
#> LikelihoodA(likelihood='a', regularization='None')
```

metadata.title: Allow for model discrimination without the creation of another BaseModel

content:
# Fields with BaseModel type that contain an instance of a model that inherits from BaseModel do not get serialized correctly

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

When I define a field with `BaseModel` type and assign an instance of a model that inherits from `BaseModel`, the value gets serialized as `{}`.

Actual result from the reproducer:
```
{'parent': {'data': 'DEFAULT'}, 'parent_base_model': {}}
```

Expected result:
```
{'parent': {'data': 'DEFAULT'}, 'parent_base_model': {'data': 'DEFAULT'}}
```
or a validation error while instantiating `Child` or setting `parent_base_model` field. I tried setting various `model_config` options and they did not have any impact (incl. `validate_assignment=True` and `c.parent_base_model=p`)

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import *


class Parent(BaseModel):
    data: str = Field(default="DEFAULT")


class Child(BaseModel):
    parent: Parent | None = Field(default=None)
    parent_base_model: BaseModel | None = Field(default=None)


p = Parent()
c = Child(parent=p, parent_base_model=p)
print(c.dict())
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.6.4
        pydantic-core version: 2.16.3
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=false
                 install path: /usr/lib/python3.11/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.11.9 (main, Apr 08 2024, 06:18:15) [GCC]
                     platform: Linux-6.8.7-1-default-x86_64-with-glibc2.39
             related packages: email-validator-2.0.0.post2 mypy-1.9.0 typing_extensions-4.11.0
                       commit: unknown
```

## Maintainer reply

Hi @dmach,

You'll want to use the `SerializeAsAny` annotation if you want all of the data to be dumped for that second field. For example:

```py
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field, SerializeAsAny


class Parent(BaseModel):
    data: str = Field(default="DEFAULT")


class Child(BaseModel):
    parent: Parent | None = Field(default=None)
    parent_base_model: SerializeAsAny[BaseModel] | None = Field(default=None)


p = Parent()
c = Child(parent=p, parent_base_model=p)
print(c.model_dump())
#> {'parent': {'data': 'DEFAULT'}, 'parent_base_model': {'data': 'DEFAULT'}}
```

Ping me if you have any questions. You can learn more about that annotation [here](https://docs.pydantic.dev/dev/concepts/serialization/#serializeasany-annotation)!

# Discriminated Unions with callable `Discriminator` fail to create a class dynamically with `Optional` annotation

### Description

In our code we need to convert existing pydantic `BaseModel`s into new classes with optional fields dynamically. We utilize `pydantic.create_model` for that wrapping field annotations with `Optional`. This works for fine for fields being discriminated unions defined as `Field(discriminator="<property>")`, but fails for ones defined as `Discriminator(callable)` with corresponding `Tag` annotation.

In the provided example I built new-style and old-style discriminated unions side-by-side for comparison. The error the new-style discriminated union raises is the following, as if `NoneType` messes up with the inner union:

For further information visit https://errors.pydantic.dev/2.7/u/callable-discriminator-no-tag
```

### Example Code

```Python
from typing import Any, Literal, Optional, Union

from pydantic import BaseModel, Discriminator, Field, Tag, create_model
from typing_extensions import Annotated

# Definitions

def model_x_discriminator(v: Any) -> str:
    if isinstance(v, int):
        return "int"
    if isinstance(v, str):
        return "str"
    raise ValueError()


class ValueInt(BaseModel):
    type: Literal["int"] = "int"
    x: int


class ValueStr(BaseModel):
    type: Literal["str"] = "str"
    x: str


# old-style discriminator by class property

OldDiscriminatedType = Annotated[
    Union[ValueInt, ValueStr],
    Field(discriminator="type"),
]


class OldDiscriminatedModel(BaseModel):
    value: OldDiscriminatedType


class OldDiscriminatedModelOptional(BaseModel):
    value: Optional[OldDiscriminatedType]


old_value_field_info = OldDiscriminatedModel.model_fields["value"]
print(
    create_model(
        "OldDiscriminatedModelOptionalTest",
        __base__=OldDiscriminatedModel,
        **{
            "value": (
                Optional[OldDiscriminatedType],
                old_value_field_info,
            )
        },
    )
)
# outputs a new class


# new-style Discriminator and Tag

NewDiscriminatedType = Annotated[
    Union[
        Annotated[ValueInt, Tag("int")],
        Annotated[ValueStr, Tag("str")],
    ],
    Discriminator(model_x_discriminator),
]


class NewDiscriminatedModel(BaseModel):
    value: NewDiscriminatedType


class NewDiscriminatedModelOptional(BaseModel):
    value: Optional[NewDiscriminatedType]


new_value_field_info = NewDiscriminatedModel.model_fields["value"]
print(
    create_model(
        "NewDiscriminatedModelOptionalTest",
        __base__=NewDiscriminatedModel,
        **{
            "value": (
                Optional[NewDiscriminatedType],
                new_value_field_info,
            )
        },
    )
)
# raises an error
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
$ python3 -c "import pydantic.version; print(pydantic.version.version_info())"

        pydantic-core version: 2.18.2

@chiselko6,

Thanks for the detailed description and reproducible code snippet. Definitely looks like a bug. Can be a bit difficult to track down with our complicated discriminator application logic, but I'll look into a fix for this in 2.8!

# StrEnum isn't coerced into str when used with a Literal

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

When using a type of string Literals, passing a StrEnum value is not coerced to the string value of the enum.
In v1 this worked.
It could be a configuration issue, but if so I'm not sure which one.

### Example Code

```Python
from typing import Literal
import enum

import pydantic
from pydantic import ConfigDict


class Alphabet(enum.StrEnum):
    Z = "Z"
    Y = "Y"
    X = "X"

class TheBestLetters(pydantic.BaseModel):
    model_config = ConfigDict(use_enum_values=True)
    value: Literal["C", "X"]

print(TheBestLetters(value="C"))
print(TheBestLetters(value=Alphabet.X))

outputs

value='C'
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "C:\Users\ZD6189\AppData\Roaming\JetBrains\PyCharm2024.1\scratches\test_pydantic_enums_2.py", line 18, in <module>
    print(TheBestLetters(value=Alphabet.X))
          ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "C:\Users\ZD6189\.venv\geco-311\Lib\site-packages\pydantic\main.py", line 175, in __init__
    self.__pydantic_validator__.validate_python(data, self_instance=self)
pydantic_core._pydantic_core.ValidationError: 1 validation error for TheBestLetters
value
  Input should be 'C' or 'X' [type=literal_error, input_value=<Alphabet.X: 'X'>, input_type=Alphabet]
    For further information visit https://errors.pydantic.dev/2.7/v/literal_error

expected output:

value='C'
value='X'
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.7.0
        pydantic-core version: 2.18.1
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
                 install path: C:\Users\ZD6189\.venv\geco-311\Lib\site-packages\pydantic
               python version: 3.11.6 (tags/v3.11.6:8b6ee5b, Oct  2 2023, 14:57:12) [MSC v.1935 64 bit (AMD64)]
                     platform: Windows-10-10.0.19045-SP0
             related packages: email-validator-1.3.1 typing_extensions-4.10.0
                       commit: unknown
```

## Maintainer reply

@JesseDeLoore,

Thanks for the question. This behavior makes sense to me - the `use_enum_values` logic only applies to fields of `enum` types. If you need to work around this, you could do:

```py
from typing import Literal
from enum import Enum
import pydantic
from pydantic import ConfigDict


class Alphabet(str, Enum):
    Z = "Z"
    Y = "Y"
    X = "X"

class TheBestLetters(pydantic.BaseModel):
    model_config = ConfigDict(use_enum_values=True)
    value: Literal["C", "X"]

print(TheBestLetters(value="C"))
print(TheBestLetters(value=Alphabet.X.value))

"""
value='C'
value='X'
"""
```

Or, use a custom validator for that field. If you make it an `enum` type instead of `Literal`, you could get the behavior you're expecting.

Closing as resolved, as I don't think this is something we're going to change in the near term.

### Initial Checks

# Unexpected behaviour while initializing v2 models

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

Hello there,

I ran into an issue while migrating our codebase to pydantic v2.

Previously in v1, we were using a logic similar to my example code. I just realized that this used to work as long as you added the `underscore_attrs_are_private = True` as a part of the configuration. Since this option is now removed and set to `True` for all v2 models, I thought I could migrate it as I displayed in the example.

Unfortunately, this fails with a `'MyClass' object has no attribute '__pydantic_private__'` error, and the error message itself is also a bit confusing. 

As a potential solution, I thought about moving the logic within the `__init__` call to a `model_validator` with the parameter `mode="before"` but I was not sure if this was the most optimal solution here. Moreover, I want to report this in case it indicates a bug in the code.

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel


class MyClass(BaseModel):

    _hidden_param: int

    def __init__(self, hidden_param: int = None, **kwargs):
        # If hidden param is provided, overwrite the hidden value.
        if hidden_param:
            self._hidden_param = hidden_param

        super(MyClass, self).__init__(**kwargs)

    # ---- Only uncomment while testing it in v1 ----
    # class Config:
    #     underscore_attrs_are_private = True


a = MyClass()  # This works!

a._hidden_param = 4  # This works as well
print(f"The private value {a._hidden_param}")

print(f"Class vars: {MyClass.__class_vars__}")
print(f"Pydantic Private: {MyClass.__pydantic_private__}")
print(f"Private Attributes: {MyClass.__private_attributes__}")

MyClass(hidden_param=4)  # This fails in v2 with: 'MyClass' object has no attribute '__pydantic_private__'.
```


### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.6.4
pydantic-core version: 2.16.3
pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
install path: /Users/bariscandurak/.pyenv/versions/3.10.11/envs/zenml/lib/python3.10/site-packages/pydantic

commit: unknown
```

Thanks for reporting your issue! Could you please submit the original code snippet that you were using in V1 as well?

content:
# model_dump() for list of models produces not accurate type

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

When we are trying to use RootModel which is the list of other models, `model_dump` produces the result as `list[Model]` when in reality it should be `list[dict[str, Any]]`.

The proposal is to add additional Generic along with `RootModelRootType` which will "override" the serialized type, like:
```python
class RootModel(BaseModel, typing.Generic[RootModelRootType, RootModelDumpType], metaclass=_RootModelMetaclass):
    ...
```

I do not think that it will produce huge orverhead on the developer to specify additional "dump" type, just if it is int, then he has to specify
```python
class MyModelList(pydantic.RootModel[list[int, int]]):
```

Just a proposal, actually, as we have overrode the `model_dump` for our custom RootModel to fix the mypy issue.

### Example Code

```Python
import pydantic

class MyModel(pydantic.BaseModel):
  key: str

class MyModelList(pydantic.RootModel[list[MyModel]]):
    pass


reveal_type(MyModelList([{'key': 'value'}]).model_dump())  # produces `list[MyModel]`
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.6.4
        pydantic-core version: 2.16.3
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=false
                 install path: /home/user/venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.11.8 (main, Mar 12 2024, 08:49:29) [GCC 12.2.0]
                     platform: Linux-6.6.16-linuxkit-aarch64-with-glibc2.36
             related packages: mypy-1.9.0 typing_extensions-4.10.0
                       commit: unknown
```

## Maintainer reply

@sshishov,

I don't believe this is a bug. See this example:

```py
import pydantic

class MyModel(pydantic.BaseModel):
  key: str

class MyModelList(pydantic.RootModel[list[MyModel]]):
    pass


dumped_data = MyModelList([{'key': 'value'}]).model_dump()
print(dumped_data)
#> [{'key': 'value'}] (which is a list[dict[str, Any]], as expected)
```

See this section of the code for a return type annotation explanation for the `model_dump()` function of a `RootModel`: https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/blob/b9ec63fe68b40a356c5003996d5e866aeb4aae7e/pydantic/root_model.py#L135-L147

metadata.title: model_dump() for list of models produces not accurate type

content:
# model.model_copy doesn't transform dicts into models

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

Nested models remain as dicts on nested data instead of being converted to their proper objects (see Example Code below)

This makes no sense and is unintuitive.

In the below example, you'd have to write:
```py
merged_model = ModelB.model_validate(model_b_1.model_copy(update=model_b_2.model_dump(include=['model_a']).model_dump())
```
Which even throws an warning:
```py
UserWarning: Pydantic serializer warnings:
  Expected `ModelA` but got `dict` - serialized value may not be as expected
  return self.__pydantic_serializer__.to_python(
```

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel



class ModelA(BaseModel):
    name: str

model_a = ModelA(name='jeff')

class ModelB(BaseModel):
    name: str
    model_a: ModelA | None = None


model_b_1 = ModelB(name='bob')
model_b_2 = ModelB(name='frank', model_a=model_a)

merged_model = model_b_1.model_copy(update=model_b_2.model_dump(include=['model_a'])

assert isinstance(merged_model.model_a, ModelA)
```


### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.6.4
        pydantic-core version: 2.16.3
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
                 install path: C:\Users\zackp\.virtualenvs\platform_automator--mWSu_5p\Lib\site-packages\pydantic        
               python version: 3.11.5 (tags/v3.11.5:cce6ba9, Aug 24 2023, 14:38:34) [MSC v.1936 64 bit (AMD64)]
                     platform: Windows-10-10.0.22621-SP0
             related packages: fastapi-0.110.0 typing_extensions-4.10.0
                       commit: unknown
```

There's just no way this is the only way to update a new model. Why convert it to a dict? Just put it back to it's object! Otherwise it's not a copy! 🤷‍♂️ It's altering the data within that model.

## Maintainer reply

@ZackPlauche,

As the `model_copy` API docs state, `model_copy`'s `update` argument expects trusted data, which is not revalidated: https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/api/base_model/#pydantic.BaseModel.model_copy. Thus, this behavior is as expected.

content:
# Generic model as parameter fails model type validation

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

The generic model when used as a parameter for another model fails validation for model type. 

Error received is
```
pydamtic_core._pydantic_core.Validation Error: 1 validation error for RootModel
name
  Input should be a valid dictionary or instance of GenericModel[SubModel1] [type=model_type, input_value=GenericModel(status='succ...Model1(name1='example')), input_type=GenericModel]
    For further information visit https://errors.pydantic.dev/2.6/v/model_type
```

### Example Code

```Python
from typing import Generic, TypeVar
from pydantic import BaseModel

T=TypeVar("T", bound=BaseModel)

class GenericModel(BaseModel, Generic[T]):
    status: str
    data: T

class SubModel1(BaseModel):
    name1: str

class SubModel2(BaseModel):
    name2: str

class RootModel(BaseModel):
    name: GenericModel[SubModel1]

#This validates and should
test_generic = GenericModel(
    status="success",
    data=SubModel1(name1="example"),
)

#This fails validation but shouldn't
test_model = RootModel(name=test_generic)

print(test_model)

#This should fail validation
test_model = RootModel(
    name=GenericModel(
        status="success",
        data=SubModel2(name1="example"),
    )
)
```


### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.6.4
pydantic-core version: 2.16.3
pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
install path: anaconda\env\pydantic
python version: 3.11.7 | packaged by Anaconda, Inc. | <censored> [MSC v.1916 64 bit (AMD64)]
platform: Windows-10-10.0.19045-SP0
related packages: fastapi-0.110.0 pydantic-settings-2.2.1 typing_extensions-4.10.0
commit: unknown
```

## Maintainer reply

@mx-raza,

Indeed, this is the expected behavior. Here's a bit more explanation:

```py
from typing import Generic, TypeVar
from pydantic import BaseModel

T=TypeVar("T", bound=BaseModel)

class GenericModel(BaseModel, Generic[T]):
    status: str
    data: T

class SubModel1(BaseModel):
    name1: str

class SubModel2(BaseModel):
    name2: str

class RootModel(BaseModel):
    name: GenericModel[SubModel1]

#This validates and should
test_generic = GenericModel(
    status="success",
    data=SubModel1(name1="example"),
)
print(repr(test_generic))
# > GenericModel(status='success', data=SubModel1(name1='example'))

#This fails validation, as test_generic is not an explicitly parametrized instance of GenericModel
test_model = RootModel(name=test_generic)

# This works, though:
test_generic_parametrized = GenericModel[SubModel1](
    status="success",
    data=SubModel1(name1="example"),
)
test_model_parametrized = RootModel(name=test_generic_parametrized)

print(repr(test_model_parametrized))
#> RootModel(name=GenericModel[SubModel1](status='success', data=SubModel1(name1='example')))

# This should fail validation, and does
test_model = RootModel(
    name=GenericModel(
        status="success",
        data=SubModel2(name2="example"),
    )
)
"""
pydantic_core._pydantic_core.ValidationError: 1 validation error for RootModel
name
  Input should be a valid dictionary or instance of GenericModel[SubModel1] [type=model_type, input_value=GenericModel(status='succ...Model2(name2='example')), input_type=GenericModel]
    For further information visit https://errors.pydantic.dev/2.7/v/model_type
"""

# This also fails:
test_model = RootModel(
    name=GenericModel[SubModel2](
        status="success",
        data=SubModel2(name2="example"),
    )
)
"""
pydantic_core._pydantic_core.ValidationError: 1 validation error for RootModel
name
  Input should be a valid dictionary or instance of GenericModel[SubModel1] [type=model_type, input_value=GenericModel[SubModel2](s...Model2(name2='example')), input_type=GenericModel[SubModel2]]
    For further information visit https://errors.pydantic.dev/2.7/v/model_type
"""
```

We've definitely had this reported before. I'll see if I can find some related issues that even more of an explanation!

metadata.title: Generic model as parameter fails model type validation

content:
# defaultdict loses its default factory in serializing/deserialising roundtrip

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

It looks like when serializing/deserialising a `BaseModel` that holds a `defaultdict`, its `default_factory` is lost.

This has been previously reported in https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/4687 for v1 and the special case of a nested defaultdict. In v2 I'm facing the issue even without nesting.

### Example Code

```py
from collections import defaultdict
from pydantic import BaseModel

class MyModel(BaseModel):
    some_dict: defaultdict[str, int] = defaultdict(lambda: 42)

instance_orig = MyModel()
print(instance_orig.some_dict["a"])  # -> 42
print(instance_orig.some_dict["b"])  # -> 42

instance_roundtrip = MyModel.model_validate_json(instance_orig.model_dump_json())
print(instance_roundtrip.some_dict["a"])  # -> 42
print(instance_roundtrip.some_dict["b"])  # -> 42
print(instance_roundtrip.some_dict["c"])  # -> 0
```

The output is:

```
42
42
42
42
0
```

Note that after the serialization/deserialization roundtrip, the specified `default_factory` is lost. This violates the invariant of the class, because it no longer behaves as intended (in this case returning 42 for missing entries).


### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.6.4
        pydantic-core version: 2.16.3
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
                 install path: /home/me/path/to/venv/lib/python3.10/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.10.13 (main, Aug 25 2023, 13:20:03) [GCC 9.4.0]
                     platform: Linux-5.4.0-173-generic-x86_64-with-glibc2.31
             related packages: mypy-1.9.0 typing_extensions-4.10.0
                       commit: unknown
```

## Maintainer reply

@bluenote10,

Huh, certainly seems like a bug. PRs welcome with a fix, and I'll add this to the 2.8 milestone.

# [BUG]: `self.model_dump()` doesn't pick up subclass instances

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

I am trying to use a custom `__repr__` method to make a readable output of the object. I want to type a field of the super class, so all subclass instances passed to the field are validated correctly. Although, the `self.model_dump()` in the `__repr__` method doesnt seem to work unless I have correctly and explicitly typed the same class in the definition of the field.

### Example Code

```Python
## WORKING CODE (explicitly typed field)

from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from pydantic.config import ConfigDict


class QueryParams(BaseModel):
    pass


class subQueryParams(QueryParams):
    test: str = "test"


class YourModel(BaseModel):
    model_config = ConfigDict(arbitrary_types_allowed=True)
    command_params: subQueryParams = Field()

    def __repr__(self) -> str:
        """Human readable representation of the object."""
        items = [
            f"{k}: {v}"[:83] + ("..." if len(f"{k}: {v}") > 83 else "")
            for k, v in self.model_dump().items()
        ]
        return f"{self.__class__.__name__}\n\n" + "\n".join(items)


YourModel(command_params=subQueryParams())

# Returns:
>>> YourModel

    command_params: {'test': 'test'}

## BUG CODE (changed the field type to a super class)

from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from pydantic.config import ConfigDict


class QueryParams(BaseModel):
    pass


class subQueryParams(QueryParams):
    test: str = "test"


class YourModel(BaseModel):
    model_config = ConfigDict(arbitrary_types_allowed=True)
    command_params: QueryParams = Field()

    def __repr__(self) -> str:
        """Human readable representation of the object."""
        items = [
            f"{k}: {v}"[:83] + ("..." if len(f"{k}: {v}") > 83 else "")
            for k, v in self.model_dump().items()
        ]
        return f"{self.__class__.__name__}\n\n" + "\n".join(items)


YourModel(command_params=subQueryParams())

# Returns
>>> YourModel

    command_params: {}
```
