# [BUG]: Sub-Class typing does not work with `type[T]` in Field

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

I want to validate a field in a model that is a subclass of another model. The class assigned to this field can change, but will always be a subclass of this specific model.

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from pydantic.config import ConfigDict


class QueryParams(BaseModel):
    pass


class subQueryParams(BaseModel):
    pass


class YourModel(BaseModel):
    model_config = ConfigDict(arbitrary_types_allowed=True)
    command_params: type[QueryParams] = Field()


YourModel(command_params=subQueryParams())
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.6.4
        pydantic-core version: 2.16.3
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
                 install path: C:\Users\jjfan\github\humblFINANCE-org\humbldata\menv\Lib\site-packages\pydantic
               python version: 3.11.7 | packaged by conda-forge | (main, Dec 23 2023, 14:27:59) [MSC v.1937 64 bit (AMD64)]
                     platform: Windows-10-10.0.22631-SP0
             related packages: fastapi-0.104.1 mypy-1.9.0 typing_extensions-4.10.0
                       commit: unknown
```

## Maintainer reply

@jjfantini,

I think this is what you're looking for:

```py
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from pydantic.config import ConfigDict


class QueryParams(BaseModel):
    pass


class subQueryParams(QueryParams):
    pass


class YourModel(BaseModel):
    model_config = ConfigDict(arbitrary_types_allowed=True)
    command_params: QueryParams = Field()


YourModel(command_params=subQueryParams())
```

First, you'll want to make sure that `subQueryParams` is indeed a subclass of `QueryParams`. Second, you don't need to use the `type` annotation, as that suggests you're passing a class, not an instance.

content:
# In Pydantic v2, v1.BaseModel objects do not validate properly as fields of v2 models

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

As we have migrated to pydantic 2, we have discovered that when other libraries (outside our control) use v1.BaseModel to accomplish pydantic 2 compatibility, that these models do not validate properly as fields of our native pydantic 2 models. 

We have worked around this by adding an annotation around use of v1 models in v2 that implements the correct validation logic, but it would be nice if it worked natively.

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic.v1 import BaseModel as BaseModelV1
from pydantic import BaseModel
class A(BaseModelV1):
    pass
class B(BaseModel):
    a: A
    
B(a=A())

# Workaround:
from pydantic import PlainValidator
from typing import Annotated
class C(BaseModel):
    a: Annotated[A, PlainValidator(A.validate)]
C(a=A())
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.6.3
        pydantic-core version: 2.16.3
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
                 install path: /isilon/home/pt10597/.conda/envs/ccrt39-0.9.0/lib/python3.9/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.9.18 | packaged by conda-forge | (main, Aug 30 2023, 03:49:32)  [GCC 12.3.0]
                     platform: Linux-4.18.0-372.9.1.el8.x86_64-x86_64-with-glibc2.28
             related packages: typing_extensions-4.10.0 fastapi-0.110.0 pydantic-settings-2.2.1 mypy-1.7.0
                       commit: unknown
```

## Maintainer reply

@ptomecek,

Thanks for your question. As reported in [the migration](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/migration/#changes-to-pydanticgenericsgenericmodel) guide:

> Mixing of V1 and V2 models is not supported which means that type parameters of such generic BaseModel (V2) cannot be V1 models.

Also, see this duplicate issue where we updated the docs to reflect this: https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/7637

metadata.title: In Pydantic v2, v1.BaseModel objects do not validate properly as fields of v2 models

content:
# Mypy plugin swallows type error when using incorrect object type

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

The Pydantic mypy plugin seems to swallow a Mypy error.
Consider the following code:
```python
# test.py
from dataclasses import dataclass
from pydantic import BaseModel


@dataclass
class First:
    y: int


@dataclass
class Second:
    y: int


class MyModel(BaseModel):
    x: Second


_ = MyModel(x=First(y=1))
```

And the following config file:
```toml
# mypy.toml
[tool.mypy]
plugins = ["pydantic.mypy"]
```

Running `mypy test.py` gives the correct error:
```txt
test.py:20: error: Argument "x" to "MyModel" has incompatible type "First"; expected "Second"  [arg-type]
Found 1 error in 1 file (checked 1 source file)
```

Running `mypy --config mypy.toml test.py` gives:
```txt
Success: no issues found in 1 source file
```

Am I missing something or is the Pydantic mypy plugin swallowing the error incorrectly?

### Example Code

# Validation not working for Annotated with conlist

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

Using `conlist` within `Annotated` isn't triggering validation -- is this expected?

### Example Code

```Python
from typing import Annotated
from pydantic import BaseModel, conlist

class MyModel(BaseModel):
    li: Annotated[list, conlist(int, min_length=2)]

print(MyModel(li=[]))  # I expected this to raise a validation error
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.6.4
        pydantic-core version: 2.16.3
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
                 install path: /tmp/venv-XGP7/lib/python3.11/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.11.0 (main, Nov 28 2022, 13:51:36) [GCC 11.3.0]
                     platform: Linux-6.5.0-18-generic-x86_64-with-glibc2.35
             related packages: typing_extensions-4.10.0
                       commit: unknown
```

## Maintainer reply

`conlist` itself is an `Annotated[list, ...]`, so you can just use `conlist` without the `Annotated`!:

```py
from typing import Annotated
from pydantic import BaseModel, conlist

class MyModel(BaseModel):
    li: conlist(int, min_length=2)

print(MyModel(li=[]))
"""
li
  List should have at least 2 items after validation, not 0 [type=too_short, input_value=[], input_type=list]
    For further information visit https://errors.pydantic.dev/2.7/v/too_short
"""
```

# GenerateSchema.match_type doesn't support classes inherited from str

### Description

I'm extensively using classes derived from `str` with some additional fields added for convenience. While used in Pydantic models, such classes are treated fine by v1, however v2 throws an error.

    schema = self._post_process_generated_schema(self._generate_schema_inner(obj))
                                                 ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File ".../lib/python3.11/site-packages/pydantic/_internal/_generate_schema.py", line 759, in _generate_schema_inner
    return self._model_schema(obj)
           ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File ".../lib/python3.11/site-packages/pydantic/_internal/_generate_schema.py", line 572, in _model_schema
    {k: self._generate_md_field_schema(k, v, decorators) for k, v in fields.items()},

  File ".../lib/python3.11/site-packages/pydantic/_internal/_generate_schema.py", line 572, in <dictcomp>
    {k: self._generate_md_field_schema(k, v, decorators) for k, v in fields.items()},
        ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File ".../lib/python3.11/site-packages/pydantic/_internal/_generate_schema.py", line 923, in _generate_md_field_schema
    common_field = self._common_field_schema(name, field_info, decorators)
                   ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File ".../lib/python3.11/site-packages/pydantic/_internal/_generate_schema.py", line 1045, in _common_field_schema
    schema = self._apply_annotations(
             ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File ".../lib/python3.11/site-packages/pydantic/_internal/_generate_schema.py", line 1747, in _apply_annotations
    schema = get_inner_schema(source_type)
             ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File ".../lib/python3.11/site-packages/pydantic/_internal/_schema_generation_shared.py", line 82, in __call__
    schema = self._handler(__source_type)
             ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File ".../lib/python3.11/site-packages/pydantic/_internal/_generate_schema.py", line 1728, in inner_handler
    schema = self._generate_schema(obj)
             ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File ".../lib/python3.11/site-packages/pydantic/_internal/_generate_schema.py", line 737, in _generate_schema
    schema = self._post_process_generated_schema(self._generate_schema_inner(obj))
                                                 ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File ".../lib/python3.11/site-packages/pydantic/_internal/_generate_schema.py", line 764, in _generate_schema_inner
    return self.match_type(obj)
           ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File ".../lib/python3.11/site-packages/pydantic/_internal/_generate_schema.py", line 851, in match_type
    return self._unknown_type_schema(obj)
           ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File ".../lib/python3.11/site-packages/pydantic/_internal/_generate_schema.py", line 397, in _unknown_type_schema
    raise PydanticSchemaGenerationError(
pydantic.errors.PydanticSchemaGenerationError: Unable to generate pydantic-core schema for <class '__main__.StrChild'>. Set `arbitrary_types_allowed=True` in the model_config to ignore this error or implement `__get_pydantic_core_schema__` on your type to fully support it.
If you got this error by calling handler(<some type>) within `__get_pydantic_core_schema__` then you likely need to call `handler.generate_schema(<some type>)` since we do not call `__get_pydantic_core_schema__` on `<some type>` otherwise to avoid infinite recursion.
For further information visit https://errors.pydantic.dev/2.6/u/schema-for-unknown-type
```
</details>

I checked the code, it's [this line](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/blob/main/pydantic/_internal/_generate_schema.py#L759) that causes the issue. Something as simple as changing it to `if str in obj.__bases__` solves the problem.

 I was going to open a PR, but went through the doc on contributing first - and here I am, creating an issue and volunteering to fix it (probably would be better for all primitive types, not only `str`) 🙂 

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel

class StrChild(str):
    pass

class Model(BaseModel):
    field: StrChild
```


### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
             pydantic version: 2.6.3
        pydantic-core version: 2.16.3
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=false
               python version: 3.11.6 (main, Oct  2 2023, 20:14:46)
                     platform: macOS-14.3-x86_64-i386-64bit
```

## Maintainer reply

@NothisIm,

Thanks for your well thought out feature request. It's my understanding that we purposefully don't automatically handle subclasses of known types (see this PR for example, which ensures that doesn't happen: https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/5737).

I'll consult with the team regarding the reasoning behind this decision. I think that the workaround with the custom schema definitions is the best way forward, for now! Closing as resolved, but I'll reopen if it turns out we'd consider a change!

Try this:

```py
from decimal import Decimal

```

That should resolve the issue!

# Cannot check isinstance when validating from json (with pure pydantic MRE)

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

I'm attempting to save out an instance of my Pydantic model to json but encountering an error with no info in the traceback.

the error

```
In [1]: from stac_model.examples import eurosat_resnet
   ...: from stac_model.schema import MLModel
   ...: model_metadata = eurosat_resnet()
   ...: metadata_json = model_metadata.model_dump_json(indent=2)
   ...: model_metadata_validated = MLModel.model_validate_json(metadata_json)
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
NotImplementedError                       Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[1], line 5
      3 model_metadata = eurosat_resnet()
      4 metadata_json = model_metadata.model_dump_json(indent=2)
----> 5 model_metadata_validated = MLModel.model_validate_json(metadata_json)

File ~/miniforge3/lib/python3.10/site-packages/pydantic/main.py:538, in BaseModel.model_validate_json(cls, json_data, strict, context)
    536 # `__tracebackhide__` tells pytest and some other tools to omit this function from tracebacks
    537 __tracebackhide__ = True
--> 538 return cls.__pydantic_validator__.validate_json(json_data, strict=strict, context=context)


```

see https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/8890 and https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/8189#issuecomment-1848226486


### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field, FilePath, AnyUrl,  field_validator
from typing import Optional, Dict

class S3Path(AnyUrl):
    allowed_schemes = {"s3"}
    user_required = False
    max_length = 1023
    min_length = 8

    @field_validator("url")
    @classmethod
    def validate_s3_url(cls, v):
        if not v.startswith("s3://"):
            raise ValueError("S3 path must start with s3://")
        if len(v) < cls.min_length:
            raise ValueError("S3 path is too short")
        if len(v) > cls.max_length:
            raise ValueError("S3 path is too long")
        return v

    @field_validator("host")
    @classmethod
    def validate_bucket_name(cls, v):
        if not v:
            raise ValueError("Bucket name cannot be empty")
        if not 3 <= len(v) <= 63:
            raise ValueError("Bucket name must be between 3 and 63 characters")
        if not re.match(r"^[a-z0-9.\-]+$", v):
            raise ValueError(
                "Bucket name can only contain lowercase, numbers, dots, and hyphens"
            )
        if v.startswith("-") or v.endswith("-"):
            raise ValueError("Bucket name cannot start or end with a hyphen")
        if ".." in v:
            raise ValueError("Bucket name cannot have consecutive periods")
        return v

    @field_validator("path")
    @classmethod
    def validate_key(cls, v):
        if "//" in v:
            raise ValueError("Key must not contain double slashes")
        if "\\" in v:
            raise ValueError("Backslashes are not standard in S3 paths")
        if "\t" in v or "\n" in v:
            raise ValueError("Key cannot contain tab or newline characters")
        return v.strip("/")


metadata = ModelArtifact(path="s3://example/s3/uri/model.pt")

json_str = metadata.model_dump_json(indent=2)

ModelArtifact.model_validate_json(json_str)
```

the error

```python
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
NotImplementedError                       Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[9], line 59
     55 metadata = ModelArtifact(path="s3://example/s3/uri/model.pt")
     57 json_str = metadata.model_dump_json(indent=2)
---> 59 ModelArtifact.model_validate_json(json_str)

File ~/miniforge3/lib/python3.10/site-packages/pydantic/main.py:538, in BaseModel.model_validate_json(cls, json_data, strict, context)
    536 # `__tracebackhide__` tells pytest and some other tools to omit this function from tracebacks
    537 __tracebackhide__ = True
--> 538 return cls.__pydantic_validator__.validate_json(json_data, strict=strict, context=context)

NotImplementedError: Cannot check isinstance when validating from json, use a JsonOrPython validator instead.
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.6.2
pydantic-core version: 2.16.3
pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
       install path: /home/rave/.cache/pypoetry/virtualenvs/stac-model-YQXQDXJF-py3.10/lib/python3.10/site-packages/pydantic
python version: 3.10.12 | packaged by conda-forge | (main, Jun 23 2023, 22:40:32) [GCC 12.3.0]
platform: Linux-6.5.0-21-generic-x86_64-with-glibc2.35
related packages: typing_extensions-4.10.0 mypy-1.0.1
commit: unknown
```

## Maintainer reply

@rbavery,

Thanks for following up with an issue!

I think you've got a few problems here. Firstly, you're using `arbitrary_types_allowed` on your `ModelArtifact` schema, which is where the `isinstance` checks are coming from. If you check the type of `path` on your `metadata` instance, it's a `str`, not a `S3Path` as you'd expect.

`AnyUrl` isn't a subclass of `BaseModel`, so the field validators take no effect in your example (there's no known way to create a core schema from the `S3Path` type. I think the best way around this issue would be to just define a custom model for your type. 

Unfortunately, inheriting from `AnyUrl` is a bit messy, given that it's not a subclass of `BaseModel`. There's not a super easy way to modify the core schema that's created, because the core schema is of type `url`, so it doesn't have the fields that you're attempting to modify (like `path`, etc).

# Base model with abstraction by computed_field | the class attribute

### Initial Checks

- [X] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [X] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

i need build the model with abstraction, which require field definition by the class attribute or computed_field.

Pseudo example base model with description which fields are expected

```python
class EntityBase(BaseModel):
    value: str
    
    @abstractmethod
    @computed_field
    def type(self) -> int | None:
        pass
```
By this I mean that the type field must be defined in one of the following ways:

1. Simple class attribute definition:

    ```python
    class EntityTypeOne(EntityBase):
        type: int
    ```
2. The model postprocessing
    ```python
    class EntityTypeTwo(EntityBase):
        context: dict = Field(exclude=True)

        @model_validator(mode="after")
        def _process_model_after(self):
            """Some complex processing with `type` computing by `context`"""
            ...

        @computed_field
        def type(self) -> int:
            return self._type
    ```



$\textcolor{yellow}{\textsf{Or it would be more correct to add a special pydantic decorator to mark such fields.}}$ 





### Profit
1. The model transparency (ABC)
2. Single point of additional processing of expected attributes - the base model


### Affected Components

- [ ] [Compatibility between releases](https://docs.pydantic.dev/changelog/)
- [ ] [Data validation/parsing](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/models/#basic-model-usage)
- [ ] [Data serialization](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/serialization/) - `.model_dump()` and `.model_dump_json()`
- [ ] [JSON Schema](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/json_schema/)
- [ ] [Dataclasses](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/dataclasses/)
- [ ] [Model Config](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/config/)
- [ ] [Field Types](https://docs.pydantic.dev/api/types/) - adding or changing a particular data type
- [ ] [Function validation decorator](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/validation_decorator/)
- [ ] [Generic Models](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/models/#generic-models)
- [X] [Other Model behaviour](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/models/) - `model_construct()`, pickling, private attributes, ORM mode
- [ ] [Plugins](https://docs.pydantic.dev/) and integration with other tools - mypy, FastAPI, python-devtools, Hypothesis, VS Code, PyCharm, etc.

## Maintainer reply

@Docdez,

Thanks for your question. This is currently supported. See https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/concepts/models/#abstract-base-classes.

Here's an example of how you can apply this: https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/blob/752daaf14181f3d8a410a015482648b8d9d896d1/tests/test_computed_fields.py#L532

## Maintainer reply

@hsteinhaus,

Thanks for reporting this. `schema_json()`, or the new (not deprecated) version of said function `model_json_schema()` are meant to be called on subclasses of `BaseModel`, not `BaseModel` itself.

Marking this as resolved.

# Render Enum Member Docstrings in JSON Schema

### Initial Checks

- [X] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [X] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

### BLUF

Proposal to:
1. Migrate JSON Schema output of multiple literal values from `enum` to `oneOf` + `const`
2. Source `description`s for literal values from enum attributes' `__doc__` when available

### What & Why

If we have a field which can take a fixed number of values, we might want to add some detail around the meaning of the
values.

For example, suppose we have a parameter called `execution_mode` which can take on values of `Synchronous` or `Asynchronous`. Perhaps we want to offer these notes to the user:

> **Synchronous:** execute synchronously and block until the task is completed  
> **Asynchronous:** kick off execution in the background and return immediately

In JSON Schema, this is best achieved through the use of `oneOf` and `const`:

```yaml
execution_mode:
  type: string
  oneOf:
    - const: Synchronous
      description: execute synchronously and block until the task is completed
    - const: Asynchronous
      description: kick off execution in the background and return immediately
```

It would be valuable if Pydantic could support this JSON Schema output, and capture the member details in any other places they belong.

### How: Reading From Enum Members' `__doc__`

Of course, we can't provide these details to `typing.Literal`. But when defining our literal values in an enum, `__doc__` seems like a natural place to source them. While there is no standard syntax to set attributes' `__doc__`, there is active interest in documenting attributes. PEP 727 [is under discussion](https://discuss.python.org/t/pep-727-documentation-metadata-in-typing/32566/146) 
and it [revived interest](https://discuss.python.org/t/revisiting-attribute-docstrings/36413) in the [PEP 224](https://peps.python.org/pep-0224/) style supported by [supported by Sphinx](https://www.sphinx-doc.org/en/master/usage/extensions/autodoc.html#directive-autoproperty) and [now Pydantic](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/6563):

```python
class SomeClass:
    class_attribute = 42
    """An integer."""
```

Despite the lack of syntax to do so, we can still set the `__doc__` dunder on enum members if we'd like. For example:

```python
from enum import StrEnum


class DocumentedStrEnum(StrEnum):
    """
    Courtesy of Ethan Furman: https://stackoverflow.com/a/50473952
    """
    def __new__(cls, value, doc=None):
        self = str.__new__(cls)
        self._value_ = value
        if doc is not None:
            self.__doc__ = doc
        return self


class EvalMode(DocumentedStrEnum):
    SYNCHRONOUS = "Synchronous", "execute synchronously and block until the task is completed"
    ASYNCHRONOUS = "Asynchronous", "kick off execution in the background and return immediately"


assert EvalMode.SYNCHRONOUS.__doc__ == "execute synchronously and block until the task is completed"
```

### Pydantic Functionality

I would hope to see the following changes to make this happen:
- Pydantic `model_json_schema()` produces `oneOf` + `const` fields instead of `enum` fields when multiple there are multiple literal value, since JSON Schema's `enum` doesn't support member descriptions
- `Enum.attribute.__doc__` values are passed through to the `description` field in the output schema

#### Implementation

I'm new to the Pydantic codebase so would need some guidance if there are places outside of Model JSON Schema this data 
would need to be used. But the first thing that comes to my mind is:

- Add something like `{c.value: c.__doc__ for c in cases if c.__doc__ is not None}` to a `enum_member_descriptions` metadata field in [get_enum_core_schema()](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/blob/3c96cf91e69df37babd4e841db5f890056ba8f0e/pydantic/_internal/_std_types_schema.py#L70)
- Update [GenerateJsonSchema.literal_schema()](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/blob/main/pydantic/json_schema.py#L722) to:
    - use `oneOf` + `const` instead of `enum`
    - source descriptions from the metadata if available

### Risks & Alternatives

I'm sure I'm missing things so looking forward to the discussion! Here's what comes to mind for me:

#### Sourcing from PEP 224 Style Docstrings

Pydantic [recently added support](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/6563) for parsing PEP 224-style attribute docstrings in Model Fields. It would be worth considering supporting that syntax alternatively or additionally.

#### Previous Migration from `anyOf` + `const` to `enum` JSON Schema

Pydantic [moved from `anyOf` + `const` to `enum`](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/2348) to better support OpenAPI. Some proper validation should be done but since OpenAPI's 3.1.0 version [primarily focused on better alignment with JSON Schema](https://www.openapis.org/blog/2021/02/18/openapi-specification-3-1-released) I expect this wouldn't be an issue.

#### Standardization Around Non-`attr.__doc__`

There is [fresh](https://discuss.python.org/t/pep-727-documentation-metadata-in-typing/32566/146) [appetite](https://discuss.python.org/t/revisiting-attribute-docstrings/36413) to document symbols, but not all the proposals involve setting the `__doc__` attribute on the class attribute itself. If a different standard were adopted in the future, Pydantic might have to support both the new standard and the nonstandard `__doc__` dunder.

### Affected Components

- [ ] [Compatibility between releases](https://docs.pydantic.dev/changelog/)
- [ ] [Data validation/parsing](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/models/#basic-model-usage)
- [ ] [Data serialization](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/serialization/) - `.model_dump()` and `.model_dump_json()`
- [X] [JSON Schema](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/json_schema/)
- [ ] [Dataclasses](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/dataclasses/)
- [ ] [Model Config](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/config/)
- [ ] [Field Types](https://docs.pydantic.dev/api/types/) - adding or changing a particular data type
- [ ] [Function validation decorator](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/validation_decorator/)
- [ ] [Generic Models](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/models/#generic-models)
- [ ] [Other Model behaviour](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/models/) - `model_construct()`, pickling, private attributes, ORM mode
- [ ] [Plugins](https://docs.pydantic.dev/) and integration with other tools - mypy, FastAPI, python-devtools, Hypothesis, VS Code, PyCharm, etc.

## Maintainer reply

@rmehyde,

Thanks for the well thought out feature request!

We recently added support for opt-in attribute docstring support. See the docs on that here: https://docs.pydantic.dev/dev/api/config/#pydantic.config.ConfigDict.use_attribute_docstrings. I think that support for this could be added in a similar way.

Feel free to submit a PR adding support for this feature, I'd be happy to review! We'd want to make sure that this is opt in, as to avoid breaking changes for other users.

metadata.title: Render Enum Member Docstrings in JSON Schema

content:
# Initializing a field with a model instance

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

Hello, please help me solve the following problem. Validation fails if I try to initialize a field with a model instance, rather than a dictionary with raw data.

Traceback
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/alexey/PycharmProjects/temp/temp9.py", line 27, in <module>
    msg = MyMessageEnvelope(
          ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/Users/alexey/PycharmProjects/temp/venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/pydantic/main.py", line 171, in __init__
    self.__pydantic_validator__.validate_python(data, self_instance=self)
pydantic_core._pydantic_core.ValidationError: 1 validation error for MessageEnvelope[BodyVer1[Body]]
body
  Input should be a valid dictionary or instance of BodyVer1[Body] [type=model_type, input_value=BodyVer1(ver_1_0_0=Body(user_uid='user_uid')), input_type=BodyVer1]
    For further information visit https://errors.pydantic.dev/2.6/v/model_type
```

### Example Code

```Python
from typing import Generic, TypeVar

from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict, Field

T = TypeVar("T")
V = TypeVar("V")


class BodyVer1(BaseModel, Generic[T]):
    model_config = ConfigDict(populate_by_name=True)

    ver_1_0_0: T = Field(alias="1.0.0")


class MessageEnvelope(BaseModel, Generic[V]):
    id: str  # noqa: A003
    producer: str
    body: V
    created_at: float = Field(description="Timestamp in seconds")


class Body(BaseModel):
    user_uid: str = 'user_uid'


MyMessageEnvelope = MessageEnvelope[BodyVer1[Body]]
msg = MyMessageEnvelope(
    id='id',
    producer='producer',
    body=BodyVer1(ver_1_0_0=Body(user_uid='user_uid')),
    created_at=0.0
)
print(msg)
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.6.2
        pydantic-core version: 2.16.3
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
                 install path: /Users/alexey/PycharmProjects/temp/venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.11.2 (main, Feb 12 2024, 11:20:56) [Clang 15.0.0 (clang-1500.1.0.2.5)]
                     platform: macOS-14.3.1-arm64-arm-64bit
             related packages: typing_extensions-4.9.0
                       commit: unknown
```

## Maintainer reply

@aleksey925,

Sure thing. There are two approaches you could take here. The first is that you could explicitly parametrize the `BodyVer1` instance that you're creating, like follows:

```py
from typing import Generic, TypeVar

from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict, Field

T = TypeVar("T")
V = TypeVar("V")


class BodyVer1(BaseModel, Generic[T]):
    model_config = ConfigDict(populate_by_name=True)

    ver_1_0_0: T = Field(alias="1.0.0")


class MessageEnvelope(BaseModel, Generic[V]):
    id: str  # noqa: A003
    producer: str
    body: V
    created_at: float = Field(description="Timestamp in seconds")


class Body(BaseModel):
    user_uid: str = 'user_uid'


MyMessageEnvelope = MessageEnvelope[BodyVer1[Body]]
msg = MyMessageEnvelope(
    id='id',
    producer='producer',
    body=BodyVer1[Body](ver_1_0_0=Body(user_uid='user_uid')),
    created_at=0.0
)
print(repr(msg))
#> MessageEnvelope[BodyVer1[Body]](id='id', producer='producer', body=BodyVer1[Body](ver_1_0_0=Body(user_uid='user_uid')), created_at=0.0)
```

The second is that you could create a subclass of `BodyVer1` parametrized with body, then use that as your constructor. For example:

```py
from typing import Generic, TypeVar

from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict, Field

T = TypeVar("T")
V = TypeVar("V")


class BodyVer1(BaseModel, Generic[T]):
    model_config = ConfigDict(populate_by_name=True)

    ver_1_0_0: T = Field(alias="1.0.0")


class MessageEnvelope(BaseModel, Generic[V]):
    id: str  # noqa: A003
    producer: str
    body: V
    created_at: float = Field(description="Timestamp in seconds")


class Body(BaseModel):
    user_uid: str = 'user_uid'

class BodyVer1Parametrized(BodyVer1[Body]):
    pass

MyMessageEnvelope = MessageEnvelope[BodyVer1Parametrized]
msg = MyMessageEnvelope(
    id='id',
    producer='producer',
    body=BodyVer1Parametrized(ver_1_0_0=Body(user_uid='user_uid')),
    created_at=0.0
)
print(repr(msg))
#> MessageEnvelope[BodyVer1Parametrized](id='id', producer='producer', body=BodyVer1Parametrized(ver_1_0_0=Body(user_uid='user_uid')), created_at=0.0)
```

Hope this helps! Let me know if you have any additional follow up questions.

content: # Poor schema naming in root model openapi json with union type ### Initial Checks - [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2 ### Description I'm having a model, which is Root model of a union discriminated type, so i can use the model as root model without having to write another class. However the problem is naming the root model in openapi which is very weird. Maybe have a way to inject name in root model? Maybe this should have been a issue about feature but basically my idea is Annotated[some_root_model, metadata] not sure if that's possible tho. just an idea. ### Example Code ```Python DiscriminatedInternalIncType = Annotated[ Union[NoDataTransInternal, NoSymptomsInternal], Field(discriminator="type"), ] DiscriminatedInternalIncModel = RootModel[DiscriminatedInternalIncType] ``` ### Python, Pydantic & OS Version ```Text pydantic version: 2.6.1 pydantic-core version: 2.16.2 pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true install path: /Users/manimozaffar/Library/Caches/pypoetry/virtualenvs/nl-rpm-CEMQou1J-py3.11/lib/python3.11/site-packages/pydantic python version: 3.11.4 (v3.11.4:d2340ef257, Jun 6 2023, 19:15:51) [Clang 13.0.0 (clang-1300.0.29.30)] platform: macOS-14.2.1-arm64-arm-64bit related packages: typing_extensions-4.9.0 pyright-1.1.351 pydantic-extra-types-2.5.0 fastapi-0.103.2 email-validator-2.1.0.post1 pydantic-settings-2.2.1 commit: unknown ``` ## Maintainer reply Hi @ManiMozaffar, You can set the title via the `Field` constructor you're using, and that'll be used in the generated JSON schema: ```py import json from typing import Annotated from pydantic import RootModel, Field my_root = RootModel[Annotated[str | int, Field(..., description="A string or an integer", title="MyRootModel")]] print(json.dumps(my_root.model_json_schema(), indent=2)) """ { "anyOf": [ { "type": "string" }, { "type": "integer" } ], "description": "A string or an integer", "title": "MyRootModel" } """ ```

content:
# Pydantic V2, union mode prioritizes str infront of RootModel with Literal type  

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

Hi!

Using Pydantic v2, should literal types be able to take precedence over str types?
In the following code I've written a small type which is supposed to be set to either its intended value or none, Or if the special case strings `_ignore` and `_inherit` are hit we are supposed to return the matching classes.

This works for all other types than str, because when parsing str we hit this rule:
```
An exact type match, for example an int input to a float | int union validation is an exact type match for the int member
```

Which then returns str types instead of the rootmodels.

Test output:
![image](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/assets/13900053/c1fd24cf-d209-48a0-b3c6-6b4660d7d19c)

### Example Code

```Python
class Ignore(RootModel):
    root: Literal["_ignore"]


class Inherit(RootModel):
    root: Literal["_inherit"]


T = TypeVar("T")
ConfigValue = Union[Ignore, Inherit, T, None]


And the tests:

```py
import pytest
from pydantic import TypeAdapter

@pytest.mark.parametrize(
    "t,value,expected",
    [
        (str, "_ignore", Ignore),
        (int, "_ignore", Ignore),
        (bool, "_ignore", Ignore),
        (list[int], "_ignore", Ignore),
        (str, "_inherit", Inherit),
        (int, "_inherit", Inherit),
        (bool, "_inherit", Inherit),
        (list[int], "_inherit", Inherit),
        (str, str(), str),
        (int, int(), int),
        (bool, bool(), bool),
        (list[int], list[int](), list),
    ],
)
def test_typeadapter_ignore_inherit_for_types(t, value, expected):
    adapter = TypeAdapter(ConfigValue[t])
    result = adapter.validate_python(value, strict=True)
    assert isinstance(result, expected)
```
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
python -c "import pydantic.version; print(pydantic.version.version_info())"
             pydantic version: 2.6.1
        pydantic-core version: 2.16.2
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
                 install path: /home/n/git/bitbucket-gitlab-migration/.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.12.1 (main, Dec 10 2023, 15:07:36) [GCC 11.4.0]
                     platform: Linux-6.5.0-17-generic-x86_64-with-glibc2.35
             related packages: typing_extensions-4.9.0 mypy-1.8.0
                       commit: unknown
```

## Maintainer reply

@N0K0,

Good question. For now, you can set the priority that you desire by using `union_mode='left_to_right'`. Here's an example of how to do that, which results in all of your tests passing:

```py
from pydantic import RootModel, Field
from typing import Literal, Union, TypeVar, Annotated

class Ignore(RootModel):
    root: Literal["_ignore"]


class Inherit(RootModel):
    root: Literal["_inherit"]


T = TypeVar("T")
ConfigValue = Union[Ignore, Inherit, T, None]

import pytest
from pydantic import TypeAdapter

@pytest.mark.parametrize(
    "t,value,expected",
    [
        (str, "_ignore", Ignore),
        (int, "_ignore", Ignore),
        (bool, "_ignore", Ignore),
        (list[int], "_ignore", Ignore),
        (str, "_inherit", Inherit),
        (int, "_inherit", Inherit),
        (bool, "_inherit", Inherit),
        (list[int], "_inherit", Inherit),
        (str, str(), str),
        (int, int(), int),
        (bool, bool(), bool),
        (list[int], list[int](), list),
    ],
)
def test_typeadapter_ignore_inherit_for_types(t, value, expected):
    adapter = TypeAdapter(Annotated[ConfigValue[t], Field(union_mode="left_to_right")])
    result = adapter.validate_python(value, strict=True)
    assert isinstance(result, expected)
```

I do believe that the behavior you're witnessing without the `union_mode` set is expected, as a `str` is a perfect match for the input data.

Marking as resolved for now, as there's a pretty easy workaround 👍. Let me know if you have any follow up questions!

metadata.title: Pydantic V2, union mode prioritizes str infront of RootModel with Literal type

# Requiring Literal validation of class types works, but is not permitted per Literal documentation

### Initial Checks

- [X] I have searched GitHub for a duplicate issue and I'm sure this is something new
- [X] I have searched Google & StackOverflow for a solution and couldn't find anything
- [X] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this is a bug
- [X] I am confident that the issue is with pydantic (not my code, or another library in the ecosystem like [FastAPI](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com) or [mypy](https://mypy.readthedocs.io/en/stable))

### Description

I need to validate and type hint a model to accept exactly one of two different arbitrary classes, as follows:
```
class Foo: ...
class Bar: ...

class ObjectParams(BaseModel, arbitrary_types_allowed=True):
    obj_type: Literal[Foo, Bar]

x = ObjectParams(obj_type=Foo)
y = ObjectParams(obj_type=Bar)
z = ObjectParams(obj_type=str)
```

This code runs and properly errors out on the last line.
```
pydantic.error_wrappers.ValidationError: 1 validation error for ObjectParams
obj_type
  unexpected value; permitted: <class '__main__.Foo'>, <class '__main__.Bar'> (type=value_error.const; given=<class 'str'>; permitted=(<class '__main__.Foo'>, <class '__main__.Bar'>))
```

However, Pylance throws an error on the declaration of the Literal, saying:
```
Type arguments for "Literal" must be None, a literal value (int, bool, str, or bytes), or an enum value.
```

This matches the official docs for Literal as well, [as seen here](https://typing.readthedocs.io/en/latest/spec/literal.html#legal-parameters-for-literal-at-type-check-time:~:text=Any%20other%20types%3A%20for%20example%2C%20Literal%5BPath%5D%2C%20or%20Literal%5Bsome_object_instance%5D%20are%20illegal.%20This%20includes%20typevars%3A%20if%20T%20is%20a%20typevar%2C%20Literal%5BT%5D%20is%20not%20allowed.%20Typevars%20can%20vary%20over%20only%20types%2C%20never%20over%20values.).

I can't find any documentation that suggests the proper way to both typehint and validate this. Setting the field to `Any` and then adding a custom validator works fine, but removes the hinting.

Is there anything I'm missing here, or is this just not properly supported?

### Example Code

```Python
class Foo: ...


class Bar: ...


class ObjectParams(BaseModel, arbitrary_types_allowed=True):
    obj_type: Literal[Foo, Bar]


x = ObjectParams(obj_type=Foo)
y = ObjectParams(obj_type=Bar)
z = ObjectParams(obj_type=str)
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 1.10.13
            pydantic compiled: True
                 install path: C:\Users\AU21FN\AppData\Local\pypoetry\Cache\virtualenvs\onsight-kWhrw4G--py3.11\Lib\site-packages\pydantic
               python version: 3.11.7 (tags/v3.11.7:fa7a6f2, Dec  4 2023, 19:24:49) [MSC v.1937 64 bit (AMD64)]
                     platform: Windows-10-10.0.19045-SP0
     optional deps. installed: ['devtools', 'dotenv', 'typing-extensions']
```

### Affected Components

- [ ] [Compatibility between releases](https://docs.pydantic.dev/changelog/)
- [X] [Data validation/parsing](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/models/#basic-model-usage)
- [ ] [Data serialization](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/serialization/) - `.model_dump()` and `.model_dump_json()`
- [ ] [JSON Schema](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/json_schema/)
- [ ] [Dataclasses](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/dataclasses/)
- [ ] [Model Config](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/config/)
- [ ] [Field Types](https://docs.pydantic.dev/api/types/) - adding or changing a particular data type
- [ ] [Function validation decorator](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/validation_decorator/)
- [ ] [Generic Models](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/models/#generic-models)
- [ ] [Other Model behaviour](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/models/) - `model_construct()`, pickling, private attributes, ORM mode
- [ ] [Plugins](https://docs.pydantic.dev/) and integration with other tools - mypy, FastAPI, python-devtools, Hypothesis, VS Code, PyCharm, etc.

## Maintainer reply

@Wyko,

Yep! You've got the right approach now. Here's a full example:

```py
from pydantic import BaseModel, ValidationError

class Foo: 
    ...

class Bar: 
    ...


class ObjectParams(BaseModel):
    obj_type: type[Foo | Bar]

x = ObjectParams(obj_type=Foo)
print(repr(x))
#> ObjectParams(obj_type=<class '__main__.Foo'>)
y = ObjectParams(obj_type=Bar)
print(repr(y))
#> ObjectParams(obj_type=<class '__main__.Bar'>)
try:
    z = ObjectParams(obj_type=str)
except ValidationError as e:
    print(e)
    """
    pydantic_core._pydantic_core.ValidationError: 2 validation errors for ObjectParams
    obj_type.is-subclass[Foo]
    Input should be a subclass of Foo [type=is_subclass_of, input_value=<class 'str'>, input_type=type]
        For further information visit https://errors.pydantic.dev/2.7/v/is_subclass_of
    obj_type.is-subclass[Bar]
    Input should be a subclass of Bar [type=is_subclass_of, input_value=<class 'str'>, input_type=type]
        For further information visit https://errors.pydantic.dev/2.7/v/is_subclass_of
    """
```

Let me know if you have any follow up questions :).

metadata.title: Requiring Literal validation of class types works, but is not permitted per Literal documentation

content:
# timedelta doesn't parse fractional seconds

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

A field of the `timedelta` type, when given a `float`, seems to floor it to an `int` before creating a `timedelta`, even though `timedelta` supports fractional seconds.

### Example Code

```Python
from datetime import timedelta
from pydantic import BaseModel

class Foo(BaseModel):
    t: timedelta

print(repr(Foo(t=.5).t))
# datetime.timedelta(0)

print(repr(timedelta(seconds=.5)))
# datetime.timedelta(microseconds=500000)
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
$ python -c "import pydantic.version; print(pydantic.version.version_info())"
             pydantic version: 2.6.1
        pydantic-core version: 2.16.2
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
                 install path: .../venv/lib/python3.8/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.8.18 (default, Aug 25 2023, 13:20:30)  [GCC 11.4.0]
                     platform: Linux-6.1.0-1033-oem-x86_64-with-glibc2.35
             related packages: email-validator-2.1.0.post1 typing_extensions-4.9.0 fastapi-0.109.0 pydantic-settings-2.1.0 mypy-1.8.0
                       commit: unknown
```
```

## Maintainer reply

@brianmedigate,

When I run this on `main` with the latest version of `speedate`, I get the following:

```py
from datetime import timedelta
from pydantic import BaseModel

class Foo(BaseModel):
    t: timedelta

print(repr(Foo(t=.5).t))
# datetime.timedelta(microseconds=500000)

print(repr(timedelta(seconds=.5)))
# datetime.timedelta(microseconds=500000)
```

Additionally, with this extra config setting, I get the following behavior:

```py
from datetime import timedelta

from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict

class Foo(BaseModel):
    t: timedelta

    model_config = ConfigDict(ser_json_timedelta='float')


print(repr(Foo(t=.5).t))
# datetime.timedelta(microseconds=500000)

print(repr(timedelta(seconds=.5)))
# datetime.timedelta(microseconds=500000)

print(Foo(t=.5).model_dump_json())
#> {"t":0.5}

print(Foo(t=timedelta(seconds=.5)).model_dump_json())
#> {"t":0.5}
```

Marking this as resolved, as I think this shows that the behavior you're looking for is present.

metadata.title: timedelta doesn't parse fractional seconds

content:
# Union type: No parameter named "extra" for `Field` (type hints, `TypeAdapter` / `Annotated`)

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

Currently static type checkers like mypy emit a type error, when using [`extra="forbid"`](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/api/config/#pydantic.config.ConfigDict.extra) to validate and properly resolve smart union types via `TypeAdapter` + `Annotated`. Otherwise this feature already works functionally, hence a fix could look similar to:
```python
def Field(
    ...
    extra: Literal["allow", "forbid", "ignore"] | None = _Unset,
    ...
```

### Example Code

```Python
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Annotated

from pydantic import Field, TypeAdapter


@dataclass
class Foo:
    p1: list[str]


@dataclass
class Bar:
    p1: list[str]
    p2: int


## triggers 'No parameter named "extra"'
MyUnion = Annotated[Foo | Bar, Field(extra="forbid")]

## ... but works functionally:
## (variable) out: Bar | Foo
out = TypeAdapter(MyUnion).validate_python(Bar(p1=["a", "b"], p2=42))


## In contrast this wouldn't resolve properly:
## (variable) out: UnionType
# MyUnion = Foo | Bar
# out = TypeAdapter(MyUnion).validate_python(Bar(p1=["a", "b"], p2=42))
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.6.1
pydantic-core version: 2.16.2
pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
python version: 3.11.7
related packages: typing_extensions-4.9.0
commit: unknown
```

## Maintainer reply

@carol0801,

I'm a bit confused about your report here - is this a feature request, or a bug report? We don't currently support `extra` specifications on a per-field basis. You can add an `extra` specification on a model via `ConfigDict`.

Perhaps what you're looking for is using a `RootModel`, see more docs here: https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/concepts/models/#rootmodel-and-custom-root-types.

Going to close this as not planned for now, but happy to iterate more on what you're looking for / expecting here.

# @validate_call fails when class method returns an instance of the class

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

Let's say you have a simple class that has a method that returns an instance of the same class (see code below). This causes an error in Pydantic on import, even when validate_return is set to False. 

This is a case that both python (and mypy) can handle this (due to `from __future__ import annotations`) and is impacting a planned deployment (since it affects our ability to provide accurate intellisense for users while also allowing us to use pydantic's validation features).

## Maintainer reply

@caner-cetin,

Right - we implemented this warning here: https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/8243, so this behavior is expected and intentional.

A field name can't be the same as the type used for the field. Using an `alias` here is a good workaround, if you really need the field to be named `Stash`!

content:
# `__getstate__` implementation assumes existence of `__pydanitc_private__` and `__pydantic_extra__`. 

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

I have a use case where we are retrieving `SqlModel` instances from our databases and occasionally caching to minimize database hits. During the caching process, we are pickling the database results. However, as a byproduct of pickling the `__getstate__` method is called but the `__pydantic_private__` and `__pydantic_extra__` attributes do not exist on the model. 

This may ultimately be a problem with `SqlModel` and how it retrieves models, but I was curious if we actually _should_ be assuming that these attributes exist?

### Example Code

_No response_

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.6.1
        pydantic-core version: 2.16.2
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
                 install path: 
.venv/lib/python3.10/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.10.9 (v3.10.9:1dd9be6584, Dec  6 2022, 14:37:36) [Clang 13.0.0 (clang-1300.0.29.30)]
                     platform: macOS-13.5.2-arm64-arm-64bit
             related packages: typing_extensions-4.9.0 fastapi-0.108.0 pydantic-extra-types-2.5.0 pydantic-settings-2.1.0 mypy-0.942 email-validator-2.1.0.post1
                       commit: unknown
```

## Maintainer reply

@nwilson314,

Thanks for your question. The `__pydantic_private__` and `__pydantic_extra__` attributes will be set to `None` on instances where there are no extra or private values, and this is expected behavior. You can see the `BaseModel` api docs here: https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/api/base_model/#pydantic.BaseModel.

Does this answer your question?

content:
# Error on validating instance with generic class

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

Given an instance that conforms to a generic class and its type variables, it fails validation if it has been instantiated from the plain class. Upon inspection in the debugger, all constraints seem to be fulfilled.

On one side one can argue the object is actually not an instance of the parametrized class, but if the generic is only seen as a hint (not creating a new class) itshould only depend on the value of the attributes that are marked with a type variable.

A use case is when you are given already instantiated objects and want to check whether they also conform to a stricter or specific parametrized model.

### Example Code

```Python
from typing import Generic, TypeVar
import pydantic


class B(BaseModel):
    pass


T = TypeVar("T")


class A(BaseModel, Generic[T]):
    b: T


a1 = A(b=B())
a2 = A[B](b=B())

assert isinstance(a2, A)
A.model_validate(a2)

assert isinstance(a2, A[B])
A[B].model_validate(a2)

assert isinstance(a1, A[B]) # Fails
A[B].model_validate(a1)     # Fails
# a1 is an instance of A where attribute b is an instance of B
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.6.0
        pydantic-core version: 2.16.1
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
                 install path: /home/user/software/miniconda3/envs/my-env/lib/python3.9/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.9.18 (main, Sep 11 2023, 13:41:44)  [GCC 11.2.0]
                     platform: Linux-5.15.0-91-generic-x86_64-with-glibc2.35
             related packages: mypy-1.8.0 typing_extensions-4.9.0 pydantic-settings-2.1.0 email-validator-2.1.0.post1
                       commit: unknown
```

## Maintainer reply

@aeisenbarth,

Thanks for your question. The behavior that you're observing is expected, but can be a bit confusing. Here's some code with inline explanations:

```py
from typing import Generic, TypeVar

from pydantic import BaseModel


class B(BaseModel):
    pass


T = TypeVar("T")


class A(BaseModel, Generic[T]):
    b: T


a1 = A(b=B())
a2 = A[B](b=B())

assert isinstance(a1, A)
A.model_validate(a1)

assert isinstance(a2, A[B])
A[B].model_validate(a2)

# this is expected to fail; Pydantic creats a new class for A[B], so it's
# true that a1 is an instance of A, but it's not an instance of A[B]
assert isinstance(a1, A[B])

# this is also expected to fail, for a similar reason as above
A[B].model_validate(a1)

# this succeeds - because a1 has the b attribute set to an instance of B, revalidating it as an instance of A[B] by looking
# at indiivdual attributes will succeed
A[B].model_validate(a1, from_attributes=True)
```

One relevant section of the docs is [here](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/concepts/models/#generic-models).

> While it may not raise an error, we strongly advise against using parametrized generics in isinstance checks.
For example, you should not do isinstance(my_model, MyGenericModel[int]). However, it is fine to do isinstance(my_model, MyGenericModel). (Note that, for standard generics, it would raise an error to do a subclass check with a parameterized generic.)
If you need to perform isinstance checks against parametrized generics, you can do this by subclassing the parametrized generic class. This looks like class MyIntModel(MyGenericModel[int]): ... and isinstance(my_model, MyIntModel).

Given this advice, I'd recommend only performing `isinstance` checks on subclasses as recommended in the last line.

content:
# valid Pydantic model with fields having `required` attribute generates invalid JSON schema

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

I have discovered that using `required` attribute in the field declaration brings to an invalid JSON schema generation.

Below two valid Pydantic models are defined:

```python
class ValidModel(pydantic.BaseModel):
    f: int = pydantic.Field(...)


class InvalidModel(pydantic.BaseModel):
    f: int = pydantic.Field(..., required=True)
```

Which produce following JSON schemas:

```json
{
  "properties": {
    "f": {
      "title": "F",
      "type": "integer"
    }
  },
  "required": [
    "f"
  ],
  "title": "ValidModel",
  "type": "object"
}

{
  "properties": {
    "f": {
      "required": true,
      "title": "F",
      "type": "integer"
    }
  },
  "required": [
    "f"
  ],
  "title": "InvalidModel",
  "type": "object"
}
```

One can see, that `InvalidModel` has an additional `required` attribute for the field `f`:

```json
    "f": {
      "required": true,
      "title": "F",
      "type": "integer"
    }
```

which will raise an exception from `jsonschema` when attempting to validate:

```python
jsonschema.validate(InvalidModel(f=1).dict(), InvalidModel.schema())
```

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/tmp/2.py", line 17, in <module>
    jsonschema.validate(InvalidModel(f=1).dict(), InvalidModel.schema())
  File "/usr/lib/python3/dist-packages/jsonschema/validators.py", line 930, in validate
    cls.check_schema(schema)
  File "/usr/lib/python3/dist-packages/jsonschema/validators.py", line 294, in check_schema
    raise exceptions.SchemaError.create_from(error)
jsonschema.exceptions.SchemaError: True is not of type 'array'

Failed validating 'type' in metaschema['properties']['properties']['additionalProperties']['properties']['required']:
    {'default': [],
     'items': {'type': 'string'},
     'type': 'array',
     'uniqueItems': True}

On schema['properties']['f']['required']:
    True
```

The schema is indeed invalid (tested with `ajv-cli`)

Setting any other attribute will generate valid schema (I am not an expert in JSON schema specification, but probably it works for all non-reserved keywords):

```python
class InvalidModel(pydantic.BaseModel):
    f: int = pydantic.Field(..., other_attrib=True)
```


### Example Code

```Python
import pydantic
import jsonschema


class ValidModel(pydantic.BaseModel):
    f: int = pydantic.Field(...)


class InvalidModel(pydantic.BaseModel):
    f: int = pydantic.Field(..., required=True)


print(pydantic.version.version_info())
print(ValidModel.schema_json(indent=2))
print(InvalidModel.schema_json(indent=2))

try:
    jsonschema.validate(InvalidModel(f=1).dict(), InvalidModel.schema())
except jsonschema.exceptions.SchemaError as e:
    print(e)
```


### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

reproduced both with Pydantic 1.x and Pydantic 2.x

#### Pydantic 1.x

```Text
             pydantic version: 1.9.0
            pydantic compiled: True
                 install path: /home/kostunin/.local/lib/python3.10/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.10.12 (main, Nov 20 2023, 15:14:05) [GCC 11.4.0]
                     platform: Linux-5.14.0-1036-oem-x86_64-with-glibc2.35
     optional deps. installed: ['dotenv', 'typing-extensions']
```

#### Pydantic 2.x

```Text
             pydantic version: 2.6.1
        pydantic-core version: 2.16.2
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
                 install path: /home/kostunin/.local/lib/python3.10/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.10.12 (main, Nov 20 2023, 15:14:05) [GCC 11.4.0]
                     platform: Linux-5.14.0-1036-oem-x86_64-with-glibc2.35
             related packages: typing_extensions-4.9.0 typing_extensions-4.5.0
                       commit: unknown
```

## Maintainer reply

@kostunin,

Thanks for your question. Indeed, you can pass additional metadata to the `Field` function which ends up getting added to `json_schema_extra` and being attached to the JSON schema.

I'd consider it a case of user error here - we don't support `required` as an argument of `Field`, so it's on the user to only specify extra info for the JSON schema that they're confident will result in a valid JSON schema. Pydantic interprets which fields are required or not, and adds that information to a valid JSON schema, as you've noted as well.

See this section of the migration guide re various ways to indicate that a field is required: https://docs.pydantic.dev/dev/migration/#required-optional-and-nullable-fields

Feel free to let me know if you have any follow up questions!

# pickling models with a PlainSerializer Annotated field

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

hi there,

i am struggling to pickle a model which has a PlainSerializer Annotated field. I'm using below snippet in a FastAPI app and want to use PlainSerializer to return JSONMaybeSecretStr field secret values to the frontend when models are serialised to JSON. We also perform some pickling of models in the backend.

thanks!


### Example Code

```Python
import os
import pickle

from pydantic import BaseModel, SecretStr

from typing_extensions import Annotated
from pydantic.functional_serializers import PlainSerializer, WrapSerializer


class MaybeSecretStr(SecretStr):
    def _display(self) -> str:
        if os.getenv("SHOULD_LOG_SECRETS"):
            return self.get_secret_value()
        else:
            return super()._display()


# # get secret value when serialise to JSON - simplifies returning these fields from routes back to FE
JSONMaybeSecretStr = Annotated[
    MaybeSecretStr, PlainSerializer(lambda x: x.get_secret_value(), return_type=str, when_used="json")
]


class Model(BaseModel):
    field: JSONMaybeSecretStr

instance = Model(field=JSONMaybeSecretStr("super secret value"))

pickle.dumps(instance)  # raises _pickle.PicklingError: Can't pickle <function <lambda> at 0x000001BB8BA43E20>: attribute lookup <lambda> on __main__ failed
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.6.0
        pydantic-core version: 2.16.1
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
                 install path: C:\Users\hughesa\Miniconda3\envs\genie_py310\Lib\site-packages\pydantic
               python version: 3.10.13 | packaged by conda-forge | (main, Dec 23 2023, 15:27:34) [MSC v.1937 64 bit (AMD64)]
                     platform: Windows-10-10.0.22621-SP0
             related packages: email-validator-2.1.0.post1 fastapi-0.103.1 pydantic-settings-2.1.0 typing_extensions-4.9.0
                       commit: unknown
```

## Maintainer reply

@aleks-sch,

Indeed, as @andresliszt suggested (thanks!), you can avoid using the lambda function in order to successfully pickle your instance:

```py
import os
import pickle

from pydantic import BaseModel, SecretStr

from typing_extensions import Annotated
from pydantic.functional_serializers import PlainSerializer, WrapSerializer


class MaybeSecretStr(SecretStr):
    def _display(self) -> str:
        if os.getenv("SHOULD_LOG_SECRETS"):
            return self.get_secret_value()
        else:
            return super()._display()

def serializer(x: MaybeSecretStr) -> str:
    return x.get_secret_value()

# # get secret value when serialise to JSON - simplifies returning these fields from routes back to FE
JSONMaybeSecretStr = Annotated[
    MaybeSecretStr, PlainSerializer(serializer, return_type=str, when_used="json")
]


class Model(BaseModel):
    field: JSONMaybeSecretStr

instance = Model(field=JSONMaybeSecretStr("super secret value"))

dumped_data = pickle.dumps(instance)
loaded_instance = pickle.loads(dumped_data)
print(loaded_instance.field.get_secret_value())
#> super secret value
```

# Using Literal to over-constrain an Enum does not validate JSON correctly

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

Unfortunately pydantic won't coerce "a_backend" from json into Backends.A for AModel even though it would for CommonModel. If pydantic accepts Literal with enum values at model build time I would expect it to be more aligned with json behaviour for enums elsewhere.

This seems like a bug since in this example the JSON schema looks correct for what I was trying to achieve.

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import Literal
from enum import Enum
from pprint import pprint

class Backends(Enum):
    A = "a_backend"
    B = "b_service"
    C = "smile"

class CommonModel(BaseModel):
    backend: Backends
    common_arg: int

class AModel(CommonModel):
    backend: Literal[Backends.A]
    a_specific_arg: int

class BCModel(CommonModel):
    backend: Literal[Backends.B, Backends.C]
    b_or_c_specific_arg: str

class Request(BaseModel):
    config: AModel | BCModel

pprint(BCModel.model_json_schema())

BCModel.model_validate_json('{"backend": "b_service", "b_or_c_specific_arg": ":)", "common_arg": 12}')
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.6.0
        pydantic-core version: 2.16.1
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
                 install path: /home/wstott/repos/tritium/cloud/service_proxy/venv/lib/python3.10/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.10.12 (main, Nov 20 2023, 15:14:05) [GCC 11.4.0]
                     platform: Linux-6.5.0-14-generic-x86_64-with-glibc2.35
             related packages: typing_extensions-4.9.0 pyright-1.1.349 mypy-1.8.0
                       commit: unknown
```

## Maintainer reply

@willstott101,

This has been discussed previously in https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/3850 and https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/2257.

There's a pretty simple solution, which is inheriting from both `str` and `Enum` in your `Backends` class. That being said, this issue has come up enough that I think a docs update would help to make this clear for users in the future.

See this example for how to resolve your problem:

```py
from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import Literal
from enum import Enum
from pprint import pprint

class Backends(str, Enum): # note, subclassing from str as well
    A = "a_backend"
    B = "b_service"
    C = "smile"

class CommonModel(BaseModel):
    backend: Backends
    common_arg: int

class AModel(CommonModel):
    backend: Literal[Backends.A]
    a_specific_arg: int

class BCModel(CommonModel):
    backend: Literal[Backends.B, Backends.C]
    b_or_c_specific_arg: str

class Request(BaseModel):
    config: AModel | BCModel

pprint(BCModel.model_json_schema())

BCModel.model_validate_json('{"backend": "b_service", "b_or_c_specific_arg": ":)", "common_arg": 12}')
```

# Models A and B are pydantic models. One attribute type of model B is model A. I rewrote dict of model A. When I called dict of model B, dict of model A was not triggered.

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

Models A and B are pydantic models. One attribute type of model B is model A. I rewrote dict of model A. When I called dict of model B, dict of model A was not triggered.
When I use pydantic v1 version, this scenario will trigger dict of a, but when I switch to v2 version, this function is not available?

### Example Code

```Python
class A(BaseModel):
        a: int = 1

        def dict(self, *args, **kwargs):
            print("xxxxx")
            return super().dict(*args, **kwargs)


    class B(BaseModel):
        a: Optional[A] = None
        b: int = 2


    model = B(a={"a": 123}, b="111")
    print(model.dict())
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
import pydantic.version; print(pydantic.version.version_info())
             pydantic version: 2.6.0
        pydantic-core version: 2.16.1
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
                 install path: /Users/xxxx/xxx/Venv/xxxx/lib/python3.10/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.10.13 (main, Dec 22 2023, 17:45:36) [Clang 15.0.0 (clang-1500.1.0.2.5)]
                     platform: macOS-14.2.1-arm64-arm-64bit
             related packages: email-validator-1.3.1 typing_extensions-4.9.0
                       commit: unknown
```

## Maintainer reply

@jaxonister,

Right, as stated in the migration guide, `dict()` usage has been replaced with `model_dump()` usage: https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/migration/#changes-to-pydanticbasemodel.

If you write a custom serializer for `A`, that should be called when you call `model_dump()` on some instance of `B`.

```py
from typing import Any, Dict, Optional

from pydantic import BaseModel, model_serializer


class A(BaseModel):
    a: int = 1

    @model_serializer(mode='wrap')
    def ser_model(self, handler) -> Dict[str, Any]: 
        print("xxxxx")  
        return handler(self)


class B(BaseModel):
    a: Optional[A] = None
    b: int = 2


model = B(a={"a": 123}, b="111")
print(model.model_dump())
#> xxxxx
#> {'a': {'a': 123}, 'b': 111}
```

Closing this as resolved.

# Unexpected behaviour with attributes using `default` or `default_factory`

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

I came across an unexpected behaviour when comparing two instances of the same model in my own `__eq__` method.

When using a `default` value or `default_factory`, the attribute name is contained in `model_fields_set` if the attribute is provided. If it is not provided, however, the attribute name doesn't appear in `model_fields_set`:

```python
class DummyModel(BaseModel):
    model_config = ConfigDict(extra="allow")

    plain: str
    default: str = "B"
    default_factory: str = Field(default_factory=lambda: "C")


model_a = DummyModel(
    plain="A",
    default="B",
    default_factory="C",
    extra="D",
)

print("fields_set", model_a.model_fields_set)
# fields_set {'default', 'extra', 'plain', 'default_factory'}

model_b = DummyModel(plain="A", extra="B")

print("fields_set", model_b.model_fields_set)
# fields_set {'extra', 'plain'}
```

After thinking about this, this is somewhat expected based on the documentation on `model_fields_set`:
> Returns the set of fields that have been explicitly set on this model instance.

So my approach to take a look at _all_ non-pydantic attributes (apart from `computed` attributes) is 
```python
set(vars(model_b).keys()) | model_b.model_fields_set
# {'default', 'plain', 'extra', 'default_factory'}

# vars(...) alone is not enough: 
set(vars(model_b).keys())
# {'default', 'default_factory', 'plain'} 
# misses the extra attribute
```

I'd favor a more explicit note in the documentation for someone like me that didn't think this whole explicit thing through. I'm happy to contribute a PR with a documentation update if you think that a note would be nice :) 

If you are aware of a better solution than mine, I would love to include that in the documentation as well!


### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict, Field


class DummyModel(BaseModel):
    model_config = ConfigDict(extra="allow")

    plain: str
    default: str = "B"
    default_factory: str = Field(default_factory=lambda: "C")


model_a = DummyModel(
    plain="A",
    default="B",
    default_factory="C",
    extra="D",
)

print("fields_set", model_a.model_fields_set)
# fields_set {'default', 'extra', 'plain', 'default_factory'}

model_b = DummyModel(plain="A", extra="B")

print("fields_set", model_b.model_fields_set)
# fields_set {'extra', 'plain'}

# vars(...) alone is not enough
print("vars", set(vars(model_b).keys()))
# {'default', 'default_factory', 'plain'}


print("vars | model_fields_set", set(vars(model_b).keys()) | model_b.model_fields_set)
{"default", "plain", "extra", "default_factory"}
```


### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.5.3
        pydantic-core version: 2.14.6
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=false
                 install path: /Users/thomasmarwitz/mambaforge/envs/test/lib/python3.12/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.12.1 | packaged by conda-forge | (main, Dec 23 2023, 08:01:35) [Clang 16.0.6 ]
                     platform: macOS-14.1.1-arm64-arm-64bit
             related packages: typing_extensions-4.9.0 pydantic-settings-2.1.0
```

## Maintainer reply

@ThomasMarwitzQC,

Thanks for the detailed inquiry. I'm not entirely sure what you mean by "all non Pydantic attributes", but if your goal is to get the list of all of the values provided - both pydantic fields and extra values, I think the following approach might be the cleanest option. I've shown the yield of said union as well:

```py
set(model_b.model_extra) | set(model_b.model_fields)
#> {'extra', 'plain', 'default_factory', 'default'}
```

Let me know what you think about this solution! Then we can chat about a docs update 🚀. Thanks for your willingness to contribute!

metadata.title: Unexpected behaviour with attributes using `default` or `default_factory`

# Class with after model validator doesnt work as optional parameter 

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

If a `BaseModel` subclass has an optional parameter that can be another `BaseModel`  subclass with a model validator, `None` is always assigned regardless of the input parameter passed. 

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel, model_validator


class ValidatedCls(BaseModel):
    """A class with a model validator"""

    @model_validator(mode='after')
    def ensure_lengths_equal(self):
        print(self)


class Other(BaseModel):
    """A non-validated class."""
    

class Parent(BaseModel):
    """Class containing attrs which are instances of the other two."""

    validated: ValidatedCls | None = None
    other: Other | None = None


if __name__ == "__main__":
    validated = ValidatedCls()
    other = Other()
    parent = Parent(validated=validated, other=other)
    assert parent.other == other  # works fine

    assert parent.validated is not None  # this fails
    assert parent.validated == validated  # as does this
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.5.3
pydantic-core version: 2.14.6
pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
install path: C:\Users\xik9\AppData\Roaming\Python\Python311\site-packages\pydantic
python version: 3.11.5 | packaged by Anaconda, Inc. | (main, Sep 11 2023, 13:26:23) [MSC v.1916 64 bit (AMD64)]
platform: Windows-10-10.0.19045-SP0
related packages: typing_extensions-4.7.1
```

## Maintainer reply

@d-chambers,

You have a small error in your `model_validator` logic. You need to `return self` at the end of the function. If you make this change, then everything works as expected :).

```py
from pydantic import BaseModel, model_validator


class ValidatedCls(BaseModel):
    """A class with a model validator"""

    @model_validator(mode='after')
    def ensure_lengths_equal(self):
        print(self)
        return self


class Other(BaseModel):
    """A non-validated class."""
    

class Parent(BaseModel):
    """Class containing attrs which are instances of the other two."""

    validated: ValidatedCls | None = None
    other: Other | None = None


if __name__ == "__main__":
    validated = ValidatedCls()
    other = Other()
    parent = Parent(validated=validated, other=other)
    assert parent.other == other  # works fine

    assert parent.validated is not None  # works fine
    assert parent.validated == validated  # works fine
```

# getting error while parsing list[int] from Fastapi query function 

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

getting bellow error while using list[int] from typing module
![Screenshot from 2024-01-24 19-23-41](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/assets/26969620/b9c08d87-8e15-4793-a69e-2ff471dd9695)




### Example Code

```Python
from typing import List, Optional, Annotated, Union
from fastapi import Query, Path, Header

class MarketCompaniesSummariesQueryParams:
    def __init__(
        self,
        region: NaRegionEnum,
        level: MarketCompanyMarketsLevel,
        level_id: List[int] = Query(None,ge=0),
        comp_id: Annotated[List[int], Query(ge=0)] = None,
        sector_id: Optional[List[int]] = Query(None, ge=0),
      ):
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.5.3
        pydantic-core version: 2.14.6
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
                 install path: /home/aasthaprasad/.local/share/virtualenvs/data_api_one-mK7EIkj0/lib/python3.10/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.10.13 (main, Aug 25 2023, 13:20:03) [GCC 9.4.0]
                     platform: Linux-5.15.0-91-generic-x86_64-with-glibc2.31
             related packages: fastapi-0.101.0 typing_extensions-4.9.0


Fastapi: version : 0.109.0
```

## Maintainer reply

@mausam20,

Right, this is expected behavior. The constraint `ge` belongs on the `int` type, not the `list`. So you can do something like the following:

```py
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field, ValidationError

from typing import Annotated, List

class Foo(BaseModel):
    bar: List[Annotated[int, Field(ge=0)]]

f = Foo(bar=[1, 2, 3])
print(f)
#> bar=[1, 2, 3]

try:
    f = Foo(bar=[1, -2, 3])
except ValidationError as e:
    print(e)
    """
    1 validation error for Foo
    bar.1
    Input should be greater than or equal to 0 [type=greater_than_equal, input_value=-2, input_type=int]
        For further information visit https://errors.pydantic.dev/2.6/v/greater_than_equal
    """
```

In your case, you can use `= None` to set the default, as well as `Query` instead of `Field`. 

Closing, as this isn't a bug to be fixed in `pydantic`. Let me know if you have any follow up questions!

# HttpUrl docs example has type issues

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

If you run the following example from the docs through mypy you get errors:

<https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/blob/e131d53a07da080c2ba642775efa9e81cb2cf0ba/pydantic/networks.py#L117-L144>

Here are the mypy errors being raised:

```
test.py:8: error: Argument "url" to "MyModel" has incompatible type "str"; expected "Url"  [arg-type]
test.py:14: error: Argument "url" to "MyModel" has incompatible type "str"; expected "Url"  [arg-type]
test.py:24: error: Argument "url" to "MyModel" has incompatible type "str"; expected "Url"  [arg-type]
```

In pydantic v1, you'd use `parse_obj_as` to convert the url so I was looking for an example of how to handle this and unfortunately this example doesn't work. 

### Example Code

_No response_

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
             pydantic version: 2.5.3
        pydantic-core version: 2.14.6
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
                 install path: /home/david/Projects/Compute-PMC/server/.venv/lib/python3.9/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.9.15 (main, Dec  1 2022, 15:04:29)  [GCC 9.4.0]
                     platform: Linux-5.4.0-164-generic-x86_64-with-glibc2.31
             related packages: email-validator-2.1.0.post1 mypy-1.8.0 fastapi-0.108.0 typing_extensions-4.9.0 pydantic-settings-2.1.0 pyright-1.1.344

mypy version: 1.8.0
```

## Maintainer reply

@daviddavis,

Thanks for your question. Indeed, `mypy` would prefer something along the lines of:

```py
m = MyModel(url=HttpUrl('http://www.example.com/'))
```

Even though with the code as it stands, the string is converted into a `HttpUrl` instance during the validation process.

I'll add an update to the example to reflect this. Thanks for bringing this to our attention :).
