# Unexpected error encountered when using Optional[xx]

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

I can't use Optional[str] to make a field invisible when model_dumping

### Example Code

```Python
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

from pprint import pprint
from typing import Optional, Literal

from pydantic import BaseModel


class ContentParts(BaseModel):
    """
    请求体
    """

    class Image(BaseModel):
        url: str
        detail: Optional[Literal["low", "high", "auto"]] = "auto"

    type: str
    text: Optional[str]
    image_url: Optional[Image]


try:
    ContentParts(type="text", text="testing")
except Exception as e:
    pprint(e)
    """
    1 validation error for ContentParts
image_url
  Field required [type=missing, input_value={'type': 'text', 'text': 'testing'}, input_type=dict]
    For further information visit https://errors.pydantic.dev/2.5/v/missing
    """

try:
    ContentParts(type="image", image_url=ContentParts.Image(url="https://www.baidu.com"))
except Exception as e:
    pprint(e)
    """
    1 validation error for ContentParts
text
  Field required [type=missing, input_value={'type': 'image', 'image_...du.com', detail='auto')}, input_type=dict]
    For further information visit https://errors.pydantic.dev/2.5/v/missing

    """


class ContentPartsAnother(BaseModel):
    """
    请求体
    """

    text: str
    image: Optional[bool] = None


try:
    pprint(ContentPartsAnother(text="testing").model_dump_json())
except Exception as e:
    pprint(e)
    """
    '{"text":"testing","image":null}'
    """
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
❯ python -c "import pydantic.version; print(pydantic.version.version_info())"
             pydantic version: 2.5.3
        pydantic-core version: 2.14.6
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
                 install path: /home/<USER>/Project/LlmKira/Openaibot/venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.11.6 (main, Nov 14 2023, 09:36:21) [GCC 13.2.1 20230801]
                     platform: Linux-6.6.10-arch1-1-x86_64-with-glibc2.38
             related packages: pydantic-settings-2.1.0 typing_extensions-4.8.0
```

## Maintainer reply

@sudoskys,

See our detailed docs on the usage of `Optional` types here: https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/migration/#required-optional-and-nullable-fields.

In order for a field to be not required, you must specify a default value, even if you specify the type as `Optional[x]`.

If you would like to exclude fields with `None` values from the model dump, you can use `model_dump(exclude_none=True)`.

Closing this, as this is all expected behavior. Please see the docs on serialization for more detail: https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/concepts/serialization/

### Initial Checks

See the code example below, it's quite self-explanatory

from pydantic import BaseModel

class State(BaseModel):
    c: int = 42

ref = State.c # fails with AttributeError: c
```

Any standard pythonic class has no such issue:
```
ref = NonPydantic.c

print(ref)
```
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.5.1
        pydantic-core version: 2.14.3
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=false
                 install path: /Users/renarde/Library/Application Support/hatch/env/virtual/schorle/Z6f97Wm-/schorle/lib/python3.10/site-packages/pydantic

             related packages: typing_extensions-4.8.0 mypy-1.8.0 fastapi-0.104.1
```

## Maintainer reply

@renardeinside,

This is by design. If you want to access an attribute on the class, you can do the following:

```py
from typing import ClassVar

from pydantic import BaseModel

class State(BaseModel):
    c: ClassVar[int] = 42

ref = State.c
print(ref)
#> 42
```

content:
# Type errors with @model_validator

The fix for #7152 was merged in v2.2.2 but apparently there has been a regression because with pydantic 2.5.3 pyright again reports a type error. It should be noted that this doesn't only happen for `mode="wrap"`.

The same type error can also be observed with `mode="before"` and `mode="after"`, for example the code from [the documentation](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/concepts/validators/#model-validators):

```python
from typing import Any

from pydantic import BaseModel, ValidationError, model_validator


class UserModel(BaseModel):
    username: str
    password1: str
    password2: str

    @model_validator(mode='before')
    @classmethod
    def check_card_number_omitted(cls, data: Any) -> Any:
        if isinstance(data, dict):
            assert (
                'card_number' not in data
            ), 'card_number should not be included'
        return data

    @model_validator(mode='after')
    def check_passwords_match(self) -> 'UserModel':
        pw1 = self.password1
        pw2 = self.password2
        if pw1 is not None and pw2 is not None and pw1 != pw2:
            raise ValueError('passwords do not match')
        return self
```

results in the type errors:

```
/private/tmp/foo/foo.py
  /private/tmp/foo/foo.py:11:6 - error: Argument of type "(cls: type[Self@UserModel], data: Any) -> Any" cannot be assigned to parameter of type "_AnyModeBeforeValidator"
    Type "(cls: type[Self@UserModel], data: Any) -> Any" cannot be assigned to type "_AnyModeBeforeValidator"
      Type "(cls: type[Self@UserModel], data: Any) -> Any" cannot be assigned to type "(cls: Any, __value: Any, __info: ValidationInfo) -> Any"
        Function accepts too many positional parameters; expected 2 but received 3

# Import issue with auto schema creation with dataclass

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

Hi,

I boiled down 2 issues (that look like just 1 really) to these 2 files and steps.

When running file 1 as is, I get the following error:
```
pydantic.errors.PydanticUndefinedAnnotation: name 'Optional' is not defined
```

if I import manually `Optional` in file 1, then it "works".
In a seemingly, related issue, at this point, I get this error:
```
pydantic.errors.PydanticUndefinedAnnotation: name 'datetime' is not defined
```
which can be corrected by changing the import to 
```Python
import datetime
```
and updating the usages to
```Python
Optional[datetime.datetime]
```

All in all, it looks like Pydantic does not use/execute the imports for file 2 when running file 1 even though it uses file 2

Having 2 files is required for the issue to reproduce (all in 1 file does not crash).

### Example Code

file 1:

```Python
from __future__ import annotations


from my_class import MyClass
from pydantic import ConfigDict, validate_call


@validate_call(
    config=ConfigDict(strict=True, arbitrary_types_allowed=True),
    validate_return=True,
)
def test(my_class: MyClass):
    pass
```

file 2:
```Python
from __future__ import annotations

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import datetime
from typing import Optional


@dataclass
class MyClass:
    member: Optional[str] = None
    created: Optional[datetime] = None
    updated: Optional[datetime] = None
    deleted: Optional[datetime] = None
```


### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
$ python -c "import pydantic.version; print(pydantic.version.version_info())"

             pydantic version: 2.5.3
        pydantic-core version: 2.14.6
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=false
                 install path: .virtualenvs/env3/lib/python3.9/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.9.12 (main, May 12 2023, 09:21:14)  [Clang 14.0.3 (clang-1403.0.22.14.1)]
                     platform: macOS-14.1.2-x86_64-i386-64bit
             related packages: email-validator-2.0.0.post2 typing_extensions-4.7.1
```

## Maintainer reply

@lpgros,

If you remove `from __future__ import annotations` from `file2`, things work as expected. This doesn't seem to be an issue with `pydantic`, but rather your import patterns.

# `model.model_copy(deep=True)` does not make a copy of `model_config`

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

Pretty simple issue, I am not sure if this is intended (or documented) but it tripped me up today:

When I modify model_config on a deep-copied object, the original object's config is affected. I can work around this, but it seems unintentional - feel free to close if that is not the case.

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel

class MyModel(BaseModel, frozen=True):
    a: int

m = MyModel(a=1)
m_for_editing = m.model_copy(deep=True)  # make a copy
m.model_config['frozen'] = False  # unfreeze the copy so we can make edits
m_for_editing.a = 2

assert m_for_editing.a == 2, 'copy was not updated'  # this passes as expected
assert m.a == 1, 'original was modified'  # of course this passes too
assert m.model_config['frozen'], "original was unfrozen"  # this fails
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.5.2
        pydantic-core version: 2.14.5
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true

content:
# [mypy] unexpected behaviour for `pydantic-orm` errors when composing a `model_config`

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

I recently migrated a code base with lots of complex pydantic models from v1 to v2. One thing we had in a lot of places was a `CustomPydanticConfig` class, which most of our models inherited. I replaced this with a common `ConfigDict` object.

Basically, we have:

```python
# v1

class CustomPydanticConfig:
    ...

class MyModel(BaseModel):
    
    class Config(CustomPydanticConfig):
        ...

# v2

CUSTOM_PYDANTIC_CONFIG = ConfigDict(...)

class MyModel(BaseModel):
    model_config = CUSTOM_PYDANTIC_CONFIG | ConfigDict(...)
```

When specifying `from_attributes=True` in the `ConfigDict` that gets combined with `CUSTOM_PYDANTIC_CONFIG`, I have unexpected errors from the pydantic mypy plugin (see MRE).


Apart from that, the migration process was relatively straightforward (`bump-pydantic` is great!), and the v2 API is much nicer to use, kudos! :clap: 

One other error I encountered was with nested discriminated unions, but this seems to already be tracked in #7487 .

Thanks a lot for your work on `pydantic`!

### Example Code

```Python
from typing import NamedTuple
from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict

COMMON_PYDANTIC_CONFIG = ConfigDict(extra="ignore", use_enum_values=True)


class MyTuple(NamedTuple):
    s: str
    i: int


class FromAttributes(BaseModel):
    s: str
    i: int
    model_config = COMMON_PYDANTIC_CONFIG | ConfigDict(from_attributes=True)


class FromAttributesNonComposedConfig(BaseModel):
    s: str
    i: int
    model_config = ConfigDict(extra="ignore", use_enum_values=True, from_attributes=True)


class NotFromAttributes(BaseModel):
    s: str
    i: int
    model_config = COMMON_PYDANTIC_CONFIG


t = MyTuple(s="hello", i=42)

# mypy error (pydantic-orm): '"FromAttributes" does not have from_attributes=True'. No runtime error. False positive ?
FromAttributes.from_orm(t)
FromAttributes.model_validate(t)  # no error

FromAttributesNonComposedConfig.from_orm(t)  # no error
FromAttributesNonComposedConfig.model_validate(t)  # no error

# mypy error (pydantic-orm): '"FromAttributes" does not have from_attributes=True'. Runtime error
NotFromAttributes.from_orm(t)
# no mypy error, but a runtime error. False negative ?
NotFromAttributes.model_validate(t)
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.5.2
       pydantic-core version: 2.14.5
         pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
                install path: /home/lpeschke/work/toucan/laputa/.direnv/python-3.11/lib/python3.11/site-packages/pydantic
              python version: 3.11.6 (main, Nov 19 2023, 14:12:09) [GCC 13.2.1 20231011 (Red Hat 13.2.1-4)]
                    platform: Linux-6.6.4-200.fc39.x86_64-x86_64-with-glibc2.38
            related packages: mypy-1.2.0 typing_extensions-4.7.1 pydantic-settings-2.1.0
```

## Maintainer reply

@lukapeschke,

Looks like all of the `mypy` issues that you're getting are with calls to `from_orm`. Given that this method is deprecated in favor of `model_validate`, I don't think these are `mypy` bugs that we're likely to focus on fixing.

See notes about `from_orm` -> `model_validate` in the [migration guide](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/migration/#changes-to-pydanticbasemodel).

If I've missed something here about the nature of your errors, feel free to let me know 👍

## Maintainer reply

@BigNerd,

Thanks for reporting this. This is due to the fact that your TestRequest model isn't able to generate a json schema. See more details [here](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/api/errors/#pydantic.errors.PydanticInvalidForJsonSchema) re the error that you're getting.

See the [`customzing json schema`](https://docs.pydantic.dev/dev/concepts/json_schema/#withjsonschema-annotation) section of the docs re how to resolve this issue.

# `RecursionError` while using nested model with references to parent

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

I have implemented a hierarchical tree-like object, where each `Operation` has both `parent` and `children`.
When I try to run the example code, I get the following error:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/mstechly/Documents/code/2023-11-30-serialization/knifey/pydantic_test.py", line 30, in <module>
    op_1 == op_2
  File "/Users/mstechly/.pyenv/versions/2023-11-30-serialization/lib/python3.10/site-packages/pydantic/main.py", line 873, in __eq__
    and self.__dict__ == other.__dict__
  File "/Users/mstechly/.pyenv/versions/2023-11-30-serialization/lib/python3.10/site-packages/pydantic/main.py", line 873, in __eq__
    and self.__dict__ == other.__dict__
  File "/Users/mstechly/.pyenv/versions/2023-11-30-serialization/lib/python3.10/site-packages/pydantic/main.py", line 873, in __eq__
    and self.__dict__ == other.__dict__
  [Previous line repeated 280 more times]
  File "/Users/mstechly/.pyenv/versions/2023-11-30-serialization/lib/python3.10/site-packages/pydantic/main.py", line 864, in __eq__
    if isinstance(other, BaseModel):
  File "/Users/mstechly/.pyenv/versions/2023-11-30-serialization/lib/python3.10/site-packages/pydantic/_internal/_model_construction.py", line 226, in __instancecheck__
    return hasattr(instance, '__pydantic_validator__') and super().__instancecheck__(instance)
  File "/Users/mstechly/.pyenv/versions/3.10.10/lib/python3.10/abc.py", line 119, in __instancecheck__
    return _abc_instancecheck(cls, instance)
RecursionError: maximum recursion depth exceeded in comparison
```
The thing that's causing the issues is this: `child.parent = self` , as if I comment it out it all works.
However, the serialization with `model_dump` works well (see `exclude=True`).

### Example Code

```Python
from typing import Any, Dict, Optional
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field

class Operation(BaseModel):
    name: str
    parent: Optional["Operation"] = Field(exclude=True, default=None)
    children: Dict[str, "Operation"] = Field(default_factory=dict)

    def __init__(self, **data: dict[str, Any]):
        super().__init__(**data)
        for child in self.children.values():
                child.parent = self


def example_operation():
    child_1_op = Operation(name="child_1")
    child_2_op = Operation(name="child_2")

    my_op = Operation(
        name="root",
        children={"child_1": child_1_op, "child_2": child_2_op},
    )

    return my_op


if __name__ == "__main__":
    op_1 = example_operation()
    op_2 = example_operation()
    op_1 == op_2
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.5.2
        pydantic-core version: 2.14.5
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
                 install path: /Users/mstechly/.pyenv/versions/3.10.10/envs/2023-11-30-serialization/lib/python3.10/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.10.10 (main, Oct  6 2023, 13:57:03) [Clang 14.0.3 (clang-1403.0.22.14.1)]
                     platform: macOS-13.3-arm64-arm-64bit
             related packages: typing_extensions-4.8.0 mypy-1.7.1
```
```

## Maintainer reply

Hi @mstechly,

I think you're correct, this is a rare enough use case that I don't think it makes sense to change the `__eq__` behavior.

content:
# Classes that are generic in stubs but not at runtime causes TypeError

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

The use of generic classes in stubs but not at runtime, as mentioned in the [mypy docs](https://mypy.readthedocs.io/en/stable/runtime_troubles.html#using-classes-that-are-generic-in-stubs-but-not-at-runtime), within Pydantic models can lead to a TypeError.

**Code Example:**
> Sorry, but I fall to provide some reproducible example without third-party dependencies
 
Consider the following code snippet:

```python
from __future__ import annotations
from asyncpg import Connection, Record

class Test(BaseModel):
    a: Connection[Record]
```

**Error Output:**
The resulting error output is as follows:

```
 File "/home/butvinm/Dev/app/api_v1/routes/tables.py", line 24, in <module>
    class Test(BaseModel):
  File "/home/butvinm/Dev/assignment/.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/pydantic/_internal/_model_construction.py", line 181, in __new__
    set_model_fields(cls, bases, config_wrapper, types_namespace)
  File "/home/butvinm/Dev/.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/pydantic/_internal/_model_construction.py", line 426, in set_model_fields
    fields, class_vars = collect_model_fields(cls, bases, config_wrapper, types_namespace, typevars_map=typevars_map)
                         ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/home/butvinm/Dev/.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/pydantic/_internal/_fields.py", line 120, in collect_model_fields
    type_hints = get_cls_type_hints_lenient(cls, types_namespace)
                 ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/home/butvinm/Dev/.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/pydantic/_internal/_typing_extra.py", line 212, in get_cls_type_hints_lenient
    hints[name] = eval_type_lenient(value, globalns, localns)
                  ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/home/butvinm/Dev/.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/pydantic/_internal/_typing_extra.py", line 224, in eval_type_lenient
    return typing._eval_type(value, globalns, localns)  # type: ignore
           ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/home/butvinm/.pyenv/versions/3.12.0/lib/python3.12/typing.py", line 393, in _eval_type
    return t._evaluate(globalns, localns, recursive_guard)
           ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/home/butvinm/.pyenv/versions/3.12.0/lib/python3.12/typing.py", line 900, in _evaluate
    eval(self.__forward_code__, globalns, localns),
    ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "<string>", line 1, in <module>
TypeError: type 'Connection' is not subscriptable
```

**Motivation:**
The ability to use such types is crucial in certain scenarios and cannot be circumvented. For example, when such a type is employed in a FastAPI path operation.

### Example Code

_No response_

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.5.2
        pydantic-core version: 2.14.5
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
                 install path: /home/butvinm/Dev/.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.12.0 (main, Nov 19 2023, 10:38:13) [GCC 13.2.1 20230801]
                     platform: Linux-6.6.2-1-MANJARO-x86_64-with-glibc2.38
             related packages: fastapi-0.104.1 mypy-1.7.1 typing_extensions-4.8.0
```

## Maintainer reply

Not much Pydantic can do here (stubs are purely decoupled from runtime). Anyway, you would have to define a custom type to perform validation as Pydantic does not know about the `Connection` class. So I think you could make it work by [creating a subclass](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/concepts/types/#customizing-validation-with-__get_pydantic_core_schema__) specific for Pydantic.

# Unable to create an instance for a model that uses alias in the field

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

Using alias in an Pydantic model is throwing error. The reason for including `alias` is to ensure it works for the usage in:

```
runtime_manifest = {
    "schema-version":"1.0",
    "name":"Random",
    "version":"1.0",
    "type":"synthetic-sensor",
    "asset":"lib.so"
}

temp = Manifest(**runtime_manifest)
print(temp)
```

### Example Code

```Python
import typing
import pydantic



class Manifest(pydantic.BaseModel):
    schema_version: typing.Optional[str] = pydantic.Field(alias="schema-version")
    name: str
    version: str
    type_: str = pydantic.Field(alias="type")
    asset: str


manifest = Manifest(
    schema_version="1.0",
    name="Random",
    version="1.0",
    type="synthetic-sensor",
    asset="lib.so"
)


print(manifest)
```

@alamathe1,

You can do one of the following:

class Manifest(pydantic.BaseModel):

# generic tagged union

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

Hi,

I'm trying to work with a generic tagged union. It seems to work well with `TypedDict`, for instance:

```python
from typing import TypedDict
from typing import Generic, Literal, TypeAlias, TypeVar

T = TypeVar("T")


class TriangleDict(TypedDict, Generic[T]):
    tag: Literal["triangle"]


class SquareDict(TypedDict, Generic[T]):
    tag: Literal["square"]


ShapeDict: TypeAlias = TriangleDict[T] | SquareDict[T]


def foo_dict(shape: ShapeDict[int]) -> None:
    if shape["tag"] == "triangle":
        reveal_type(shape)  # Revealed type is "TypedDict('shapes_dict.TriangleDict', {'tag': Literal['triangle']})"
```

But with the example code **bellow** using **BaseModel** instead, `mypy` is still ok, but Python fails with:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "(...)", line 18, in <module>
    def foo(shape: Shape[int]) -> None:
                   ~~~~~^^^^^
TypeError: __main__.Triangle | __main__.Square is not a generic class
```

Thanks!

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import Generic, Literal, TypeAlias, TypeVar

T = TypeVar("T")


class Triangle(BaseModel, Generic[T]):
    tag: Literal["triangle"]


class Square(BaseModel, Generic[T]):
    tag: Literal["square"]


Shape: TypeAlias = Triangle[T] | Square[T]


def foo(shape: Shape[int]) -> None:
    if shape.tag == "triangle":
        reveal_type(shape)  # mypy: Revealed type is "shapes.Triangle[builtins.int]"
```

# Understanding Changes in Pydantic Behavior: Why Optional Fields are Required in V2

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

Optional fields don't default to None and are required. 
I didn't have this issue in the past with Pydantic V1, is this a bug or a new feature?

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import Optional

class Student(BaseModel):
    name: str
    age: Optional[int]

# Creating an instance of Student with both name and age
# THIS WORKS
student_with_age = Student(name='Jane', age=25)
print(student_with_age)  # Output: name='Jane' age=25

# Creating an instance of Student without providing age
# It will default to None because it's declared as Optional
# THIS DOESN'T WORK ANYMORE, CAUSES ERROR
# Field required [type=missing, input_value={'name': 'John'}, input_type=dict]

student_without_age = Student(name='John')
```


### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.5.2
        pydantic-core version: 2.14.5
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true

@innicoder,

Thanks for your question. This is intentional. See [this section](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/migration/#required-optional-and-nullable-fields) of the migration guide for a detailed table and explanation of `Optional` logic.

# Overrided methods in nested models do not work

### Description

When overriding the `model_dump` method in a nested model it doesn't work. In the example, notice that the first model successfully ignores the `b` variable when it's None but the same strategy doesn't work in the nested `ExtraInfo`. 
When I run the example with a debugger I noticed that the model never passes through the second `model_dump`. Does the top model override bottom model methods?

### Example Code

```Python
class ExtraInfo(BaseModel):
    d: Optional[int] = None
    e: Optional[int] = None

    def model_dump(self, *args, **kwargs):
        if self.e is None:
            kwargs.setdefault('exclude', {}).update({'e': True})
        output = super().model_dump(*args, **kwargs)
        return output

class TinyModel(BaseModel):
    a: int
    b: Optional[int] = None
    c: Optional[str] = None
    d: ExtraInfo

    def model_dump(self, *args, **kwargs):
        if self.b is None:
            kwargs.setdefault('exclude', {}).update({'b': True})
        output = super().model_dump(*args, **kwargs)
        return output
    
payload = dict(a=1, c='a', d=dict())
model = TinyModel(**payload)

# Manually setting `exclude` doesn't work

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

Hey there 👋 
I want to exclude from serialization certain fields in specific cases but setting the `field.exclude` doesn't work

### Example Code

```Python
class TinyModel(BaseModel):
    a: int
    b: Optional[int] = None

    @model_validator(mode="after")
    def _unset_if_value_is_none(self):
        if self.b is None:
            self.model_fields['b'].exclude = True
        return self

payload = dict(a=1)
model = TinyModel(**payload)
print(model.model_dump())
# prints {'a': 1, 'b': None}
```

I know I can use `exclude_unset`, `exclude_none` and so on in the model_dump but for my needs I want to set specific fields to not be serialized


### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.5.1
        pydantic-core version: 2.14.3
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
                 install path: venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.11.1 (main, Jan 17 2023, 22:23:12) [Clang 13.0.0 (clang-1300.0.29.3)]
                     platform: macOS-13.2.1-arm64-arm-64bit
             related packages: typing_extensions-4.8.0 mypy-1.1.1
```

## Maintainer reply

`model_field` is a class variable, meaning modifying it from an instance will result in all other instances (and the class itself) to have `model_fields['b'].frozen` set to `True`.

Instead, I would suggest overriding `model_dump` on `TinyModel` and force the exclusion of `b` based on your condition. 

Note: If you use a type checker, you will get an error when trying to alter class variables from instance methods (such as your `_unset_if_value_is_none` method).

metadata.title: Manually setting `exclude` doesn't work

content:
# .

## Maintainer reply

Hi @danielniccoli,

I've made 2 small modifications to the example from your first comment:

```py
from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict
from pydantic.alias_generators import to_camel

config = ConfigDict(
    extra="forbid",
    frozen=True,
    populate_by_name=True, # <-- this is the important one
    hide_input_errors=True,
    validate_assignment=True,
    alias_generator=to_camel,
)


class User(BaseModel):
    model_config = config
    display_name: str


# Programmatically via parameters
user = User(display_name="Jon Doe")
user_dict = dict(user)
assert user_dict["display_name"] == "Jon Doe"

# Loading/Dumping JSON
user = User.model_validate_json('{"displayName": "Jon Doe"}')
assert user.model_dump_json(by_alias=True) == '{"displayName":"Jon Doe"}' # <-- using by_alias
```

The `populate_by_name` is key here, as it adds support for initializing a `User` instance with either `display_name` or `displayName`.

Regarding the discrepancy in behavior with validation / serialization and aliases, I understand the inconsistency you're pointing out, but at this point, changing this behavior would be a **significant** breaking change, so it's not on our roadmap.

This might not address all of your questions, but it does address the errors that you mentioned having in your first example.

# `.model_dump()` fails for frozen models nested in `set`

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

I have a pydantic model that contains a `set` of instances of another pydantic model. Because `set` requires hashable elements, I set the child model's config to `frozen=True`. This works well during instantiation, but fails during calls to `.model_dump()`:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/***/isolate2.py", line 15, in <module>
    Parent(children={Child(x=1)}).model_dump()  # Fails
    ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/home/***/.venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/pydantic/main.py", line 308, in model_dump
    return self.__pydantic_serializer__.to_python(
           ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
TypeError: unhashable type: 'dict'
```

I expected that `.model_dump()` would work as it does with other models and that a child (with all hashable properties) would properly behave as a hashable class even during `.model_dump()`.

I currently work around this more or less manually (sketched  in the Example Code as well).

### Example Code

```Python
from typing import Any
from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict


class Child(BaseModel):
    model_config = ConfigDict(frozen=True)

    x: int


class Parent(BaseModel):
    children: set[Child]


Parent(children=set()).model_dump()  # Ok
Parent(children={Child(x=1)}).model_dump()  # Fails


# Current workaround


def safe_dump(model: BaseModel) -> dict[str, Any]:
    dumped = dict(model)
    dumped['children'] = [child.model_dump() for child in dumped['children']]
    return dumped


safe_dump(Parent(children={Child(x=1)}))  # Ok, but not working in general case
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.5.1
        pydantic-core version: 2.14.3
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
                 install path: /home/***/.venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.11.4 (main, Jun  7 2023, 12:45:49) [GCC 9.4.0]
                     platform: Linux-5.15.0-10075-tuxedo-x86_64-with-glibc2.31
             related packages: typing_extensions-4.8.0
```

## Maintainer reply

@igordertigor,

I'd say so. We'll leave #8016 open as a feature request for a more specific error / suggestion.

As mentioned in that issue, you can also use `dict(model)` if you don't intend for the results to all be JSON serializable.

metadata.title: `.model_dump()` fails for frozen models nested in `set`

content: # Can't pass `tuple[T, ...]` into `Sequence[T]` parameter when using strict mode ### Initial Checks - [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2 ### Description Pydantic raises validation error when you're trying to pass tuple into a sequence field when using `strict=True`, even though tuple is a sequence: ```py Traceback (most recent call last): File "test.py", line 12, in Model(items=(1, 2, 3)) File ".venv\Lib\site-packages\pydantic\main.py", line 164, in __init__ __pydantic_self__.__pydantic_validator__.validate_python(data, self_instance=__pydantic_self__) pydantic_core._pydantic_core.ValidationError: 1 validation error for Model items Input should be a valid list [type=list_type, input_value=(1, 2, 3), input_type=tuple] For further information visit https://errors.pydantic.dev/2.5/v/list_type ``` This makes it pretty much impossible to use abstract types/collections with strict mode 🤔 ### Example Code ```Python from collections.abc import Sequence from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict class Model(BaseModel): model_config = ConfigDict(strict=True) items: Sequence[int] Model(items=(1, 2, 3)) ``` ### Python, Pydantic & OS Version ```Text pydantic version: 2.5.1 pydantic-core version: 2.14.3 pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true install path: .venv\Lib\site-packages\pydantic python version: 3.11.5 (tags/v3.11.5:cce6ba9, Aug 24 2023, 14:38:34) [MSC v.1936 64 bit (AMD64)] platform: Windows-10-10.0.19045-SP0 related packages: mypy-1.7.0 pydantic-settings-2.1.0 typing_extensions-4.8.0 ``` ## Maintainer reply @ThirVondukr, This is expected behavior according to our Conversion Table in the docs: https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/concepts/conversion_table/#__tabbed_1_1. content:
# Use of `pydantic.validate_call` change behaviour of functions that make in-place modifications

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

Consider the following code. Goal of `f` (and `g`) is to make some in-place changes in one of the arguments. These changes do take place inside both the functions, but these have no effect outside in case of `g`.

I am not sure if it's a bug or if it's intended by design, but this is definitely not an expected behaviour.

### Example Code

```Python
>>> 
>>> 
>>> import pydantic
>>> 
>>> def f(x: list[str], y: str) -> None:
...     print(f"{x=}")
...     x.append(y)
...     print(f"{x=}")
... 
>>> 
>>> g = pydantic.validate_call(f)
>>> 
>>> X1 = ["a", "b"]
>>> X2 = ["a", "b"]
>>> 
>>> f(X1, "c")
x=['a', 'b']
x=['a', 'b', 'c']
>>> 
>>> g(X2, "c")
x=['a', 'b']
x=['a', 'b', 'c']
>>> 
>>> print(X1)
['a', 'b', 'c']
>>> print(X2)
['a', 'b']
>>> 
>>>
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.5.1
pydantic-core version: 2.14.3
pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
install path: /home/anirban/conda-environments/genai/lib/python3.11/site-packages/pydantic
python version: 3.11.5 (main, Sep 11 2023, 13:54:46) [GCC 11.2.0]
platform: Linux-5.15.133.1-microsoft-standard-WSL2-x86_64-with-glibc2.35
related packages: typing_extensions-4.8.0 fastapi-0.104.1
```

## Maintainer reply

Of course, pydantic has to copy the list during validation.

Pr welcome to document this better, but it's the expected behaviour.

metadata.title: Use of `pydantic.validate_call` change behaviour of functions that make in-place modifications

# model_dump not properly serializing some children

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

`model_dump()` does not properly serialize params field

### Example Code

```Python
from typing import Any
from pydantic import BaseModel

class Person(BaseModel):
    name: str
    age: int

class TestModel(BaseModel):
    params: dict[str, Any] | BaseModel = {}

assert TestModel(params=Person(name="Scott", age=25)).model_dump(mode='json') != {'params': {}}

# >> assert TestModel(params=Person(name="Scott", age=25)).model_dump(mode='json') != {'params': {}}
# >>        ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
# >> AssertionError
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.5.1
        pydantic-core version: 2.14.3
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
                 install path: /home/scott/.cache/pypoetry/virtualenvs/ats-I0EmD-Zs-py3.11/lib/python3.11/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.11.6 (main, Oct  3 2023, 16:09:20) [GCC 13.2.1 20230918 (Red Hat 13.2.1-3)]
                     platform: Linux-6.5.11-300.fc39.x86_64-x86_64-with-glibc2.38
             related packages: typing_extensions-4.8.0 pydantic-extra-types-2.1.0 email-validator-2.1.0.post1 mypy-1.7.0 pydantic-settings-2.1.0
```

## Maintainer reply

Hi @skewty,

Thanks for reporting this. In this case, you'll have to use `SerializeAsAny` to get the behavior you're expecting:

```py
from typing import Any, Dict, Union
from pydantic import BaseModel, SerializeAsAny

class Person(BaseModel):
    name: str
    age: int

class TestModel(BaseModel):
    params: SerializeAsAny[Union[Dict[str, Any], BaseModel]] = {}

t = TestModel(params=Person(name="Scott", age=25))
print(t.model_dump())
#> {'params': {'name': 'Scott', 'age': 25}}

print(t.model_dump(mode='json'))
#> {'params': {'name': 'Scott', 'age': 25}}
```

You can read more about that [here](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/concepts/serialization/#serializing-with-duck-typing). If you use `Person` instead of `BaseModel`, you don't have to use this annotation.

content:
# Error generating JsonSchema for numpy.float64 field

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

Hi there!

I'm currently in the process of migrating a project to pydantic V2 and have come across an issue related to the JsonSchema generation for a field with type `numpy.float64`.

This is the V1 code: 
```
from pydantic import BaseModel
import numpy as np


class FooV1(BaseModel):
    name: str
    age: np.float64

    class Config:
        arbitrary_types_allowed = True

foo = FooV1(name="test", age=np.float64(14.64))
foo.schema()
```

which produces the following

```
{'title': 'Test',
 'type': 'object',
 'properties': {'name': {'title': 'Name', 'type': 'string'},
  'age': {'title': 'Age', 'type': 'number'}},
 'required': ['name', 'age']}
 ```
 
However, in the migrated V2 code, using the `model_json_schema()` method, I encounter a `PydanticInvalidForJsonSchema` error:
 
 ```
 from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict
import numpy as np


class FooV2(BaseModel):
    name: str
    age: np.float64

    model_config = ConfigDict(arbitrary_types_allowed=True)

foo = FooV2(name="test", age=np.float64(14.64))
foo.model_json_schema()
```

The error message is: `PydanticInvalidForJsonSchema: Cannot generate a JsonSchema for core_schema.IsInstanceSchema (<class 'numpy.float64'>)`.

I have attempted to address this by adding a json_encoder that converts `np.float64` to `float`. Unfortunately, this doesn't resolve the issue.

Could you kindly help me understand if there's something incorrect in my V2 migration code? Additionally, considering that we are dealing with the same `np.float64` type in both versions, is this behavior intended for the new version of pydantic? If so, is there a recommended way to generate a Json Schema for fields with `numpy.float64` type?

### Example Code

_No response_

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.5.1
        pydantic-core version: 2.14.3
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
                 install path: /Users/jeronimo/[redacted]/env/lib/python3.10/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.10.6 (v3.10.6:9c7b4bd164, Aug  1 2022, 17:13:48) [Clang 13.0.0 (clang-1300.0.29.30)]
                     platform: macOS-14.0-arm64-arm-64bit
             related packages: typing_extensions-4.8.0
```

## Maintainer reply

Hi @JeronimoMendes,

Great question. You can use `Annotated` along with `WithJsonSchema` to support the json schema generation for this arbitrary type. Consider the following:

```py
from typing_extensions import Annotated

from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict, WithJsonSchema
import numpy as np


np_float64 = Annotated[np.float64, WithJsonSchema({'type': 'float'})]

class FooV2(BaseModel):
   name: str
   age: np_float64

   model_config = ConfigDict(arbitrary_types_allowed=True)

foo = FooV2(name="test", age=np.float64(14.64))
print(repr(foo))
#> FooV2(name='test', age=14.64)
print(foo.model_json_schema())
#> {'properties': {'age': {'title': 'Age', 'type': 'float'}, 'name': {'title': 'Name', 'type': 'string'}}, 'required': ['name', 'age'], 'title': 'FooV2', 'type': 'object'}
```

Let me know if you have any follow up questions :)

metadata.title: Error generating JsonSchema for numpy.float64 field

# Parse default value of ImportString

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

There is a mention in documentation:

> A known limitation: setting a default value to a string won't result in validation (thus evaluation). This is actively being worked on.

As of pydantic 2.5.0 the issue is still present. And I don't see any open issue or pull request related to this. 

### Example Code

```Python
import math
from pydantic import BaseModel, ImportString

class MyModel(BaseModel):
  attr: ImportString = "math.cos"

assert MyModel().attr == "math.cos"  # not parsed
assert MyModel(attr="math.sin").attr == math.sin  # parsed
```

[Here](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/6456) I see an proposal like:
```python
from pydantic import BaseModel, ImportString

class MyModel(BaseModel):
  module = ImportString.import_string('os.path')  # equivalent to `module = os.path`
```

Which does not work:
```python
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "<stdin>", line 2, in ImportStringModel
AttributeError: type object 'ImportString' has no attribute 'import_string'
```

ImportString also does not support passing import string as constructor argument:
```python
>>> ImportString('os.path')
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
TypeError: ImportString() takes no arguments
```

The only working workaround I get is this:
```python
from pydantic import BaseModel, ImportString, validator

class MyModel(BaseModel):
    attr: ImportString = ""

    @validator("attr", always=True, pre=True)
    def _default_sttr_value(cls, value):
        if value:
            return value

        import math
        return math.cos

assert MyModel().attr == math.cos
assert MyModel(attr="math.sin").attr == math.sin

```


### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.5.0
        pydantic-core version: 2.14.1
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
                 install path: /home/maxim/Repo/horizon/.venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.11.5 (main, Sep  2 2023, 14:16:33) [GCC 13.2.1 20230801]
                     platform: Linux-6.6.0-1-MANJARO-x86_64-with-glibc2.38
             related packages: mypy-1.7.0 pydantic-settings-2.0.3 fastapi-0.103.2 typing_extensions-4.7.1
```

## Maintainer reply

Hi @dolfinus,

You can use the `validate_default` flag to resolve this issue.

```py
import math
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field, ImportString

class MyModel(BaseModel):
  attr: ImportString = Field(default="math.cos", validate_default=True)

assert MyModel().attr == math.cos  # parsed
assert MyModel(attr="math.sin").attr == math.sin  # parsed
```

# `core_schema.union_schema()` matters order in custom type after 2.5.0

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

I declared a new type using `__get_pydantic_core_schema__,` and it worked fine before updating 2.5.0.

I think `core_schema.union_schema()` is affected by its orders. An error also changes when changing `core_schema.no_info_plain_validator_function()` orders.

### Example Code

```Python
from collections.abc import Callable
from typing import Any

from pydantic import BaseModel
from pydantic_core import core_schema


class SomeModel(BaseModel):
    key: str


class T:
    @classmethod
    def __get_pydantic_core_schema__(
        cls,
        _source_type: Any,
        _handler: Callable[[Any], core_schema.CoreSchema],
    ) -> core_schema.CoreSchema:
        def validate_from_some_model(v: SomeModel) -> dict:
            assert v.key is not None
            return {"key": v.key}

        def validate_from_dict(v: dict) -> dict:
            assert v.get("key") is not None
            return v

        python_schema = core_schema.union_schema(
            [
                core_schema.no_info_plain_validator_function(validate_from_dict),
                core_schema.no_info_plain_validator_function(validate_from_some_model),
            ]
        )

        return core_schema.json_or_python_schema(
            json_schema=python_schema,
            python_schema=core_schema.union_schema(
                [
                    python_schema,
                    core_schema.is_instance_schema(dict),
                ]
            ),
        )


class Bar(BaseModel):
    bar: T


# python repl
>>> Bar(bar={"key": "key"})  # ok
Bar(bar={'key': 'key'})
>>> Bar(bar=SomeModel(key="key"))
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/Users/spike/Library/Caches/pypoetry/virtualenvs/pydantic-bug-ALq1cXaj-py3.12/lib/python3.12/site-packages/pydantic/main.py", line 164, in __init__
    __pydantic_self__.__pydantic_validator__.validate_python(data, self_instance=__pydantic_self__)
  File "/Users/spike/examples/pydantic-bug/bug.py", line 24, in validate_from_dict
    assert v.get("key") is not None
           ^^^^^
  File "/Users/spike/Library/Caches/pypoetry/virtualenvs/pydantic-bug-ALq1cXaj-py3.12/lib/python3.12/site-packages/pydantic/main.py", line 761, in __getattr__
    raise AttributeError(f'{type(self).__name__!r} object has no attribute {item!r}')
AttributeError: 'SomeModel' object has no attribute 'get'
```


### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.5.0
        pydantic-core version: 2.14.1
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
                 install path: /Users/spike/Library/Caches/pypoetry/virtualenvs/pydantic-bug-ALq1cXaj-py3.12/lib/python3.12/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.12.0 (main, Oct  2 2023, 12:03:24) [Clang 15.0.0 (clang-1500.0.40.1)]
                     platform: macOS-14.1-arm64-arm-64bit
             related packages: typing_extensions-4.8.0
```

## Maintainer reply

Hi @hard-coders,

Thanks for reporting this. This mirrors the issue reported here: https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/8111.

One workaround that you can use in the meantime is raising a `ValueError` instead of an `AssertionError` in your custom validators. That being said, our docs on validators do state:
> validators should either return the parsed value or raise a ValueError or AssertionError (assert statements may be used).

I'm not sure what the issue is with the `AssertionError` in this case, but we will look into this bug 🐛. Thanks again for the report!

metadata.title: `core_schema.union_schema()` matters order in custom type after 2.5.0

# Optional + default value not compatible with annotated fields

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

In following the [notes ](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/concepts/fields/#numeric-constraints)on how to best include compound types and constraints, I ran into an issue where pydantic kept insisting that a field was required when I thought I had properly allowed it to be `None`. In investigating the issue I think I have a good simply summary of it:

The ability to have an optional value with a default value is not compatible with defining the default value through an annotated field.

### Example Code

```Python
from typing import Optional
from typing_extensions import Annotated
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field

class OptionalFieldsTest(BaseModel):
    w1: Optional[str] = None
    w2: Optional[str] = Field(default=None)
    w3: Optional[Annotated[str, Field(description='this works!')]] = None
    w4: Annotated[str, Field(default=None)]
    e1: Optional[Annotated[str, Field(default=None)]]


# all the w fields are proper ways of defining defaults
OptionalFieldsTest.model_validate({'e1': 'required'})

# returns error that e1 is missing
OptionalFieldsTest.model_validate({})
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.5.0
        pydantic-core version: 2.14.1
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
                 install path: /usr/local/lib/python3.10/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.10.12 (main, Jun  7 2023, 19:32:10) [GCC 10.2.1 20210110]
                     platform: Linux-5.10.102.1-microsoft-standard-WSL2-x86_64-with-glibc2.31
             related packages: typing_extensions-4.8.0
```

## Maintainer reply

`e1` is equivalent to `Annotated[str, Field(default=None)] | None`, which feels a bit weird: the default value only applies to `str`, and not to the complete type, so Pydantic probably infers it as a required value in the end.

It doesn't really make sense to use `Annotated` this way though, `Annotated[str | None, Field(default=None)]` should be better (or even `str | None = None` if you don't need to specify anything else in `Field`).

One of `Annotated`'s goals is to be more intuitive about default values.
- Without `Annotated`, we would use `my_field: int = Field(description=...)`, which is counterintuitive as it makes people think `my_field` is not required because it has a _defaut value_ (in Python terms, not Pydantic!)
- With `Annotated`, we would use `my_field: Annotated[int, Field(description=...)]` (and specify a default with ` = ...`, not `Field(default=...)`).

---

The only case where we would apply `Optional[...]` or similar on top of `Annotated` is for constraints on compound types as mentioned in the linked docs, but `Field` arguments should only be related to the type it directy applies to:

`my_field: Annotated[Annotated[int, Field(ge=0)] | None, Field(description=...)]` (although a bit ugly)

metadata.title: Optional + default value not compatible with annotated fields

content:
# Inferred nested generic fails validation.

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

When nesting generic models, unless the inner model's type is also concretely typed the outer validation fails. 

It's trivial to fix for this simple example, but for more complex/dynamic cases I haven't found any way of getting round this without coming up with something outside of pydantic.

```
Runtime type is 'Inner'
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "repo/ptest.py", line 41, in <module>
    fails = Holder[int](inner=inferred)
            ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "repo/venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/pydantic/main.py", line 164, in __init__
    __pydantic_self__.__pydantic_validator__.validate_python(data, self_instance=__pydantic_self__)
pydantic_core._pydantic_core.ValidationError: 1 validation error for Holder[int]
inner
  Input should be a valid dictionary or instance of Inner[int] [type=model_type, input_value=Inner(inner=1), input_type=Inner]
    For further information visit https://errors.pydantic.dev/2.4/v/model_type
```

### Example Code

```Python
import typing as tp

from pydantic import BaseModel

T = tp.TypeVar("T")


class Inner(BaseModel, tp.Generic[T]):
    inner: T


class Holder(BaseModel, tp.Generic[T]):
    inner: Inner[T]


inferred = Inner(inner=1)
tp.reveal_type(inferred)  # Type of "inferred" is "Inner[int]" (Pylance)

works = Holder[int](inner=Inner[int](inner=1))
fails = Holder[int](inner=inferred)
```


### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.4.2
        pydantic-core version: 2.10.1
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=false
                 install path: repo/venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.11.6 (main, Oct  2 2023, 13:45:54) [Clang 15.0.0 (clang-1500.0.40.1)]
                     platform: macOS-14.1-arm64-arm-64bit
             related packages: mypy-1.6.1 pyright-1.1.335 typing_extensions-4.8.0 email-validator-2.1.0 fastapi-0.103.2
```

## Maintainer reply

Hi @zakstucke,

Thanks for reporting this. I'm inclined to think this is not a bug due to the fact that `typing` generics don't support `isinstance` or `issubclass` checks, so `pydantic` doesn't support these either.

From the `pydantic` docs on [generic models](https://docs.pydantic.dev/dev/concepts/models/#generic-models):
> While it may not raise an error, we strongly advise against using parametrized generics in isinstance checks.
For example, you should not do isinstance(my_model, MyGenericModel[int]). However, it is fine to do isinstance(my_model, MyGenericModel). (Note that, for standard generics, it would raise an error to do a subclass check with a parameterized generic.)
If you need to perform isinstance checks against parametrized generics, you can do this by subclassing the parametrized generic class. This looks like class MyIntModel(MyGenericModel[int]): ... and isinstance(my_model, MyIntModel).

For example, in your use case:
```py
print(isinstance(Inner(inner=1), Inner[int]))
#> False
```
helps to explain why you're getting the validation error that you are.

The initial (ish) decision to not support this kind of behavior is discussed here: https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/4970#issuecomment-1405342820.

Happy to discuss further, but for now, the behavior you're seeing can be explained by the fact that `pydantic` has mirrored the support / behavior of standard `typing` generics.

metadata.title: Inferred nested generic fails validation.

# TypeAdapter performs strict type checking by default and regardless of strict argument

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

We are in the process of updating the dependencies of our API (which uses fastapi) including migrating from pydantic v1 to v2. After making the necessary changes we noticed type coercion of our request bodies (which use pydantic models) appears to be strictly enforced. From what I have read of the pydantic documentation, it appears strict enforcement is not the intended default behaviour. I looked into [how fastapi is performing the validation](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/480620372a662aa9025c47410fbc90a255b2fc94/fastapi/_compat.py#L125) and it appears to be using a `TypeAdapter`.

The minimal example I have included results in the following error regardless of whether the `strict=False` argument is or is not included:
```
    output = type_adapter.validate_python(input, strict=False)
             ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/home/aidan/Development/experiment/pydantic-demo/venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/pydantic/type_adapter.py", line 283, in validate_python
    return self.validator.validate_python(__object, strict=strict, from_attributes=from_attributes, context=context)
           ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
pydantic_core._pydantic_core.ValidationError: 1 validation error for dict[str,str]
age
  Input should be a valid string [type=string_type, input_value=27, input_type=int]
    For further information visit https://errors.pydantic.dev/2.4/v/string_type
```

My expectation is that by default (and with the `strict=False` argument included) I would expect the `int` to be coerced to a `str` rather then resulting in a `ValidationError`. Am I misunderstanding the intended behaviour?

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import TypeAdapter

type_adapter = TypeAdapter(dict[str, str])

input = {"age": 27}

output = type_adapter.validate_python(input, strict=False)
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.4.2
        pydantic-core version: 2.10.1
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
                 install path: /home/aidan/Development/experiment/pydantic-demo/venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.11.2 (main, Jul 28 2023, 16:05:56) [GCC 11.3.0]
                     platform: Linux-6.2.0-36-generic-x86_64-with-glibc2.35
             related packages: typing_extensions-4.8.0
```

## Maintainer reply

You probably need to set `coerce_numbers_to_str`: https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/api/config/#pydantic.config.ConfigDict.coerce_numbers_to_str

content:
# Incorret generated JSON for a model containing a 'set' -> error on validation model

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

Hello, 

the JSON generated from a model containing a 'set' is not correct.  It is impossible to validate it.

It seems that the generated JSON is not correct:
```
{'my_set': ['bbb', 'aaa']}
```

instead of 
```
{'my_set': {'bbb', 'aaa'}}
```

When I validate the model (with strict mode), I have the following error

```
Input should be a valid set [type=set_type, input_value=['bbb', 'aaa'], input_type=list]
  For further information visit https://errors.pydantic.dev/2.4/v/set_type
```


### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel

class MyObject(BaseModel):
    my_set: set[str]

me = MyObject(my_set={"aaa", "bbb"})

model_as_json = me.model_dump(mode='json')

# validate the model, an error is thrown
model = MyObject.model_validate(model_as_json, strict=True)
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.4.2
        pydantic-core version: 2.10.1
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
                 install path: /home/yoann/.local/lib/python3.9/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.9.16 (main, Apr  3 2023, 12:39:37)  [GCC 8.5.0 20210514 (Red Hat 8.5.0-18)]
                     platform: Linux-4.18.0-477.10.1.el8_8.x86_64-x86_64-with-glibc2.28
             related packages: email-validator-2.0.0.post2 typing_extensions-4.7.1 fastapi-0.103.1
```

## Maintainer reply

`{'my_set': {'bbb', 'aaa'}}` isn't valid JSON (there's no such things as sets, so pydantic uses arrays instead).

So yes, trying to use `model_validate` with `strict=True` will raise a `ValidationError` because the set was "converted" to an array. Instead, you can do:

```python
model_as_json = me.model_dump_json()

model = MyObject.model_validate_json(model_as_json
```
Or avoid using `strict`

metadata.title: Incorret generated JSON for a model containing a 'set' -> error on validation model

# Validation fails with TypeError: datetime.date annotation with pandas.NaT input

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

Validation fails with a TypeError (instead of ValidationError) when a pandas.NaT value is passed as input to any field with datetime.date annotation

It seems under the hood pydantic interprets pandas.NaT as a float, tries to convert that to an int (presumably to treat it as a year or similar) and raises a TypeError during the process

Exception raised is:

```
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
TypeError                                 Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[33], line 7
      4 class TestSchema(BaseModel):
      5     date: datetime.date
----> 7 TestSchema(**{'date': pandas.NaT})

File ~/.../site-packages/pydantic/main.py:164, in BaseModel.__init__(__pydantic_self__, **data)
    162 # `__tracebackhide__` tells pytest and some other tools to omit this function from tracebacks
    163 __tracebackhide__ = True
--> 164 __pydantic_self__.__pydantic_validator__.validate_python(data, self_instance=__pydantic_self__)

TypeError: 'float' object cannot be interpreted as an integer
```

I noticed that the using `float('nan')` as input works as expected (i.e. produces ValidationError, not TypeError), so pandas.NaT must be a special type of float-like value that does not get caught by existing nan-detection logic.

Thanks :)

### Example Code

```Python
import datetime
import pandas
from pydantic import BaseModel
class TestSchema(BaseModel):
    date: datetime.date

TestSchema(**{'date': pandas.NaT})
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.2.0
        pydantic-core version: 2.6.0
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true mimalloc=true
               python version: 3.10.2 (main, Jan 28 2022, 16:19:08) [GCC 10.3.0]
                     platform: Linux-5.15.0-87-generic-x86_64-with-glibc2.31
     optional deps. installed: ['typing-extensions']
```

## Maintainer reply

Hi @hottwaj,

Thanks for reporting this. That behavior is a bit odd - given that we aren't going to build in logic to check against pandas types within `pydantic`, I think the best solution here is to add a field validator on your `date` field that checks for input of type `pandas.NaT` and converts that to `float('nan')` or something similar.

# Inconsistent handling of enum values with smart union

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

I've come across what I believe to be another inconsistency along the lines of #7097, involving enum values. Passing a string value for an enum seems to lead to the smart union preferring a raw dictionary over a compatible pydantic model.

Below is a stripped down version of something I ran into when migrating to v2 (with our workaround here: https://github.com/Materials-Consortia/optimade-python-tools/pull/1844)

### Example Code

```Python
from enum import Enum
from typing import Annotated, Any, Union

from pydantic import BaseModel, Field, __version__


class ResourceType(Enum):
    link = "link"
    image = "image"


class ResourceAttributes(BaseModel):
    name: Annotated[str, Field()]
    media_type: Annotated[ResourceType, Field()]


class Resource(BaseModel):
    attributes: Annotated[ResourceAttributes, Field()]


class Wrapper(BaseModel):
    data: Annotated[Union[Resource, dict[str, Any]], Field()]


class Wrapper2(BaseModel):
    data: Annotated[Union[Resource, dict[str, Any]], Field(union_mode="left_to_right")]

class Wrapper3(BaseModel):
    data: Resource


if __name__ == "__main__":
    print(f"{__version__=}")
    test_data = {"data": {"attributes": {"name": "123", "media_type": "link"}}}

    d = Wrapper(**test_data)
    print(d)

    d2 = Wrapper2(**test_data)
    print(d2)

    d3 = Wrapper3(**test_data)
    print(d3)

    test_data["data"]["attributes"]["media_type"] = ResourceType.link
    d = Wrapper(**test_data)
    print(d)

```

Running the above yields:

```
__version__='2.4.2'
data={'attributes': {'name': '123', 'media_type': 'link'}}
data=Resource(attributes=ResourceAttributes(name='123', media_type=<ResourceType.link: 'link'>))
data=Resource(attributes=ResourceAttributes(name='123', media_type=<ResourceType.link: 'link'>))
data=Resource(attributes=ResourceAttributes(name='123', media_type=<ResourceType.link: 'link'>))
```

i.e., in the second case, the test data is perfectly able to fit into the model, but unless `union_mode` is set to `left_to_right`, the smart mode (first case) prefers a dictionary. In the 3rd case, I have removed the union altogether, and the enum is correctly handled. Finally, if I explicitly set `media_type` using the enum (fourth case), then smart mode works fine.

Is this expected behaviour?


### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.4.2
        pydantic-core version: 2.10.1
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
                 install path: /home/mevans/.local/miniforge3/envs/pydantic_test/lib/python3.11/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.11.6 | packaged by conda-forge | (main, Oct  3 2023, 10:40:35) [GCC 12.3.0]
                     platform: Linux-5.15.90.1-microsoft-standard-WSL2-x86_64-with-glibc2.35
             related packages: typing_extensions-4.8.0
```

## Maintainer reply

Hi @ml-evs,

Thanks for reporting this. As of now, this is indeed how the smart union mode works. @adriangb mentioned in a different issue:
> v2 does multiple passes on unions. the first without coercion and if none of the options match it does another pass with coercion.

Your safest bet for now is to use one of the other three alternatives that you have listed. This PR in `pydantic-core` has the potential to change this behavior: https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic-core/pull/867.

I think it probably makes sense to close this issue for now, given that this is the expected behavior. That being said, we can certainly use this as a test case if / when we make changes to `pydantic-core` that support the behavior you're expecting.

metadata.title: Inconsistent handling of enum values with smart union

content:
# ValidationError on Optional Field

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

I am running into an error with `Optional` fields, where unless a default is set for the field, a `ValidationError` is thrown, declaring that the field is required.

### Example Code

```Python
from typing import Optional
from pydantic import BaseModel

class person(BaseModel):
    name: str
    title: Optional[str]

n = person(name="bob")
```

Result:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "test.py", line 8, in <module>
    n = person(name="bob")
        ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/home/user/.cache/pypoetry/virtualenvs/bbot-yxGMlPK5-py3.11/lib/python3.11/site-packages/pydantic/main.py", line 164, in __init__
    __pydantic_self__.__pydantic_validator__.validate_python(data, self_instance=__pydantic_self__)
pydantic_core._pydantic_core.ValidationError: 1 validation error for person
title
  Field required [type=missing, input_value={'name': 'bob'}, input_type=dict]
    For further information visit https://errors.pydantic.dev/2.4/v/missin
```

The error will not trigger if a default is set for the field, e.g.:

```python
title: Optional[str] = ""
```

... but this causes the default value to show up in `.dict()`:

```json
{"name": "bob", "title": ""}
```

The desired behavior is for the field to be omittable and not present in the serialized output.

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.4.2
        pydantic-core version: 2.10.1
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
                 install path: /home/user/.cache/pypoetry/virtualenvs/bbot-yxGMlPK5-py3.11/lib/python3.11/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.11.5 (main, Sep  2 2023, 14:16:33) [GCC 13.2.1 20230801]
                     platform: Linux-6.5.7-arch1-1-x86_64-with-glibc2.38
             related packages: typing_extensions-4.8.0
```

## Maintainer reply

Hi @TheTechromancer,

Check out our migration guide section on `Optional` types: https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/migration/#required-optional-and-nullable-fields

Also, check out our docs on excluding default values from serialized data: https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/concepts/serialization/#model-and-field-level-include-and-exclude

Let me know if you have any other questions!

class OpenAIChatFunction(BaseModel):

                                                ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^

  File "/p/i/promptchain/venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/pydantic/json_schema.py", line 2152, in model_json_schema

           ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
AssertionError: this is a bug! please report it

```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.4.2
        pydantic-core version: 2.10.1
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=true
                 install path: /p/i/promptchain/venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.11.5 (main, Aug 28 2023, 00:00:00) [GCC 12.3.1 20230508 (Red Hat 12.3.1-1)]

## Maintainer reply

Hi @ivanstepanovftw,

Thanks for reporting this. First off, you can only call `model_json_schema` on a subclass of `BaseModel`, it doesn't make sense to call it on `BaseModel` itself.

Secondly, you won't be able to assign it via default like you're attempting to do. That information isn't available at that point. You could define a `computed_field` to effectively act as an alias for the `model_json_schema` result, though maybe that doesn't fit all of your needs.

Happy to iterate on further solutions to this issue if the above doesn't work.

content:
# UserWarning about Self not being a type

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

I would like to build a generic "Tree" class which represents a directed acyclic graph. Core of this is a self-referencing node-like class, and a container class - all based on pydantic. See the code below. Running this in Python 3.10.12 generates a warning:
```
.../pydantic/_internal/_generate_schema.py:355: UserWarning: typing_extensions.Self is not a Python type (it may be an instance of an object), Pydantic will allow any object with no validation since we cannot even enforce that the input is an instance of the given type. To get rid of this error wrap the type with `pydantic.SkipValidation`.
```
I tried to apply `SkipValidation` on all levels, but that did not mitigate the warning. It seems like this is another special case that needs to be handled specifically in pydantic. If there is anything wrong with my code example, let me know. Thank you!

### Example Code

```Python
from typing import Generic, Sequence, TypeVar
from typing_extensions import Self
import pydantic
import weakref

T_co = TypeVar("B_co", bound=pydantic.BaseModel, covariant=True)


class Container(pydantic.RootModel[list[T_co]]):
    model_config = pydantic.ConfigDict(arbitrary_types_allowed=True)
    root: list[T_co]

    def __init__(self, *args: T_co) -> None:
        super().__init__(list(args))


class Tree(Generic[T_co], pydantic.BaseModel):

    """this class allows to store hierarchical trees of objects, with helper methods to
    traverse the tree"""

    model_config = pydantic.ConfigDict(arbitrary_types_allowed=True)

    _parent: weakref.ReferenceType[Self] | None = pydantic.PrivateAttr()  # to: Tree[T_co]
    item: T_co
    child_nodes: Container[Self]

    def __init__(
            self,
            item: T_co,
            child_nodes: Sequence[Self] | None = None,
            parent: Self | None = None,
    ) -> None:
        my_children = Container.model_construct(
            root=[] if child_nodes is None else list(child_nodes)
        )
        # we need to work around pydantic magic
        super().__init__(item=item, child_nodes=my_children)  # type: ignore[call-arg]
        for i in self.child_nodes.items():
            i._parent = weakref.ref(self)
        self._parent = None if parent is None else weakref.ref(parent)
        if parent is not None:
            parent._adopt_child(self)

    def _adopt_child(self, child: Self) -> None:
        """ensure we are part of the new parent's child_nodes"""
        if any(id(i) == id(child) for i in self.get_child_nodes()):
            # I am already in the (new) parent
            return
        self.child_nodes.append(child)
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.4.2
        pydantic-core version: 2.10.1
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=false
                 install path: .../.venv/lib/python3.10/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.10.12 (main, Jul  3 2023, 09:50:10) [Clang 14.0.0 (clang-1400.0.29.202)]
                     platform: macOS-13.5.2-x86_64-i386-64bit
             related packages: mypy-1.6.1 typing_extensions-4.8.0 pydantic-settings-2.0.3 typing_extensions-4.4.0
```

## Maintainer reply

Hi @marekro,

Thanks for your question. I think you might want to use postponed annotations here for your recursive (self referencing) model. Check out our docs on that [here](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/concepts/postponed_annotations/).

Basically, you can replace `Self` with `Tree` if you use `from __future__ import annotations` or `'Tree'` or a `ForwardRef`.

Also, I'd recommend that you inherit from `BaseModel` and `Generic[T_co]` in that order. Inheriting from `Generic` first can cause problems. We are currently working on a docs update + warning for the case where `Generic` is listed first.

metadata.title: UserWarning about Self not being a type

content:
# Optional[date] behaviour is weird

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

I'm reporting this issue on V2 but I see weird behavior also in v1
I'll make different examples to explain it clearly.
The problem is: How can I define a non-required optional date field in my model? (I made an equivalent example for int and it works)
In theory, this can be achieved like this:
```
class LegitimateDateModel(BaseModel):
    date: Optional[date] = None
```
But I get a Validation error when I pass a value to the model

### Example Code

```Python
from datetime import date
from typing import Optional

from pydantic import BaseModel

class LegitimateDateModel(BaseModel):
    date: Optional[date] = None

class LegitimateIntModel(BaseModel):
    x: Optional[int] = None

class LegitimateDateNotRequiredModel(BaseModel):
    x: Optional[int]

LegitimateDateModel(date=date(2020, 1, 1))  # ValidationError: Input should be None. I got same error in v1 as-well
LegitimateDateModel()  # date is None and everything is OK
LegitimateIntModel(x=1)  # x = 1. Everything is OK
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.4.2
        pydantic-core version: 2.10.1
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=false
                 install path: /Users/sabersolooki/.pyenv/versions/3.11.4/envs/pydanticplay-v2/lib/python3.11/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.11.4 (main, Jun 20 2023, 16:51:49) [Clang 14.0.0 (clang-1400.0.29.202)]
                     platform: macOS-12.4-x86_64-i386-64bit
             related packages: typing_extensions-4.8.0
```

## Maintainer reply

Hi @saber-solooki,

Thanks for reporting this. The problem here is that your field `date` is named the same as the type `date`. You can fix this with the following change, or some similar modification:

```py
from datetime import date
from typing import Optional

from pydantic import BaseModel

class LegitimateDateModel(BaseModel):
    date_: Optional[date] = None
```

or
```py
from datetime import date as date_
from typing import Optional

from pydantic import BaseModel

class LegitimateDateModel(BaseModel):
    date: Optional[date_] = None
```

metadata.title: Optional[date] behaviour is weird

content:
# running create_model on model_json_schema fails

### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

I have a fairly complex pydantic model that I want to convert the schema of to its own Pydantic BaseModel to return as a `response_model` in a FastAPI endpoint.  The reason is to allow users to recreate the original model from the schema without having the original files.  This used to work in v1 by using `create_model` on the schema dump of the model.   It no longer works in v2, due to the schema fields missing type annotations, see the error below.  

I recognize the reasons for the changes from v1 to v2, in adhering closer to the JSON/OpenAPI specification.  I've read through https://docs.pydantic.dev/2.4/concepts/json_schema/ but can't readily identify a solution.  How would I modify the output of `model_json_schema` to resolve the `model-field-missing-annotation` error?  Or is there another solution to get this working again.  

"""
PydanticUserError: A non-annotated attribute was detected: `$defs = {'A': {'properties': {'a': {'default': 1, 'title': 'A', 'type': 'integer'}, 'b': {'default': False, 'description': 'this is a boolean', 'title': 'B', 'type': 'boolean'}}, 'title': 'A', 'type': 'object'}}`. All model fields require a type annotation; if `$defs` is not meant to be a field, you may be able to resolve this error by annotating it as a `ClassVar` or updating `model_config['ignored_types']`.

For further information visit https://errors.pydantic.dev/2.4/u/model-field-missing-annotation
"""

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel, create_model, Field

class A(BaseModel):
    a: int = 1
    b: bool = Field(False, description='this is a boolean')

class B(BaseModel):
    abc: A = Field(..., description='A model')
    c: str = 'dfdfd'

schema = B.model_json_schema()

SchemaModel = create_model("SchemaModel", **schema())

#
# example FastAPI endpoint in other code
# @app.get('schema', response_model=SchemaModel)
# def blah():
#     return B.model_json_schema()
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.4.2
        pydantic-core version: 2.10.1
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=false
                 install path: /Users/Brian/anaconda3/envs/valis_test/lib/python3.10/site-packages/pydantic
               python version: 3.10.12 | packaged by conda-forge | (main, Jun 23 2023, 22:39:40) [Clang 15.0.7 ]
                     platform: macOS-10.14.6-x86_64-i386-64bit
             related packages: pydantic-extra-types-2.1.0 typing_extensions-4.8.0 fastapi-0.104.0 pydantic-settings-2.0.3 email-validator-2.1.0.post1
```

## Maintainer reply

Hi @havok2063,

Thanks for reporting this. So this isn't a bug - in general, we don't support creating models from json schema.

You could certainly write a tool to help parse the model json schema and pass those results into the `create_model` call.

I'm going to close this for now as it's not a bug, but feel free to post a discussion to get some help with designing the behavior you want!

metadata.title: running create_model on model_json_schema fails
